Year of Mysteries Coming Soon!.name: 谜团之年即将到来！ Year of Mysteries Coming Soon!

Unveil the Secrets of 2026!.name: 揭开2026年的秘密！ Unveil the Secrets of 2026!
Unveil the Secrets of 2026!.text:
与《谜团之年》！一起揭开2026年的秘密！

全年扮演传奇怪物与神话生物，让冒险永不落幕。

在2026年的每个月，你都会解锁一个全新的族裔——并随之在你的《谜团之年》 PDF中发现一个隐藏的谜题。解开谜题，在battlezoo.com输入答案，见证宏大谜团缓缓揭晓。

作为一名谜团探寻者，你的名字将出现在排行榜上，完成最终真相即可获得专属特别奖品，并揭示最后一个隐藏秘密带入你的游戏中。

踏入未知。解开谜题。夺取秘密。

立即报名参加《谜团之年》

Unveil the Secrets of 2026 with The Year of Mysteries!

Keep the adventure alive by playing as legendary monsters and mythical creatures all year long.

Each month in 2026, you’ll unlock a brand-new ancestry—and with it, a mystery puzzle hidden in your Year of Mysteries PDF. Solve the puzzle, enter the answer at battlezoo.com, and watch the grand mystery slowly unfold.

As a Seeker of Mysteries, you’ll see your name on the leaderboards, earn an exclusive special prize for completing the final truth, and uncover one last hidden secret to bring into your games.

Step into the unknown. Solve the puzzles. Claim the secrets.

Sign up Today for the Year of Mysteries

开放游戏许可协议

开放游戏许可协议 1.0a版

以下文本为威世智（Wizards of the Coast, Inc.）之财产，并受版权保护 © 2000 威世智（Wizards of the Coast, Inc.）（“Wizards”）。保留所有权利。

1. 定义：(a) “贡献者（Contributors）”指贡献开放游戏内容之版权和/或商标所有人；(b) “衍生材料（Derivative Material）”指受版权保护的材料，包括衍生作品与翻译（包括翻译为其他计算机语言）、转化、修改、校正、增补、扩展、升级、改进、汇编、删节或其他形式，使既有作品得以被重述、变形或改编；(c) “分发（Distribute）”指复制、授权、出租、租赁、销售、播送、公开展示、传输或以其他方式分发；(d) “开放游戏内容（Open Game Content）”指游戏机制，并包括方法、程序、流程与例行步骤，限于该等内容不体现产品标识，且相对于现有技术构成增强，以及贡献者明确标示为开放游戏内容的任何附加内容；并指任何受本许可协议涵盖的作品，包括依版权法之翻译与衍生作品，但明确排除产品标识。(e) “产品标识（Product Identity）”指产品与产品线名称、标志与识别性标记（包括商业外观）；器物、生物、角色、故事、故事线、情节、主题要素、对话、事件、语言、艺术作品、符号、设计、描绘、肖像、格式、姿势、概念、主题以及图形、摄影与其他视觉或音频呈现；角色、法术、附魔、人格、队伍、身份、肖像与特殊能力之名称与描述；地点、位置、环境、生物、装备、魔法或超自然能力或效果、标志、符号或图形设计；以及由产品标识所有人明确识别为产品标识的任何其他商标或注册商标；并明确排除开放游戏内容。(f) “商标（Trademark）”指贡献者用于识别其自身或其产品，或贡献者向开放游戏许可协议所贡献的相关产品之标志、名称、标记、符号、格言、设计。(g) “使用（Use）”、“已使用（Used）”或“正在使用（Using）”指使用、分发、复制、编辑、排版、修改、翻译，以及以其他方式基于开放游戏内容创作衍生材料。(h) “你（You）”或“你的（Your）”指就本协议而言之被许可方。

2. 许可协议：本许可协议适用于任何包含声明、指明该开放游戏内容仅可依据本许可协议及其条款予以使用的开放游戏内容。你必须将此类声明附于你所使用的任何开放游戏内容之上。除本许可协议自身所述外，不得向本许可协议增添或删减任何条款。对于使用本许可协议分发的任何开放游戏内容，不得适用任何其他条款或条件。

3. 要约与承诺：你对开放游戏内容的使用表示你接受本许可协议之条款。

4. 授权与对价：作为同意使用本许可协议之对价，贡献者授予你一项永久的、全球范围的、免版税的、非排他的许可，使你得以依本许可协议之确切条款使用该开放游戏内容。

5. 贡献权限之声明：若你以开放游戏内容的形式贡献原创材料，你声明你的贡献为你的原创创作，和/或你拥有足够权利以授予本许可协议所传达之权利。

6. 许可协议版权声明通知：你必须更新本许可协议中“版权声明（COPYRIGHT NOTICE）”部分，使其包含你正在复制、修改或分发之任何开放游戏内容的“版权声明（COPYRIGHT NOTICE）”的确切文本；并且你必须将你所分发的任何原创开放游戏内容的标题、版权日期与版权持有人姓名加入“版权声明（COPYRIGHT NOTICE）”。

7. 产品标识之使用：你同意不使用任何产品标识，包括作为兼容性之指示，除非你与各产品标识要素之所有人另行签订独立协议并获得明确授权。你同意不就包含开放游戏内容的作品，结合任何商标或注册商标来指示兼容性或共同适配性，除非你与该商标或注册商标之所有人另行签订独立协议并获得明确授权。在开放游戏内容中使用任何产品标识并不构成对该产品标识所有权的挑战。任何在开放游戏内容中被使用的产品标识之所有人应保留对该产品标识的全部权利、所有权与利益。

8. 标识：若你分发开放游戏内容，你必须清楚指明你所分发作品中的哪些部分属于开放游戏内容。

9. 许可协议更新：Wizards或其指定代理可以发布本许可协议的更新版本。你可以使用本许可协议的任何经授权版本，复制、修改并分发任何最初依据本许可协议任何版本所分发的开放游戏内容。

10. 本许可协议副本：你必须在你所分发的每一份开放游戏内容副本中包含本许可协议的一份副本。

11. 贡献者署名之使用：除非你获得贡献者的书面许可，否则你不得使用任何贡献者之姓名来营销或宣传开放游戏内容。

12. 无法遵守：若因法令、司法命令或政府规章之故，你无法就部分或全部开放游戏内容遵守本许可协议任何条款，则你不得使用任何受影响的开放游戏材料（Open Game Material）。

13. 终止：若你未遵守本许可协议所载全部条款，且在知悉违约后30日内未纠正该等违约，本许可协议将自动终止。所有分许可在本许可协议终止后仍然有效。

14. 修正：若本许可协议任何条款被认定为不可执行，该条款仅应在使其可执行所必需的范围内予以修正。

15. 版权声明

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx.name: 摘自艾瑟里娅·尼克斯的档案 From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx

To Rise and Fall.name: 兴衰浮沉 To Rise and Fall
To Rise and Fall.text:
摘自艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯的档案
兴起与陨落

社会结构被设计成教人服从、让人安分守己，使人沦为文明机器里不加思考的齿轮，于是多数文化中许多流行的措辞都在歌颂顺从、贬斥独立思考。既然如此，我们把那些能为自己思考的天使称作“堕落天使”，又有什么好奇怪的呢？

如今，事实是：你偶尔会见到一些天使猛然堕入真正的邪恶，像流星般直坠下方的地狱——他们被周遭的框架逼到极限，唯一的出路便是暴烈、突兀而彻底。这对我来说并不太令人意外，尽管大多数人会觉得震惊或自相矛盾：一位善的典范不经逐步滑落、而是一下子径直坠入堕落深渊。原因也相同：柔韧的柳树能在风的压力下弯曲并存活，而强壮的橡树在足够的压力面前却显得脆弱，始终不变，直到彻底倾覆。

但这种暴烈的堕落只是例外，并非常态。大多数堕落天使以更安静的方式、因更温和的理由做出决定。尽管如此，任何选择自己命运的天使，都被称为“堕落”。即便其中一些天使仍同样仁善、并为他人的缘故不知疲倦地战斗——事实上，我甚至会说，当他们选择如此行事时，他们更是善的典范，因为他们是在出于自身意志、并且拥有按自己所愿行事的选择权时选择了善，而不是被他者指派去扮演善的楷模。将自己的决定内化，会导向一种更真实、更深刻的信念表达……至少，我是这么想的。

并非所有“堕落者”都活在这两种极端之中：许多人只是想走自己的路，不论它会把他们带到哪里，因此大多数人既不是光辉耀眼的善之巅峰，也不是被巨大邪恶所驱使的反派。他们成为流浪者、农夫、诗人、公务员、隐士、冒险者等等。天界中的每一位天使都安居于其恰当的位置，因此彼此之间共享一些相似之处；但每一位堕落天使都鲜活、独特、与众不同。

也正因如此——再加上他们允许自己进化、前行、并为自己思考——我更愿意把这些所谓“堕落”的天使称作已然“升起”，而非“坠落”。我们又凭什么因他们做出那样的选择而评判他们？在我们能摆脱成见之前，也许是我们自己成了偏见的受害者。

——艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯，空间元素先驱，怪物倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
To Rise and Fall

The structure of society is designed to teach obedience and keep people in their place, unthinking cogs in the machine of civilization, leading to many of the terms popular in most cultures glorifying compliance and vilifying thinking for oneself. So is it any wonder that we call angels who can think for themself "fallen angels?"

Now, it's a fact that you'll occasionally see angels who flame out into true evil and fall like a meteor straight to the hells below—they're pushed so far by the framework surrounding them that the only escape is violent, sudden, and complete. It's not too surprising to me, even if most people would find it shocking or paradoxical, to see a paragon of good pass directly into the depths of depravity all at once without a gradual slide. That's for the same reason that a flexible willow can bend with stress from the wind and survive it, while the mighty oak proves brittle to sufficient stress, remaining unchanged until it topples entirely.

But these violent falls are the exceptions rather than the rule. Most fallen angels come to their decisions in quieter ways and for gentler reasons. Nonetheless, the term for any angel that chooses their destiny is known as "fallen." Even though some of these angels continue to be just as benevolent and fight tirelessly for the sake of others—in fact, I would even say they're more so paragons of good when they choose to do so, as they're choosing good of their volition and with the option to act however they choose, rather than simply being assigned the role of an exemplar of good from another. Internalizing one's decisions leads to a truer and deeper expression of belief… at least, that's what I think.

Not all fallen live at either of these extremes: many simply wish to follow their own path, wherever it might take them, leading most to be neither shining pinnacles of good nor villains possessed of great evil. They become wanderers, farmers, poets, civil servants, hermits, adventurers, and more. Every angel in the heavens fits in their proper place, and so they share some similarities with each other, but every fallen angel is vibrant, distinctive, and unique.

It's for that reason, along with the fact that they've allowed themselves to evolve, progress, and think for themselves, that I liked to refer to these so-called "fallen" angels as having risen, rather than fallen. Who are we to judge them for making that choice? Until we can break free from our preconceptions, it might be we who have fallen victim to our own prejudices.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Angel Ancestry.name: 天使族裔 Angel Ancestry
Angel Ancestry.text:
Not good enough for Heaven, not wicked enough for Hell, here you are instead.

Angels have free will. They more or less must have it in order to carry out their celestial duties effectively—if a being is to make choices, to act independently, to be a person and not merely a person-shaped automaton, then they must possess a measure of free will. Angelic free will isn’t quite as vibrant as that of mortals (or as violently perverse as that of fiends), but it does exist. It must exist.

Where free will exists, there is the opportunity for disagreement. Disobedience. Strife. Typically, when one is suffused with celestial virtue, these disagreements tend to be fairly benign. Most angels approach others with beneficent charity and sweet-natured compassion. And on the rare occasion that there are arguments between angels, they tend to be painfully polite. And yet, things do occasionally get out of hand. One hears stories, of course. Myths, more accurately, of archangels, consumed by their own pride, seeking to overturn the very order of things. Of angels succumbing to lust and temptation, to wrath and murder.

These angels are cast into the deepest depths, their halos confiscated, their wings burning away in the descent. It is said that the lords of Hell were virtuous once.

Then there are the less extreme cases. Not every angelic fall is such a grand affair. Sometimes, angels stray, have a difference of opinion or get distracted from their cause. In such cases, rather than a grand divorce in which they break the furniture and call the bailiffs, the higher-ups simply show the angel to one of Heaven’s side doors, give them a knapsack and a hearty handshake, and tell them they should make themselves scarce for a century or two until things blow over.

Some angels do not so much fall as they sidle in a vaguely earthward direction.

You Might...

  • Have complex and philosophical views on the nature of good and evil.
  • Occasionally refer to scripture or myth as if you were an eyewitness.
  • Avoid lying outright, preferring to sidestep things instead.

Others Probably...

  • Wonder where you got such a fine feathered cloak, and why you wear it everywhere.
  • React dramatically if they realize you’re actually a fallen angel (panic, endless curiosity, requests for atonement, etc).
  • Have a hard time figuring out just how old you are.

Physical Description

Earthbound fallen angels tend to look like the dominant humanoid inhabitants of wherever they first came to the mortal world. Most of the time, this means they look basically human, but angels take on the appearance of elves, dwarves, or even g’mayun, depending on where they land. They often manifest unusual coloration, such as a fallen planetar with greenish skin or a fallen balisse with orange-red eyes. These physical manifestations are more extreme than angelic planar scions, but fortunately for the fallen, they can usually manage to hide them and blend in, if not as an ordinary mortal, then at least as a planar scion.

The primary mark of a fallen angel is their cloak. In the process of departing the celestial realms, an angel’s wings are transformed into a feathered cloak, the feathers matching their now-lost wings. This cloak is not an entirely mundane object but rather is a manifestation of the angel’s soul. Angels always keep it close at hand if not wearing it, and if it is lost or destroyed, the angel feels

sickly and out-of-sorts until it re-manifests. Fallen angels are not strictly immortal, but they age so slowly that the span of the world is not enough time for them to grow old. A few angels who have been in the mortal world the longest (centuries if not millennia) do start to show very modest signs of aging—crow’s feet at the corners of their eyes or a few wisps of gray in their hair, but most retain the very same appearance they had when leaving Heaven.

Angelic Adventurers

There are very few fallen angels in the world, but among them, a surprising number may be counted as adventurers—after all, nothing else about a fallen is ordinary, so why should their lives be any different? Angelic adventurers usually have backgrounds that are analogous to acolyte, artist, emissary, guard, hermit, or warrior, although the life of an angelic acolyte is going to be rather different from that of a terrestrial one, for example. For classes, anything that deals with divine magic is an obvious choice, and so many angels become champions, clerics, or angelic sorcerers. Monks, with their practices of self-discipline, and druids, with their connection to remote and holy wilds, make up much of the remainder.

Society

Fallen angels are rare, though perhaps not quite as rare as people are inclined to think. On the one hand, the circumstances that combine to allow an angel to fall partway, as it were, simply don’t come up all that often. On the other hand, once in the mortal world, angels tend to live more or less indefinitely. They hardly age, and if careful, they rarely die of mischance or misadventure. On the rare occasion that an angel does “die,” the practical theology tends to become so complicated that not even the most fervent theologians can agree on precisely what happens next. Are they destroyed outright? Are they sent into the cycle of souls? Are they sent directly back to Heaven, or maybe somewhere else? There’s some evidence to support all these theories, and the truth might be a blend rather than a single answer. In total, most societies estimate that there are perhaps between three-score and a hundred fallen angels living in the world at any given time, but this number is undoubtedly an underestimate, as few mortals could tell how many fallen angels are living in hiding.

Most fallen angels prefer not to advertise their existence. As a purely practical matter, the fall tends to strip angels of much of their power—they can eventually get it back, but the difference between a mighty planetar leading a host of warrior angels and that same planetar fresh-fallen to the mortal realm is extreme. There are all manner of entities out in the world that would love to get their hands on an angel too weak to do anything about it, whether out of occult curiosity or bloody-minded malice. More mundanely, even if a fallen angel can avoid such murderous attention, publicly announcing oneself to be an angel fallen from Heaven tends to make things quite awkward when talking to mortals. Mortals often panic, start asking questions about the afterlife, make wild assumptions, and get churches involved. Confiding in mortals about their fallen angel status is often best avoided whenever possible.

At the same time, most angels prefer to live in mortal communities. There are the occasional hermit angels, occupying remote wilderness cottages or meditating atop mountains, but fallen angels like to be around people. Some of this is, again, practicality. Fallen angels have fallen flesh, and so they require all the necessities of life without which mortals tend to expire. They need food, shelter, and clothing, and these things tend to be easier to obtain in a community of some kind (especially for angels who are unused to considering such matters). The other part is that—well—angels get lonely. Heaven is a communal place, and most angels have spent their entire existence working as part of a grand host. While the fallen do tend to have their share of iconoclastic sorts, many angels seek mortal companionship to make up for what they have left behind.

There’s also another factor that even the fallen rarely like to dwell on. Even with the best of intentions, the fall is a hugely traumatic event. Far better to stay busy and involved with living people than to sit alone and brood over it. Nothing good can come from dwelling on the fall, and quite a few dangerous ideas can emerge after contemplating it for too long.

Still, mortals can help with angelic loneliness only to a certain extent. Even if the angel can find good and loyal mortals who don’t balk at the reality of a fallen’s existence, they still can’t understand the nature of Heaven. There are no words for it in mortal tongues nor the language to capture the ineffable beauty of the celestial planes. And so, the fallen seek each other out.

An angel’s fall is not an instantaneous process, and it’s usually preceded by signs and portents. After all, it’s not as if an angel falls out of Heaven every day or even every decade. Those wise to such things can see in the stars and the flight of birds, in the rattle of bones and the flicker of flames when and where the fall will happen, at least in a general sense. Angelic courtesy dictates that a fallen angel shouldn’t be left to their own devices if possible, so whenever a fallen notices these signs, they usually try to find the newly fallen angel. They don’t always succeed, especially in those cases where the fallen have lost their memory along with their wings, but more often than not, they arrive after some days or weeks (or months or years—immortals can have a strange idea of haste) and take the new angel under their (metaphorical) wing.

Usually, this mentorship is brief and mainly consists of helping the fallen get set up in the mortal realm. This might involve the mentor explaining local practices such as “commerce” or “farming,” getting the newly fallen set up with food, shelter, and the like, and connecting the neophyte to the rest of the mentor’s choir.

Fallen angels don’t congregate together in large numbers, but they do correspond. Across the world, there exists a sort of network of fallen angels, each exchanging an endless stream of letters and missives with one another. They use any number of means to ensure that no outsider reads their correspondence, from curses to messenger birds to writing their letters in celestial glyphs. The result is a kind of grand, meandering conversation stretching across the centuries, one part gossip, one part practical advice, two parts philosophizing on the nature of virtue and ethics.

Within that great conversation, the fallen divide themselves further into choirs, each choir being both a sub-dialogue within the general angelic discussion as well as a label for specific types of falls. They also serve as shorthand descriptions for some of the more common types of angels one encounters in terms of personality and interest—while not every one of the Wise is a self-righteous philosopher-saint, certain that they are right and the universe is wrong, they do tend to be rather strong-willed and stubborn souls. Not all angels belong to any of these choirs (or even the Choir of Mystery, which serves somewhat as a catchall for exceptions among the fallen).

The Choir of Duty

On the whole, most angels stay in the heavens—gods know there’s enough work to be found there, running their portion of the afterlife and all its attendant tasks. But angels do visit the mortal realm regularly as messengers, counselors, and protectors, and though many of their duties are quickly done, some find themselves staying in the mortal world for a very, very long time.

Over the centuries, such an angel might acclimatize to the mortal world. This happens most often with angels tasked to watch over or interact with a mortal community, although periodically, some angel set to guard some mountain fastness or remote shrine might start to view it as their home. Their fall tends to be gradual, with no great drama but simply a quiet forgetting, as orders come more rarely and their wings slowly transform into feathered cloaks.

Fallen of the Choir of Duty are the most apt to view themselves as angels in good standing and not as fallen, evidence of their wings notwithstanding. They tend to be a quiet, patient crew, well-suited to tasks spanning centuries or even millennia. Dutiful are also usually the angels most comfortable with the mortal world and most adept at passing for mortal. On rare occasions, such a fallen might even begin to forget their celestial origins, imagining themselves as merely an improbably immortal member of a mortal kin.

The Choir of Humility

Herein is the paradox of the universe. It’s about mortals. The gods, angels, and devils might wield great power and authority, but the very fabric of reality revolves around the cycle of mortal life and mortal death, their virtue, and their sin. Angels—indeed, everyone else—are essentially ancillary to the entire process. Some angels don’t deal with this well.

Pride is a common failing among angels, especially those (such as planetars, monadic devas, or chorals) who spend little time actually interacting with mortals. It’s surprisingly easy to fall into a mindset of, in essence, angelic supremacism. Not necessarily in a malicious fashion, although that happens as well, but to adopt a sort of benign, patronizing condescension. Let the wise and virtuous angels lead, and the mortals follow. In those circumstances, the heavens sometimes choose to nip the problem in the bud by directing an angel on the verge of falling to take an extended sojourn in the mortal realms. Close contact with mortals can cure the angel’s pride, so goes the idea. Usually, it works. Occasionally, it doesn’t.

Fallen of the Choir of Humility are known, ironically, for their pride first and foremost. An angel doesn’t enter the choir unless they were singularly proud beforehand, and while life in the mortal world usually sands away the sharp edges of their ego, the process can take place at a glacial pace. In truth, considering the size and durability of some angels’ egos, metaphors involving geological erosion are quite accurate. Still, given time enough, even the densest of the Humble can realize that mortals have some value to the multiverse.

The Fall

The process of the fall is complex, traumatic, and thorough. When an angel leaves the celestial halls, their body transforms into base matter, and what once was done by divine will now must be accomplished by all kinds of squishy anatomy. This tends to come as something of a shock to newly fallen angels, who have to learn about things like dehydration, food, and shelter very quickly. The fall also comes with limited (or not so limited) amnesia. While most fallen remember the heavens, the details are often fuzzy and lack the words to explain the afterlife in any great detail.

The Choir of Love

The classical virtues include charity, temperance, patience, and kindness. They rather pointedly don’t include love. It isn’t that the heavens object to love per se. Love for god, love for one’s fellows, all such things are good and just. But love itself is not always a moral trait. In the name of love, people have slit throats and toppled kingdoms. True love, unlimited love, is a dangerous mental state, and in its name one might leave Heaven or Hell.

The Choir of Love consists of those angels who fell due to love. The classic example is when an angel on a mission to the mortal realm falls in love with a mortal and chooses to remain rather than give up their beloved. Depending on the circumstances, the angel and Heaven might negotiate such extended stays, or they could be a thing of harsh words and broken vows. Other sorts of love can also persuade angels to stay, if more rarely. The love of an angel for a family or bloodline might persuade them to remain as a sort of guardian (sometimes, these are their own descendants). Other angels fall in love with particular places, whether untouched wilderness or fascinating, bustling cities.

Fallen of the Choir of Love are creatures of passion. They tend to wear their hearts on their sleeve and are prone to obsessive whims and unrestrained enthusiasm. If a Lover is your friend, there is no greater ally in the world. But love can turn to hatred quite easily, and there are few more vengeful foes than a Lover spurned.

The Choir of Penitence

Sometimes angels succumb to temptation. The famous cases are the ones where they then proceed to hurtle, screaming in defiance, straight into Hell, but just as there are mortal sins, so too there are venial ones. Sometimes, an angel commits a crime, one which makes their judgment and ethics suspect, but the angel in question is sufficiently penitent that a few centuries of probation is a better outcome.

Angelic crimes vary. Some are crimes of passion or imprudence—an angel might be too quick to anger and judge and, in so doing, cause havoc that has no part in the divine plan. Others are crimes of disobedience or hubris—the angel thinks they know better than everyone else and so chooses to go their own way rather than align with the rest of the heavens (some overlap with the Choirs of Wisdom and Humility occurs here). Other crimes are incomprehensible to mere mortals, breaches of celestial etiquette that cannot be overlooked, issues of corruption, or infection.

Fallen of the Choir of Penitence are the most varied of the fallen, as befits the varied crimes that bring them to the mortal realm. An angel banished for succumbing to wrath might be a furious, forceful being, or they might be prudent and cautious, trying to correct for the tendency that led to their penance in the first place. Some Penitents feel guilt for their sin and seek to atone for it, while others are singularly cynical about the whole matter and are destined for a long stay on earth indeed (the world has no cynic like a fallen angel).

The Choir of Wisdom

What is good? As with so many things, the answer is “it’s complicated.” Is it a principle of the greatest good for the greatest multitude? Or about acting with personal virtue at all times? Is it a matter of consequences or of duty? The truth is that morality is a complicated business and a high-stakes one when souls are involved, and so disagreements arise even in the heavens about the thorniest cases.

Usually, angels are able to resolve such things internally, with appeals to higher powers and celestial gods. But on rare occasions, an angel will dig in their metaphorical heels on a point and refuse to budge, even when all the heavens stand against them. In such cases, an extended leave of absence is sometimes a good idea to take a few centuries of sojourn to the mortal realm to allow tempers to cool and memories to fade.

Fallen of the Choir of Wisdom are deeply and profoundly concerned with the nature of morality. They also tend to be extremely proud and courageous at once because they were willing to leave Heaven rather than bend on their idea of what is right. Occasionally, this pride leads them to a further fall, but just as often, the Wise are some of the greatest champions of virtue in the mortal realm.

Wisdom and Arguments

An angel of the Choir of Wisdom chose to fall rather than give up on their favorite argument, and so it’s safe to say they’re stubborn enough to butt heads with others in the mortal realm who oppose their moral viewpoints. This could be a great roleplaying opportunity if another fellow adventurer presents a perfect target for arguments, but only if the other player and the whole group are interested in playing that out. Talk to your group before deciding to create a Choir of Wisdom angel primed to argue with another character. And don’t let the fact that your character was so stubborn that you fell from grace over this issue block you from having your character slowly change your mind. Character arcs are much more enjoyable when characters aren’t static, so consider the possibility that the new perspective from exploring the mortal world has allowed you to broaden or refine your viewpoint and adopt a different point of view.

By far the smallest of the fallen choirs, the Choir of Mystery consists of those angels who haven’t the faintest idea why they fell. All they know is that out of seemingly nowhere, they were told to vacate Heaven, with no explanation or chance of appeal. Sometimes, whoever delivered the message gave them an injunction to stay in a particular section of the mortal realm, but just as often, they were simply informed that their services were no longer required and, thus, that they should make themselves scarce.

Why the heavens would act so seemingly unjustly is the cause of some debate and concern among the fallen. One theory is that the Mysteries must have committed some unknown unforgivable crime or violation, but this holds little water given that it’s rather hard to do something unforgivable without realizing it. Another possibility is that while the angel hasn’t done something, but that they would have done something—prophecy is hardly an unimagined gift among the heavenly authorities. A third possibility, and popular theory among many Mystery, is that they are, in essence, sleeper agents, their superiors guided by prophecy to ensure that some time hence (days, months, decades, eons), an angel will be at the right place at the right time. This view gives great hope to the fallen of the Choir of Mysteries, for it promises that they might return to Heaven as heroes and not as exiles.

Fallen of the Choir of Mysteries are nearly as varied as the Penitents since their lot may fall on any angel for no visible reason. Relative to most fallen, they tend to be quite active, trying to uncover the nature of their unknowing violation or unspoken mission and hoping against hope for a return to the celestial realms. Curiosity is a common quirk, along with a certain reticence that goes above and beyond even the customary discretion of the fallen—it’s one thing to fall for something you did, but falling for no reason at all seems downright embarrassing.

Beliefs

You can use the following tendencies to guide you in choosing an alignment if you’re playing with alignment in your game or to assist with characterization for fallen angels if not: In matters of morality, most fallen angels found in the mortal realm are some flavor of neutral, with shallow forays into good and evil. Truly vile fallen tend to keep falling and cease being an angel, accepting the patronage of Hell or another fiendish realm and becoming a fiend instead. Meanwhile, most angels of more idealistic tendency tend not to fall in the first place. Fallen angels do have a slight lean towards chaos simply because they tend to be the more iconoclastic angels that part ways from their brethren.

With regards to faith, some angels continue to follow the deities they served in the heavens, though typically, what an angel does can’t strictly be called “worship” so much as maintaining an old collegial relationship. Some angels shift to a new master instead but maintain the same sort of comradely air. Many angels instead swear off religion altogether, in something the manner of a recovering alcoholic, and a few become outright atheists, arguing that the gods are merely great and powerful beings but not something sacred enough to be inherently worthy of worship.

Popular Edicts actualize the potential of your choir, mentor fellow fallen when you find one, seek companionship to ease the loneliness and trauma

Popular Anathemas advertise your existence, betray your mentor or network of fallen angel contacts, make pacts with fiends

Afterlife?

Fallen angels are immortal, or if one wants to be technical about it, functionally ageless (a term that some call "amortal" instead). Nevertheless, through violence or misfortune, a fallen can perish, in which case all kinds of complications arise over what to do with their soul. Sometimes, the fallen angel’s soul is processed much as a mortal’s, with all the attendant judgment of their mortal lives. Other times, especially if the fallen dies in an act of great virtue or sacrifice, death is merely their welcome back to the heavens. And sometimes, if a fallen angel’s death comes at an inopportune time, the powers that be scoop up the wayward celestial and stick them right back into the mortal realm.

Names

All angels possess a sacred, holy name—though their name might have acquired some slight tarnish in the case of the fallen. Angels don’t use their holy name in daily life, however. It is a sacred thing, reserved for divine ceremonies or moments of great importance (also, most mortals can’t pronounce Celestial to save their lives, resulting in some absolutely painful manglings of divine names).

Instead, angels have a common name or alias. These often sound a bit old-fashioned or antiquated (angels are ancient and rarely bother to update their names) and occasionally derive from languages long dead.

Sample Names

Elmariel, Onirifan, Natab, Azra, Ibaranel, Chelsirian, Menkhanil.

Resolving the Mystery

Angels of the Choir of Mystery have a secret central element to their story: why and how did they fall? Even the angel themselves doesn't know, and they might not even have so much as a clue to the mystery at first. But that doesn't mean you the player don't have any ideas. When playing an angel of the Choir of Mystery, you'll want to have a discussion with the GM to decide on the way the mystery might unfold. If you have a specific answer you want to use and let your angel discover, let your GM know, and similarly if you want your GM to come up with something from scratch, let them know that too. Sometimes a collaboration leads to the best results.

Angelic Heritages

In the heavens, angels have their own hierarchy and their own classifications, and almost any of them might fall in the proper circumstances, whether a swift-winged astral deva or a mighty planetar. Choose one of the following angelic heritages at 1st level.

Evil Is In the Eye of the Beholder.name: 邪恶在旁观者眼中 Evil Is In the Eye of the Beholder
Evil Is In the Eye of the Beholder.text:
摘自艾瑟瑞亚·尼克斯的档案
邪恶就在旁观者的邪眼之中

有人说，美在旁观者的眼中；但当谈到我们对道德的理解时，我觉得这句格言在善与恶的概念上适用得更为强烈。每个社会对它们的定义都不相同，而且往往即便在同一个社会之内，道德哲学家与普通民众也无法形成共识。它们也许是宇宙层面的概念，但即使如此，我们自身有缺陷的凡人理解仍会将不准确与偏差投射到我们的模型与近似之中，而纯粹的宇宙力量根本不具备这些。

那么，为什么“邪眼”会被称作“邪眼”？该族裔的俗称源自一个词汇，用来指代一种带有诅咒性质或能反弹恶意凝视的神秘怒视或注视。这一现象又与一种基于相同魔法性质的女巫妖术共享其名。邪眼自身对其族裔有数百种不同称呼，并不存在单一的共识名称。足够多的邪眼觉得这个俗称迷人或有趣，于是它也成为该族裔内部最常见的名称——也许这正是邪眼普遍拥有幽默感的一个迹象。

但它们并不特别邪恶。既不是指神圣与亵渎力量之间的宇宙阵营意义上的邪恶，也不是指出于享乐或为自身获利而恶意滥用他人的那种邪恶。当然，确实有些邪眼是那样，但并不一定比其他族裔更多。

鉴于邪眼能够施加诅咒与负面状态，以这种神秘现象为族裔命名当然并非不合理。但“邪眼”这一魔法现象最初又为何得名？使用该妖术的女巫并不一定必须是邪恶的。这个术语已在世界各地、众多族裔之中被发现，因此我们也不能仅将其当作某种词源学上的怪异而一笔带过。我相信这与我们对他人目光的本能恐惧有关——以及那目光会把我们变成什么。我们害怕在他人的凝视中被评判、被嫉妒；害怕从完整的自我被削减成对方眼球表面上的一幅影像，失去深度与细腻。

从这个意义上说，我们也有罪：我们把同样的命运延续到许多其他族裔身上，其中也包括邪眼自身。我们应当努力改而像那些会反弹恶意凝视的邪眼护符那样行事，把每个人都当作独立个体来接近，并试图理解他们的文化。如此一来，我们甚至能够理解那些与我们迥然不同的存在——例如邪眼；而在此过程中，我们也同样能对自己学到许多。

— 艾瑟瑞亚·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
Evil Is In the Eye of the Beholder

Some say that beauty is in the eye of the beholder, but when it comes to our understanding of morality, I feel that the saying applies even more powerfully when it comes to the concepts of good and evil. Every society defines them differently, and often, even within a single society, there can be no consensus among moral philosophers and common folk alike. They might be cosmic concepts, but even then, our own flawed mortal understanding projects inaccuracies and deviations into our models and approximations that the pure cosmic forces simply lack.

So why is an evil eye called an evil eye? The ancestry's colloquial name comes from the term for a type of mystical glare or stare that possesses the properties of a curse or turns back malevolent gazes. This phenomenon in turn shares its name with a witch hex based on the same magical properties. Evil eyes themselves have hundreds of different names for their ancestry with no single consensus name. Enough of them find the colloquial name charming or amusing that it's come to be the most common name within the ancestry as well, perhaps a sign of the sense of humor common among evil eyes.

But they aren't especially evil. Not in the sense of cosmic alignments between holy and unholy forces or in the sense of malevolently abusing others for enjoyment or to take advantage for their own gain. Well, of course, some evil eyes are like that, but not necessarily more so than other ancestries.

It's certainly not unreasonable to name the ancestry after the mystical phenomenon, given that evil eyes are capable of applying curses and negative conditions. But how did the magic evil eye get that name in the first place? It's not like witches who use that hex must be evil. The term has been found across the world and among myriad ancestries, so we can't just dismiss it as some sort of etymological oddity either. I believe it ties into our inherent fear of the gaze of others and what that gaze can make of us. We fear being judged and envied in another's gaze, of being reduced from our full selves into a mere image on the surface of their eye, lacking depth and nuance.

In that sense, we are guilty of perpetuating that same fate on many other ancestries, including evil eyes themselves. We should strive to instead act like the evil eye talismans that turn back malicious gazes, approaching each person as an individual and seeking to understand their culture. In so doing, we can understand even beings as different from us as evil eyes, and in so doing, we can learn much about ourselves as well.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Evil Eye Ancestry.name: 邪眼 族裔 Evil Eye Ancestry
Evil Eye Ancestry.text:
Mysterious and individualistic creatures, evil eyes are known for their unusual appearance, their huge eye, strange curses, eye beams, and other powers. Many evil eyes seek their own personal path to understand the world around them, with little regard to the opinions and pressures of their forebears.

Evil eyes, as a collective ancestry, can’t come to any consensus on a single name or origin story for themselves. If you ask any given evil eye their opinion, they’ll be all too happy to explain their own favorite ancestry name and mythological creation story that differs from those you’d hear from every other evil eye in existence. These ideas are wholly their own and usually bear little resemblance to the beliefs of the evil eye parents who birthed them—a process involving two or more evil eyes entwining their stalks in a specific way and then leaving behind a spore sac to grow into the new evil eye.

Even the name “evil eye” itself is simply a moniker that more members of the ancestry are willing to accept as the least disliked compromise to their own “perfect” name. It managed to achieve this lukewarm consensus thanks to the fact that it connects the ancestry to the ancient magical concept of the evil eye, a baleful and intentional gaze that can curse others or reflect a curse back to the one who creates it, and thus it lends evil eyes a significant amount of gravitas. Some evil eyes even flat out prefer the name or something similar, and these tend to be those evil eyes who believe that their ancestry owes its origin to the mystical evil eye of legend incarnating itself into a living creature.

Some other evil eyes believe that the ancestry owes its origin to an ancient deity of wisdom ripping out their own eye and tossing it into the well of memory in exchange for knowledge of all the world’s secrets of magic. In this retelling, the disembodied eye became soaked in all the memories and knowledge of the world and became the first member of the evil eye ancestry; those evil eyes who believe in this tale often instead refer to their ancestry by names that allude to this story in some way or another, such as “Wise Ones” or “Mementos.”

Another popular origin story suggests that a scrying sensor from a truly powerful archmage became an unusual sort of living spell imbued with life, spirit, and a mind and body of its own. Then, after this living spell found a way to grow and reproduce, a new ancestry was born, entirely separate from the ancestry known as living spells. Evil eyes who prefer a similar tale often call their ancestry by names that relate to scrying magic in some way, like “Scrylings” or “Watchers.”

While variations on these three themes are the most popular among evil eyes living today, many other theories exist, with origins potentially linking evil eyes to as wild a set of different creatures and items as angels, gibbering mouthers, and even the powerful magic item known as the robe of eyes. The one thing that’s certain is that evil eyes won’t be agreeing on an origin for their ancestry any time soon.

Of course, this diversity of thought and belief doesn’t make things easy for other ancestries to communicate with evil eyes. In fact, many evil eyes find differing opinions about their ancestry’s origin and nature to be especially offensive, and they speak of their own ideas as if they were established fact. This can lead someone to repeat them later without knowing. Still, almost all evil eyes understand that other ancestries needed a name to use, and they grudgingly accept “evil eyes” while usually grumbling about how their chosen ancestry name better reflects the resplendent inner truth of the ancestry.

If you want to play a giant eyeball with mystic powers and a unique perspective on the world, you should play an evil eye.

You Might...

  • Have your own take on every significant topic, mystery, or open question of the day.
  • Remain observant at all times, even when you’re at ease, at rest, or celebrating your victory.
  • Combat your foes with unusual means, including mystical powers, curses, and eye beams.

Others Probably...

  • Expect you to have phenomenal magical abilities and inexplicable curses, even if you don’t.
  • Assume you’re dangerous or bring bad luck.
  • Walk on eggshells around details of your ancestry if they don’t know your personal beliefs yet.

Physical Description

Evil eyes have bodies consisting of a large head with a single massive eye and a maw of needle-sharp teeth, with the ability to extend arm and leg stalks and occasionally wings and fins from that central body in order to move about and carry or wear objects. Even without their stalks, wings, or fins, the sphere is usually around four feet in diameter, and with their leg stalks, an evil eye can easily stand over six feet tall.

An evil eye’s appearance is also somewhat influenced by their heritage. While it’s not as extreme as some other ancestries like devils or psychopomps, whose heritages might as well be different species, many evil eye heritages are easy to pick out at a single glance. Aquatic eyes have fins and other features that make them more aquadynamic, more easily able to operate underwater. Balloon eyes often appear visibly saggy or hollow in spots due to the fact that most of their body is filled with air. Burning eyes tend to be covered in literal flames. Mini eyes are perhaps the easiest to tell apart because they are much smaller than other evil eyes, making it obvious that they’re either members of that heritage or another type of evil eye that isn’t yet full grown.

Elder eyes, midnight eyes, and stalking eyes are the three heritages that are the hardest to tell apart from each other. As a general rule, you can tell a stalking eye by looking at its stalks and how they move (if the evil eye is retracing all their stalks, this might be difficult, so you can look for signs of an elder or midnight eye and perhaps conclude the evil eye is a stalking eye by default if you don’t find them). Elder eyes often look slightly bigger and a bit more wizened than other evil eyes, though this isn’t nearly as obvious as the tiny size of a mini eye or the bloated, airy appearance of a balloon eye. Midnight eyes tend to have darker coloration to help them blend into the night, and when they don’t, it’s possible to notice a difference in their pupils that grants them their exceptional vision even in magical darkness. In general, it takes some amount of knowledge and skill to distinguish these three heritages from each other.

Despite their unusual anatomy making it seem difficult or impossible, an evil eye is able to wear just about any sort of item a humanoid can wear. They do so through creative reshaping of their stalks, allowing them to approximate a portion of a humanoid’s body that can hold or wear the item in question. Of course, unless the evil eye is especially interested in humanoid fashion, there’s not much point to going to such an effort in order to don a mundane piece of decorative clothing. Most evil eyes put in the effort only when the apparel serves some clear functional purpose to them, especially if it happens to be a magic item with a benefit the evil eye would appreciate.

Evil Eye Stalks

Evil eyes don’t have arms or legs like a humanoid, but they can grow and extend stalks that function as up to two arms and two legs as they desire, and some evil eyes can even grow fins, wings, and other features. These function the same as arms, legs, hands, and feet, albeit a bit slower at walking than most humanoids of similar stature, and evil eyes can wear any equipment meant for humanoids, despite their unusual body shape.

Society

Most evil eyes either never know their parents at all or else meet them for only a few days at most, just after being born. Rather than form familial bonds, young evil eyes learn to hunt or gather food on their own, relying on some inborn instinctual knowledge passed down from their ancestors, which also assists them in learning to speak, read, and write. After becoming fully self-sufficient, evil eyes then often tend to keep to themselves except when pursuing their goals. Even when they deign to associate with other people in order to help them achieve what they want, most evil eyes (with the exception of emissaries) are much more likely to team up with members of various other ancestries rather than other evil eyes.

Oculi

Despite their individualistic nature, evil eyes possess certain tendencies that members of other ancestries tend to classify into six broad groups that sociologists call “oculi.” Sometimes especially clear-eyed evil eyes who fall somewhere into the historiarchs’s oculus even agree with these classifications or make their own adjustments to them. Of course, even the scholars who proposed organizing evil eye philosophies and sociology into oculi agree that many evil eyes blend the line between several of these oculi or have their own tendencies removed from all six; these are just a simplification meant to make it easier to understand the overall behavior of a highly individualistic ancestry. For their part, most evil eyes would flat-out deny being members of any such classification if asked, claiming that any similarities they have in their beliefs and actions to other evil eyes were purely coincidental. A few evil eyes are proud enough of their beliefs that they accept the label of one of the six oculi, though in that case they might then claim that they invented the oculus’s philosophy first or that they truly embody it, while others are merely hanging on their periphery.

Bodyshapers

Bodyshapers delight in the way they can shape, transmute, and grow their body, creating stalks and other appendages whenever they need them over time. While all evil eyes engage in this form of growth and change to some extent, to a bodyshaper, this sort of growth is more than just a biological process—it’s a unique art form all its own and the ultimate form of self-expression. Bodyshapers study their own bodies more deeply than evil eyes of other oculi ever do, and they use what they learn from their studies to affect extremely precise and specific cosmetic adjustments to coloration, patterning, texture, and other physical features across their entire body. In this way, a bodyshaper’s body is both their temple and their canvas.

Bodyshapers are less concerned with their origins than members of most of the other oculi: to a bodyshaper, it’s not what they were or where they came from that matters so much as where they’re going and what they might become someday. This gaze toward the future and penchant toward evolving themselves has led to many bodyshapers promulgating their own new philosophies or doctrines of enhancement and modification with fanciful names like “transoculism” or “eyevolution.” While there are individual differences, most of these philosophies essentially boil down to the belief that one day evil eyes will use magic to enhance their bodies and minds to function better, stronger, and faster than they ever could before. Bodyshapers often become adventurers in order to put these philosophies into practice, helping them to pave the path toward the future only they can see, reflected in their enormous eye.

Evil Eye Adventurers

Evil eye adventurers enjoy classes that fit well with the obsessions and predilections of their oculus. Many choose to become spellcasters such as witches, wizards, psychics, or oracles, or scouts with classes like ranger, rogue, or investigator. Evil eyes prefer backgrounds along the same lines, such as archaeologist, artist, astrologer, bookkeeper, courier, detective, emissary, hermit, hunter, magical misfit, nomad, occult librarian, root worker, scholar, scout, student of magic, and teacher.

Curseseekers

While most sapient creatures fear and disdain curses for their dangerous and malefic usages, curseseekers possess a kind of wide-eyed optimism and enthusiasm toward curses that leaves other ancestries (and even some other evil eyes) nonplussed and scratching their heads. To a curseseeker, many of whom believe a variant on the origin story that evil eyes arose from ancient curses or curse wards, a curse is an exciting and beautiful mystery to unravel, with its own story to tell. Where most might shudder in revulsion at the curse’s effect and look no further, a curseseeker gazes deeply into the abyss to learn more.

Curses rarely come out of nowhere, after all. They are tied to the essence of spirit and typically arise only out of a powerful but twisted connection between two things in the pattern of reality, often formed from a strong emotion, wish, or desire. And that means there’s much to learn from a curse! Based on the curse’s effects, vector, and victim, who might have created the curse, and why? Is that different from the person who enacted the curse, or was it an original creation? What does that tell us about the relationship between the curse’s originator, the curse’s practitioner, and the curse’s recipient? How did the practitioner feel about the recipient? What was the curse intended to do? If the curse was a form of revenge or payback, what was the original slight or grudge that spawned it? If it was a death curse, what does it tell us about the life of the creature whose mortality was extinguished to create the curse? If the curse was created out of a twisted wish, what was the original wish? And since the curse is formed of a twisted connection and connections go both ways, did the curse rebound back on the caster?

These are just some of the countless questions a curseseeker asks when they encounter a new curse for the first time. While those who first hear that evil eyes have an oculus called “curseseeker” often imagine some sort of brooding and lugubrious figure riddled with angst or spite, curseseekers are often exactly the opposite of this expectation—cheerful and excitable, especially when mysterious new curses are involved. Get a curseseeker started talking about curses, and they might not stop rapidly talking at you for hours on end. If you can manage to follow along with the conversation and choose to do so, you’ve just earned a friend for life. While you might be subjected to more such meandering rhapsodizing, if you or a loved one ever encounter an unknown curse, it just might save your life, as your curseseeker comrade is sure to barge in with all sorts of ideas. Just make sure they actually get around to treating the curse instead of examining it before it’s too late!

Emissaries

Emissaries are the oculus containing the rare evil eyes who well and truly get along with other evil eyes. In fact, emissaries take that one step further: they go out of their way to seek out isolated evil eyes and engage them in a bit of socialization, hence the name for the oculus. Because of their gregarious and extroverted nature, emissaries have an outsized chance compared to their low population to be one of the parents of a new evil eye, and when they are, they’re more likely than other evil eyes to try to meet their child and have a relationship with them. Unfortunately for these emissaries who want to form family bonds, despite the ancestral memories awakening within their child, the new evil eye is no more likely to share the emissary’s personality and social predilections than any other evil eyes are. As a result, these attempts at parental affection usually end in the same way, even if all of the parents were emissaries.

While this eventuality doesn’t stop emissaries from having more children than other evil eyes, those emissaries who fail time and time again to form relationships with their offspring tend to eventually grow a thicker eyelid when it comes to being given the cold shoulder by their children. They come to the realization that it’s best to simply let their children leave the nest with grace. Instead of trying to force a relationship with their biological offspring, these older, wiser emissaries tend to try to build a found family with other emissaries as well as members of other ancestries. This makes emissaries especially likely to join or found adventuring parties and other organizations where they can belong and meet other like-minded people, whether they be evil eyes or otherwise.

Evil Eye Settlements

Solitary as they are, there’s only a few settlements of evil eyes, founded by emissaries who gathered together in one place. The settlements possess a distinctive architecture owing to evil eye aesthetics, with a tendency toward radial symmetry and spherical rooms with large panoramic windows. These settlements are built near a source of water and structured to use all three dimensions, with nearly spherical stacks on top of each other, descending into the water below for aquatic evil eyes and into the air above for winged evil eye to easily traverse. Of course, members of other ancestries sometimes find it difficult to use these thoroughfares, as there aren’t always ladders or stairs available.

Historiarchs

Some historians call history a diverse tapestry of beautiful contradictions woven together from many conflicting first-hand accounts and rife with all sorts of different interpretations. To a historiarch, however, all these contradictions must be resolved, and one single “best” truth must prevail. There must always be a winner and a loser, leading to a dominant narrative that will stand the test of time and be remembered through all posterity. Given this fundamental struggle of history between “the truth” and a thousand thousand shades of lies, the truth needs a champion, someone who will fight for it with everything they have. And that someone is the historiarch.

Every historiarch has certain beliefs about history (and sometimes about other social sciences as well) that they consider to be fundamental and incontrovertible truths. For many, this includes their own personal version of the evil eye ancestry’s origin story, but that’s often only a single point on the vast web of history that the historiarch considers sacrosanct. Of course, a historiarch would be the first to tell you that this doesn’t mean that a historiarch is dogmatically unwilling to reconsider the truths of history in the face of definitive proof that their beliefs were wrong. Still, they’re usually looking into something in a murky enough time frame or on a vague enough scale that it’s virtually impossible to fully prove the matter one way or the other. When you add that to the fact that they’re subject to their own confirmation bias when it comes to any new information, it becomes a near certainty that they’ll hold to their convictions come what may. Still, stories tell of historiarchs who adjusted or amended their views to adapt to new evidence (the euphemism they prefer to use rather than admit they were wrong), and, of course, other historiarchs hold those who choose to do so in the highest regard. After all, without the wisdom and grace to amend their views, how else will those other historiarchs one day come to realize the historiarch’s own truth is correct?

While historiarchs give lectures and publish books and articles to promulgate their views in scholarly circles and try to “win” the battle for history, they don’t rest on their laurels when it comes to gathering data and evidence to prove their claims. Just because they know it’s right doesn’t mean others do, and they understand that those others won’t simply take the historiarch’s word for it without the proper proof. This leads historiarchs to canvass and survey for valuable data that can be used by historians and other social scientists of any ancestry regardless of their views—as long as they’re careful to check for the historiarch’s bias before they do. This often means that historiarchs are the best informed when it comes to other points of view on history, society, and other similar issues. After all, if they can’t understand an opposing viewpoint completely and become capable of explaining it in a fair and unbiased manner, it would be much harder to refute that viewpoint and pick it apart analytically. These fact-finding historiarchs often fund adventures to all sorts of places and then join along themselves—of course, if they can join a party heading out to a site that interests them on someone else’s silver, though, the more, the better!

Observers

Observers are evil eyes with a simple goal: they want to see all that they can see. Many observers believe some variant of the origin story that evil eyes arose from some kind of scrying sensor. Thus, they feel they are actualizing their inner selves by observing as many scenes and experiences as possible.

Many observers become adventurers, travelers, and wanderers in order to head to far-flung locations more easily and behold diverse vistas and cultures. Their skills of observation make observers outstanding scouts, rangers, investigators, and the like, which at first might endear them to any adventuring party. Who wouldn’t want to have a comrade who is one step ahead of their foes at spotting ambushes and gathering information? However, the dark side of their insatiable curiosity is that they don’t feel that the concepts of secrecy and privacy really apply to them. That doesn’t mean that they blab others’ secrets if they know the other person wants it to stay private—in fact, quite the opposite. Observers are excellent at keeping a secret once they uncover it. However, observers feel that they have the right to observe everything, and they will go to great lengths to invade privacy and unearth secrets in order to observe them. In the process of doing so, they might expose the secrets to others as well. For this reason, their companions quickly learn that they might as well let the observer in on their private moments and secrets to start, at least if they want to keep associating with the observer.

For their part, observers usually aren’t looking into secrets for some sort of lurid voyeuristic thrill but rather out of a more obsessive completionist tendency to observe everything, especially whatever is rare or more difficult to observe, like secrets. From seeking out these rare sights, older observers have usually seen it all, and they often use that insight to pull out a surprising solution to even the most obscure of troubles. This makes them especially handy in a pinch and can help mend their relationships with those who aren’t as willing to surrender their secrets.

Evil Eye Artwork

While bodyshapers use their own body as a canvas for their art, evil eyes of all oculi enjoy creative pursuits such as art and music. However, they have unique perspectives on aesthetics, both from other ancestries and from each other, focused on their own personal perspective of the world around them rather than a photorealistic style. This can cause serious problems for evil eyes who seek to make a living as an artist, as their works often take a long time to become popular due to their unusual nature. Over time, they might become famous as avant-garde artists due to their unique styles, but often, not until it’s too late for the evil eye artist to enjoy the benefits of their popularity. Those who become famous in their lifetime often reach legendary status within a specific kind of artistic community. These famous evil eye artists routinely earn enormous commissions for art pieces that leave most onlookers more perplexed than satisfied but spur their fans to great heights of ardor.

Wells Of Wisdom

Wells of wisdom have a parallel with observers, but where observers seek to see and experience all that the world has to offer, wells of wisdom seek to learn all the knowledge in the world. The distinction is important: observers don’t mind much about what happened in the past and don’t care for secondhand accounts. They want to see things as they are now. Meanwhile, memories and books are favorites for wells of wisdom, who often seek lost knowledge in mysterious places. Wells of wisdom tend to believe some version of the origin story for the evil eye ancestry where the first evil eye was a deity’s eye plucked out in exchange for ultimate knowledge. For this reason, they feel that knowledge is their birthright and their obligation. They have a responsibility to collect knowledge as children and aftermath of this great bargain of the gods.

Wells of wisdom are often found in libraries, academies, ancient ruins, and other places where rare, obscure, and lost knowledge can be found. Depending on the group’s motivations and conduct, this often brings them either in line or in conflict with treasure-hunting adventuring parties seeking plunder and mysteries in the same ruins. A well of wisdom can provide sage advice and knowledge as a comrade for fellow adventurers who love knowledge (or even those who don’t care about knowledge but are willing to preserve it and leave it to the well of wisdom), but they become bitter foes to adventurers who destroy historical artifacts and precious lore in their haste to quickly extract the most valuable pieces of treasure for themselves.

Beliefs

You can use the following tendencies to guide you in choosing an alignment if you’re playing with alignment in your game or to assist with characterization for evil eyes if not: Evil eyes are highly individualistic and don’t care much about matters of morality, which tends to make them more chaotic than not and neutral between good and evil. However, it’s exactly because of that extreme individualism that evil eyes of all tendencies exist. That means evil eyes range from the most virtuous evil eye champion defending the weak to the most irredeemable evil eye marauder causing chaos and devastation, and from the most capricious evil eye liberator freeing those in captivity to the most ironclad evil eye tyrant oppressing others and crushing their freedom in their iron stalks.

Evil eyes tend to have highly personal faiths involving their own unique interpretations of various deities, religions, and philosophies. While just about all deities have some level of following among evil eyes, they are somewhat more likely to gravitate toward deities of chaos, creation, knowledge, magic, and perception. In the world of Alacar, Lilin the Mother, the Three Siblings of Time (especially Herst), and Kuu the Empty Vessel are all favorites among many evil eyes, with preferences varying depending on the individual evil eye’s beliefs on the nature of the evil eye ancestry as a whole.

Popular Edicts promote your own preferred name and narrative for your ancestry, follow your own path, observe all around you, and grow from it

Popular Anathemas refuse to see that which is right in front of you, go along with the crowd just because something is popular, back down on your principles

Names

Evil eyes almost always come up with their own personal name based on the echoed remnants of their ancestral memory stirring in their mind, and even those who knew their parents and received a name from them usually pick a new name for themselves, casting off their parents’ chosen name when they strike off on their own. Depending on the individual evil eye’s preferences, the name might be short and easy to say (perfect for calling out quickly in the middle of a crisis situation), long and magnificent to the evil eye’s tastes, or anywhere in between. There’s even an evil eye named “A” due to wanting a short and easy name and a desire always to be listed alphabetically first among any lists of evil eyes ever assembled.

Sample Names

A, Azarararararaz, Eye-Eye, Gorthax, Grand Emperor Ocularius III, Harry, Isthir the All-seeing, Mati, Medium Mario, Mimir, Observer, Orbulon, Sleuth, Tollux, Xanthippe, Yan.
