Guardians of Stone and Riddle.name: 石与谜的守护者 Guardians of Stone and Riddle
Guardians of Stone and Riddle.text:
摘自艾瑟莉娅·尼克斯的档案
石与谜语的守护者

世上大多数生物都是由机缘与偶然所造就，在无数世代中以百万倍的方式层层累积。每天清晨在树枝上啁啾的小鸣禽，也不过是事故与演化的产物。没有人坐下来决定红色是它胸脯最合适的颜色，也没有人裁定“啾啾啾”比“喀啾-喀啾-喀啾”更赏心悦目。一切只是恰好如此。也没有任何神祇决定乌龟就该披戴那般辉煌的甲壳，或猫就该是那样无礼的小畜生。大多数生物的出现没有任何理由，除了它们自身；它们也没有比自我塑造更宏大的目的。

然后，还有斯芬克斯。最明确、最戏剧性地说，斯芬克斯是一种被造出来的物种。很久以前，在永恒的迷雾深处，一位神祇（……究竟是哪一位仍颇有争议）在某种神性等同于写生簿的东西前坐下，开始构思一只野兽：狮子的身体。人类、鹰、豺，或公羊的头。按口味再加上鹰的翅膀。以石雕成。注入呼吸。并赋予其对谜语不可遏止的偏爱。斯芬克斯被打造出来就是为了守护。这一单一任务决定了它们物种的整个设计。

然而。斯芬克斯与其他生物一样拥有自由意志。它们既非无心亦非无魂；事实上，它们异常聪慧，机智敏锐，并且极其喜爱谜题与悖论。当然，从神祇的角度来看，问题在于：当你拿一个聪明、会思考的存在，并给它一条要求终身服役的神圣诫命时，它们就会开始提出尴尬的问题。比如为什么，或它们的目的是什么？或者生命的意义何在？它们会发展出哲学与神学。若不十分小心，它们甚至会发展出政治——尽管斯芬克斯大多还没掌握那门黑暗艺术。

当然，它们的哲学仍以谜语为中心。要描述谜语对斯芬克斯的重要性并不容易。对大多数人而言，谜语只是游戏，是打发闲暇片刻的小小脑力谜题。对斯芬克斯而言，谜语是一种生活方式，一种思维方式。它们蕴含真理，却从不直接说出真理是什么。它们同时遮蔽与暗示。它们通过负空间揭示现实，就像一幅完全由阴影绘成的画。对斯芬克斯来说，世界就是一道谜语——它是秘密、是谜团、是谜箱，充满隐匿的真相与秘密学识。但——而这对斯芬克斯同样重要——这是一道带有线索的谜。答案永远不会显而易见，但它确实存在；如果斯芬克斯足够聪明、足够狡黠，若它们长久而深入地思索这道谜，或许就能刮去几粒真理的砂砾。这不会容易（值得做的事很少轻易），但它可以做到。

这确实是一种非常乐观的看待世界的方式。

—— 艾瑟莉娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物辩护者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
Guardians of Stone and Riddle

Most creatures in the world come about from chance and happenstance, stacked a millionfold over untold generations. The little songbird that chirps from the tree branch each morning came about from nothing but accident and evolution. No one sat down and determined that red was the most appropriate color for its breast, nor that cheep-cheep is more aesthetically pleasing than ka-chirp-ka-chirp. It just happened that way. Nor did any god decide that turtles should wear such glorious shells, nor that cats should be such impudent little beasts. Most creatures came about for no reason but their own, and have no greater purpose but what they make for themselves.

And then there’s sphinxes. The sphinxes are, most pointedly and dramatically, a made species. Long ago, in the misty depths of eternity, a deity (…which one remains a question of some debate) sat down before some deific equivalent of a sketchpad and started brainstorming a beast. Body of a lion. Head of a human, or hawk, or jackal, or ram. Wings of an eagle added to taste. Sculpted of stone. Imbued with breath. And given an ineluctable fondness for riddles. Sphinxes were crafted to guard. This singular task determined the very design of their species.

And yet. Sphinxes, no less than other creatures, have free will. They are neither mindless nor soulless and, in fact, are unusually intelligent, with sharp wits and a great fondness for puzzles and paradox. The problem, of course, from the gods’ perspective, is that when you take an intelligent, thinking being and give it a divine commandment requiring lifelong service, they start to ask awkward questions. Like why or what is their purpose? Or what is the meaning of life? They develop philosophy and theology. If one is not very careful they even develop politics, though sphinxes mostly haven’t mastered that dark art yet.

Their philosophy is still centered on riddles, of course. It’s hard to describe the importance of riddles to a sphinx. To most people, a riddle is just a game, a little mental puzzle to tide away a spare minute. To sphinxes, riddles are a way of life, a way of thinking. They contain truth, without ever saying what the truth is. They at once obfuscate and suggest. They reveal reality through their negative spaces, like a painting drawn entirely of shadows. To sphinxes, the world is a riddle—it’s a mystery, an enigma, a puzzle-box, full of hidden truth and secret lore. But—and this is just as important to sphinxes—it’s a mystery with clues. The answer will never be obvious, but it exists, and if a sphinx is clever enough, cunning enough, if they think long and hard on the riddle, they might shave away a few grains of that truth. It won’t be easy (things worth doing rarely are), but it can be done.

It’s a very optimistic way of looking at the world, really.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Sphinx Ancestry.name: 斯芬克斯族裔 Sphinx Ancestry
Sphinx Ancestry.text:
Life is a riddle—and sphinxes intend to solve it.

Long ago, when the world was young, and sparrows still built their nests in dreams, one of the gods was faced with a problem. They had ownership of a great and glorious shrine, quite the envy of every other divinity in the heavens. It had columns of marble the color of seafoam and a votive statue clad in gold foil and wreathed in laurel leaves. The altar was always heavy with frankincense and myrrh, and the priests were scrupulously pious in their rituals. Unfortunately, all of this made the temple a most appealing target for less reverent souls, and the priests, while admirably faithful, tended to be sharp of mind rather than stout of limb.

Now, the god could have simply rained lightning down on any impious bandits, but that was a tedious task, and this god had a low tolerance for boredom. So the god decided to delegate. To that end, they crafted a guardian from sand, stone, and wind. Scanning the world for inspiration, they settled on the body plan of a lion for their template. To this, the god added an eagle’s wings for flight, and a human’s head for intellect. On further consideration, the god discreetly added opposable thumbs into the stew. This was the first anthrosphinx.

“Go forth, my servant, and may you and your kin defend my temple forevermore," so the god commanded, breathing life into the first sphinx. And the sphinx obeyed. Perhaps because of how their creator made them, but likely because enthusiastically agreeing with one’s creator is the key to living longer than an hour as a new divinely made beast. We never hear about the divinely forged ancestries who rebelled at their first task.

The sphinx guarded the temple faithfully, but they quickly had a problem. The god had said to guard the temple but hadn’t specified who was or wasn’t allowed entrance—they wanted the temple to be safe, but ultimately any visitor presents some risk. On the other hand, a temple with no petitioners is a sorry sight indeed. So the sphinx took counsel with the priests of the shrine and decided to set a riddle for every petitioner that came, thus separating the worthy—those who could solve the riddle—from the unworthy. The worthy were allowed to worship at the shrine, and the unworthy were kindly thanked for their time and told of a marvelous inn down in the village they could visit instead. Excessively or persistently unworthy people instead joined the sphinx as lunch. In the fullness of time, the sphinx passed away, but their sacred role was taken up by their children. The stories aren’t clear on where those children came from, leading to competing theories, including the wild idea that since the sphinx lived with the priests for a couple of centuries, occasionally, they must have had discussions that weren’t theological. As the generations passed, the number of sphinxes grew greater than a single shrine could support—or rather more to the point, that a single shrine could feed. So, after much riddling discussion, the sphinxes took off for other parts, and thus sphinxes came into the world.

That’s part of the story.

The other part goes like this: Some time later, a different god found themselves in need of a guardian. Not inclined to mess with a winning formula, this second god came to the shrine in the form of a petitioner and observed the sphinxes guarding it most closely. Then, returning to their divine domain, they crafted another beast, with the body of a lion, the wings of an eagle, and the head of a ram—the god had always been fond of rams, and there was a line between inspiration and plagiarism.

Then, a third god did the same thing. And then a fourth.

Today, four kinds of sphinx are found in the world: the human-headed anthrosphinx, the ram-headed criosphinx, the falcon-headed hieracosphinx, and the jackal-headed cynosphinx. Traditionally, the anthrosphinx is considered the original sphinx, though this may have more to do with the fact that humans have historians and storytellers, while sheep, falcons, and jackals, outside of quite exceptional circumstances, do not.

In truth, these four heritages are four separate species of being but are similar enough that all may fit under the broad umbrella of “sphinx.” Since their origin, they have spread around the world, though always maintaining a fondness for guardianship and a love of riddles that borders on the pathological.

You Might...

  • Absolutely adore riddles, puzzles, and mysteries of all kinds.
  • Have a strong protective instinct towards certain people, places, or objects.
  • Spend a lot of time adjusting your environment to be quadruped-friendly.

Others Probably...

  • Forget that you’re a worldly and educated individual, not a rampaging monster.
  • Need to help you with doors or with carrying things over a long distance.
  • Assume that you are ancient and wise, even if you’re barely fledged.

Sphinx Adventurers

Before they settle down, many sphinxes wander the earth, in the process becoming rather similar to adventurers. The classic background for a sphinx adventurer, of course, is the hermit, with acolytes, fortune tellers, guards, and scholars rounding out the mixture. Sphinxes, with their love of twisty riddles and prodigious memories, are naturally drawn to all of the spellcasting classes—a sphinx might be a studious wizard, ruminating on ancient lore, a pious cleric serving the gods, or a bard with a streak of grandiosity to their riddle-weaving. More martially inclined sphinxes might be monks, disciplined and otherworldly, or they might choose the opposite direction and become barbarians, channeling their sublime ferocity in battle. Investigator sphinxes are, of course, quite common, as are thaumaturges.

Physical Description

Mostly, a sphinx looks like a lion. That is to say, they are large, feline creatures with a broad-shouldered frame, a short fur coat, usually of a tawny complexion, though silver-gray, brown, and yellow-red aren’t unheard of, with the belly and throat being somewhat lighter in tone. They possess a tufted tail containing a small bone “spike” within the tuft, and adult males sometimes grow a thick mane of dark-colored fur. Departing from the leonine body plan a bit, sphinxes have a subtly different structure of their front paws, giving them opposable thumbs and the ability to manipulate objects just as skillfully as any human when needed. The head of a sphinx varies significantly based on their heritage—the four known and acknowledged types are human, falcon, jackal, and sheep (though even female criosphinxes have curling horns). Adventurers have occasionally described run-ins with crocodile or vulture-headed sphinxes, or even more rarely scorpion-headed ones, but these sightings are few and far between.

A portion of sphinxes further possess a set of wings—in myth and legend, these are analogized to those of a great eagle. However, closer examination reveals them to resemble those of no known bird (if anything, they often look slightly vulture-like). Wings usually begin growing around the sphinx’s second or third decade, and while no one is entirely certain about their cause, accepted wisdom among some groups of sphinxes is that wings develop in response to a life spent wandering and hunting above ground. There’s usually a hint of censure in this view, the idea that a proper sphinx with a temple to guard wouldn’t have any need to go gallivanting around in the air.

A sphinx’s lifecycle is only partially linked to their age. Typically, the sphinxes one sees wandering around or doing adventure-adjacent activities are juveniles, only about the size of a leopard or so. A sphinx remains a juvenile until they gain a territory to protect, at which point they enter a growth spurt and become the size of a full lion—for most sphinxes this happens when they are between fifty and eighty years old, but situations where a juvenile sphinx finds a territory to protect at a younger or older age are hardly unknown. A small proportion of mature sphinxes, for reasons opaque to most, become elder sphinxes, gaining an additional growth spurt and the ability to transform into statues of stone.

Sphinxes usually live between two hundred and two hundred and fifty years, although elder sphinxes can “cheat” that by spending most of their time in idol form. Such an elder sphinx only ages when their body is flesh and blood, so many elder sphinxes live effectively for thousands of years.

Sphinxes’ physical and magical connection to stone arises from their origin as sand, stone, and wind, and so sometimes sphinxes who refuse to settle down and continue to adventure develop power over stone without finding a territory to protect or aging into an elder sphinx. These physical changes often herald a sphinx who is destined for greater adventures and might not stop to guard a territory for a long, if ever.

Society

Whatever the truth of the foundational myth related earlier, the fact of the matter is that sphinxes are clearly an artificially created species designed for protecting or guarding designated sites. Some of this artificiality manifests in their physical bodies—sphinxes eat surprisingly little for creatures of their size, and they don’t need much water either. But much of the rest manifests in their psyches, which are mostly standard for sapient species but with a couple of very significant quirks.

First, sphinxes have a powerful possessive urge that manifests itself in extreme protectiveness. Sphinxes look around and claim things as “theirs” and then go out of their way to protect what they view as theirs against anyone who might harm or desecrate it. Traditionalist sphinxes usually interpret this urge to refer to specific locations, but it could be applied to more movable objects, to people, or even to certain high-minded ideals. Young sphinxes typically spend some time looking for what they will eventually protect, but virtually every sphinx will sooner or later choose to protect something valuable and important.

Secondly, sphinxes have a mind that is amazingly skilled at making its own entertainment. They aren’t necessarily more intelligent than other species, though most sphinxes tend to be clever, but more than that they have a magnificent memory and a keen ability to visualize and organize information in their heads. A sphinx can appear to be drowsing on a rock overlooking a temple while their mind is occupied with composing an epic lyrical poem, remembering every exact word, calling up sources and citations, and keeping the entire thing in their head for hours or even days until they get around to putting pen to paper. A sphinx has a threshold for boredom an order of magnitude higher than that of other living things.

The famous sphinx fascination with riddles is part and parcel of this. On the one hand, a riddle for a sphinx is a way of sorting the worthy from the unworthy, of deciding who gets access to what the sphinx protects and who gets to join the sphinx as dinner instead (…it must be noted that some sphinxes don’t view eating sapient beings as cannibalism, though it is very gauche to do so if their dinner guests haven’t failed the sphinx’s riddle). At the same time, sphinxes view riddles as philosophical questions, particularly enjoying classical koans or other paradoxes, and spending years unraveling their intricacies.

Sphinx communities tend to be relatively small and familial, looking broadly like a lion’s pride. At the pinnacle of the pride sits the ruling couple—or ruling polycule, as the case may be. Sphinxes are pretty relaxed about the exact arrangement of relationships, but they do mate for life. A few younger or junior siblings accompany this core group, as well as possibly some aged dowager or elder sphinx who isn’t interested in running things day-to-day. Finally, most prides, barring brand new ones, include a group of cubs and younger juveniles, who learn the pride’s ways and perform all the chores their parents give them under the guise of “character-building.”

Eventually, the juvenile sphinxes strike out on their own, either solo or, less commonly, in “bachelor prides” of unattached juveniles. These are the sphinxes most likely to become adventurers, as they are looking for territories and also just generally learning more about themselves on their journeys. In due time, the young sphinx finds some combination of themselves, their territories, and their life partners, at which point they either settle down to start their own pride or return to their home pride with an eye towards eventually inheriting it.

Sphinx Prides

Sphinx prides, compared to those of more settled peoples, tend to be rather small—the average pride has only about a dozen members in it, with the largest and most powerful pride topping out at about thirty sphinxes. This makes sphinx prides slightly smaller than the lion prides that share the same collective name. Sphinx prides can be further subdivided into wilderness prides and urban prides, which each have their own distinct tendencies. Wilderness prides live alone, usually clustered around some abandoned ruin or antique shrine, and make their living off of hunting. A wilderness pride often claims a vast territory to get enough game to feed themselves, though as they don’t really care about farmland, they typically have a friendly enough relationship with local communities. Urban prides, on the other hand, live alongside larger humanoid communities, often finding themselves a niche as scholars and guardians, adjacent to gods and royalty, or very occasionally serving as monarchs and demigods in their own right.

Riddles

Cross-cutting against the system of prides is the question of riddles. Sphinxes can be ravenous and unforgiving when so inclined, but they can also be highly intellectual people, and few things interest a sphinx quite as much as a new riddle or philosophical conundrum. Most sphinxes spend a good portion of their lives contemplating deep thoughts, and over time their musings on the nature of sphinxhood have resulted in a series of philosophical schools. What does it mean to be a sphinx? Why were they made? What is the purpose of their protection? Can anything be truly safe? Sphinxes being sphinxes, they tend to arrange these philosophical schools around riddles. In addition to the riddles listed here, sphinxes have postulated countless other riddles over the millennia, including the nature of freedom and morality.

The Old Riddle

“‘What goes on four legs in the morning, two legs in the afternoon, and three legs in the evening?" “A humanoid, who crawls as a baby, walks on two legs as an adult, and uses a walking stick in their twilight years.”

One of the oldest riddles pondered by sphinxes, the Old Riddle contemplates the nature of age, time, and the transience of all such things. Sphinxes who follow the old riddle appreciate the ephemerality of all life, not only their own but also those of the living things about them, particularly the unfortunately fragile and easily digestible humans in their orbit.

Thus, sphinxes who ponder the Old Riddle tend to frame their mission as protecting life, usually fencing it in a bit as protecting not all life (a sphinx has to know their limits) but a specific community, family, city, or temple. To these sphinxes, the purpose of the original sphinx was not to protect the shrine but to guard the priests of the shrine, for without them, what is a shrine but a mere ruin? Older sphinxes of the Old Riddle usually form mutually beneficial relationships with other ancestries, and it’s not uncommon in certain old cities to find an elder sphinx serving as an advisor or sage for a mortal ruler.

Sphinxes of the Old Riddle are thus among the most sociable of sphinxes, and the ones most inclined to deal with other ancestries. After all, it’s rather tricky to protect someone if you won’t talk to them. They also tend to have a fairly benevolent view of nature as a whole, though this is tempered by their inherently predatory instincts—a sphinx might respect nature, but the nature they respect is unsympathetic to those who become prey, if not red in tooth and claw then at least a suggestive pink.

The Older Riddle

“There are two sisters: one gives birth to the other and she, in turn, gives birth to the first. Who are the two sisters?”

“Day and night.”

There is another riddle, as old as the Old Riddle and perhaps older yet. It deals with questions of time, but if the Old Riddle emphasizes the transience of life, then the Older Riddle deals with its circularity, eternity, and infinity. People may die, cities may fall, mighty empires may be toppled to the ground, but the sun and moon rise each day anew. They were there before and they will be there after.

Sphinxes that ponder the Older Riddle tend to view their task as protecting the natural order of the universe. This does raise the interesting question of how exactly one protects “time," but the sphinxes of the Older Riddle view this as a question of delegation. The gods protect the natural order, and sphinxes are the wardens of the gods. The purpose of the original sphinx protecting the shrine was to protect the gods—both from disrespect and from the distraction of having to deal with pettier mortal matters themselves. Sphinxes of the Older Riddle are most often found protecting temples, cemeteries, or other sacred spaces. Sphinxes of the Older Riddle lack the gregariousness of their Old Riddle kin. They tend to be pensive and philosophical in their attitudes, much given to ruminating on the nature of divinity and virtue, as well as other such big thoughts, even by the standards of other sphinxes. Among their kinfolk they have a reputation for being a bit gloomy, but none can dispute their wisdom.

Ancient Riddles

“I bite the biters, of my own accord I bite no one; but many are ready to bite me even though I bite. No one fears my bite, for teeth it has none.” Answer: Onion.

“Earth was I first, in earth’s recesses hidden; now fire has given me another price and name, and earth I am called no longer, though with me earth is bought.” Answer: Money.

“Great powers from little strength I bring. I open closed houses, but again I close the open. I guard the house for the master, but in turn am guarded by him.” Answer: Key.

“No fixed form is mine, yet none is stranger to me. My brightness lies within sparkling with radiant light, which shows nothing except what it has seen before.” Answer: Mirror

The Riddle Of The White Field

“In front, the oxen are led White fields are ploughed

A white plough is held

A black seed is sowed.”

“The oxen are the author’s thumb and forefinger, the field a page, the plough a pen, the seeds letters.”

One of the newer riddles among the sphinxes, the Riddle of the White Field speaks to the importance of knowledge, learning, science, reason, and understanding. It can be the riddle of mystery and mysticism, but key to the riddle is not merely the gathering of knowledge, but its recording and dissemination. To adherents of the White Field, knowledge locked away in some hoary old vault is dead and useless—only when it dances from mind to mind, by way of word and letter, does it have value.

The classic sphinx of the White Field guards a library, preferably big and bustling and filled to the brim with books, but by equal measure they might guard museums or schools or even just particularly significant historical sites. They might also guard important objects (most often books, though there is rumor of one sphinx who guards some great technological engine of belching steam and fire) or act as bodyguards for scholars, scientists, and mages.

In fact, many of the White Field’s followers are themselves mages of no little ability, or else researchers and inquirers in their own right. Their defining characteristic is their insatiable curiosity, rivaled only by their tendency to want to explain things. White Field sphinxes are natural-born teachers, which can occasionally result in a bit of awkwardness when the sphinx spends hours teaching something interesting to someone who turns out to be a trespasser or robber.

The Serivan Riddle

“As I went to Serives,

I met Nine Wives,

And every Wife had nine Sacks,

And every Sack had nine Cats,

And every Cat had nine Kittens,

Kits, cats, sacks, and wives,

How many were going to Serives?”

“One—only the speaker, for all the others are coming from Serives.”

The Serivan Riddle (so named for the town of Serives, not that anyone has ever figured out where Serives was actually located) teaches sphinxes to expect the unexpected, to embrace paradox and mystery, and to learn to think outside the box (or the temple grounds, as it were). Unless, that is, the speaker in the riddle actually met the nine wives while all were traveling the same way, in which case it’s a straightforward application of mathematics and thus cautions sphinxes about being too clever by half. Somehow the fact that no one can completely agree on the answer of the Serivan Riddle is entirely appropriate. Serivan sphinxes usually go out of their way to guard things that strike them as odd, weird, or peculiar. What that actually means differs massively from sphinx to sphinx, but it can be as grand as a mountain floating in midair or as mundane as a mortal with an interesting-looking tattoo. Among Serivan sphinxes, it’s considered extremely impolite to mock someone’s choice of guardianship, however incomprehensible the decision might be.

Serivan sphinxes tend to be off-kilter, odd personalities. It’s a riddle that appeals to the young, and in fairness, plenty of Serivan sphinxes tend to be younger than average, and a bit childish sometimes. Even older Serivan sphinxes often maintain an air of whimsicality about themselves. At the same time, whimsical doesn’t mean foolish, and their diverse experiences mean that a Serivan sphinx often knows something useful about all kinds of unexpected topics.

Heritages

The following are the most common sphinx heritages.

Anthrosphinx

Anthrosphinxes have humanlike heads, which gives them a considerable advantage in interacting with other societies. They have familiar facial expressions, and they can laugh, smile, weep, and scowl. Compared to the expressionless gaze of a falcon or the hungry grin of a jackal, an anthrosphinx feels almost homey. Most anthrosphinxes take advantage of this and play up their civil and urbane aspect—like any sphinx, an anthrosphinx can be a deadly predator when the situation calls for it, but most anthrosphinxes go out of their way to downplay their more bestial nature, swathing it in an air of refined culture and persnickety etiquette.

Anthrosphinxes are naturally drawn to the Old Riddle, guarding cities, palaces, or other places bustling with humanity—usually, if an ordinary citizen has met a sphinx, then they’ve met an anthrosphinx of the Old Riddle. Those anthrosphinxes associated with the Older Riddle are still liable to be more sociable than others who follow that riddle, guarding active temples or monasteries rather than abandoned shrines, while sphinxes of the White Field are more apt to find a school or university friendly than some ancient library. It’s not at all uncommon for such a sphinx to pick up mortal titles or gain ranks within whatever society they’ve come to call home. The Serivan Riddle appeals least to anthrosphinxes—such a sphinx is usually a misfit or loner of some sort, viewed with a mixture of bemusement and pity by their kin.

Criosphinx

Ram-headed criosphinxes are known for their calm, unflappable temperaments and are proverbial for their wisdom, even among generally learned sphinxes. That said, it might be more accurate to say that criosphinxes, rather than simply being inherently placid, tend to be slower to change their mindsets—a criosphinx has a very long fuse, but once actually enraged, they can hold a grudge until mountains grind to dust.

Criosphinxes are equally at ease with both civilization and the wilderness, though, as a general rule, most avoid the hustle and bustle that anthrosphinxes so love. A criosphinx of the White Field might prefer some silent library to an active university, while a criosphinx of the Older Riddle might choose a hermit or contemplative pilgrim to accompany. Serivan criosphinxes are fairly common, but at first glance might pass for a follower of a different riddle—they are just as apt to haunt libraries as their kin, unraveling strange mysteries from fragmentary accounts. Criosphinxes of the Old Riddle are probably the rarest, though not strictly rare, and usually find themselves with smaller communities, villages, caravans, or the like.

Cynosphinx

Jackal-headed cynosphinxes have long been associated with the dead and dying. They are mortuary monsters, much like ghosts and ghouls, though notably less dead than those examples, and whatever their habits, they find the dead to be a comforting and even familiar presence. Despite what one might expect, this doesn’t mean that cynosphinxes are a somber or melancholy bunch—quite the contrary, most cynosphinxes have a keen appreciation for life’s brevity, and approach existence with a kind of relaxed good cheer. They’ll enjoy life while they can, knowing that everything ends in time.

A cynosphinx of the Old Riddle is just as likely to haunt some old necropolis as a living city, while those of the Older Riddle might attend a mortuary temple or hallowed cemetery, and those of the White Field might inhabit some elder ruin. Serivan cynosphinxes are perhaps the more interesting of their kind, as they find ghosts and their mysteries particularly fascinating, and sometimes set themselves up as wandering speakers to the dead, resolving unfinished business and patching up the boundaries of life and death.

Hieracosphinx

Falcon-headed hieracosphinxes are the most aggressive and physical of all the sphinx heritages, though this is a relative sort of statement—even a hieracosphinx is still a thoughtful and cerebral creature. Still, they tend to be the most at ease with wilderness and solitude and the most uncomfortable with large crowds and bustling communities. Hieracosphinxes can be perfectly friendly, even loyal and trusted allies, but few hieracosphinxes enjoy dealing with more than a handful of people at a time. Strange as it may seem to say about a creature built like a monstrous lion, they tend to be shy. They tend to come out of their shells around trusted confidantes.

Hieracosphinxes usually avoid populated places (a hieracosphinx of the Old Riddle is a rare sight, to be sure) and so prefer isolated or quiet locales—holy groves for those of the Older Riddle, remote curiosities for the Serivan sphinxes, and so forth. They are, more than most sphinxes, inclined to adopt a human or even an object as their charge, in which case they follow them about rather than guarding some more substantial site or other permanent location.

Beliefs

You can use the following tendencies to guide you in choosing an alignment if you’re playing with alignment in your game, or to assist with characterization for sphinxes if not. Collectively, sphinxes are lawful to a fault. Their rules might be strange or incomprehensible to outsiders, but being a sphinx means following a particular code of conduct (especially regarding riddles) handed down since the species was first crafted, and violating those codes is a grave offense—a sphinx who kills someone who successfully solves their riddle would be a pariah, at least absent significant extenuating circumstances. Morally, sphinxes range from good to evil, though they tend to cluster around the middle. A sphinx may be basically compassionate or sadistically malicious, but generally, the rules come first.

Sphinxes worship an array of different gods, though they are drawn to the oldest deities of any pantheon, particularly identifying with those viewed as plausible candidates for creating the first sphinx. Deities of knowledge, wisdom, or the deep desert are especially common. On Alacar, they tend to worship Tovah the Father or Kuu from deities of the Balance, or older faiths from before the Balance, and some less-scrupulous sphinxes become obsessed with the malevolent cosmic mysteries that are the Enigmas.

Popular Edicts follow the rules of engagement, ponder the deeper riddles of the world, guard that which must be protected

Popular Anathemas go back on your word after a petitioner solves your riddle, display a lack of intellectual curiosity, shirk in your duties

Names

Sphinxes like long, sonorous names, something with many syllables that sounds impressive when recited while standing upon a stone plinth. In a pinch, most sphinxes are willing to accept some kind of shorter nickname for daily use like “Bak” or “Sen," but they are sure to do so with a long-suffering sigh.

Sample Names

Bakenetah, Djeserekan, Iufankhirose, Manakhtufu, Pabernefy, Tabisheretfan, Senefera.

Sphinx Heritages

Strictly speaking, the four sphinxes are four distinct species, each crafted by a separate god (supposedly, at least; the myths aren’t exactly a scrupulously reliable source), and each given different gifts and, most obviously, the heads of various creatures. Nonetheless, the four heritages can form families and prides together and have children. Usually a sphinx is of the same heritage as one of their parents, though this is far from a hard rule.

Carved in Stone.name: 镌刻于石之上 Carved in Stone
Carved in Stone.text:
出自艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯的档案
镌刻于石

凡人最终的虚荣，莫过于依照自身形象雕刻出被礼拜的肖像，将它们留给后世，直到它们也被时代的沙砾所吞没。然而，并非所有雕像都只是虚荣与自恋的纯粹演练。神圣的圣像学让凡人得以尝试将不可理解的无穷缩减为我们所熟悉的形态；我们将其拟人化，自欺欺人地相信自己能够理解。

另一方面，危险猛兽的雕像数千年来一直作为守护的象征，用以抵御神秘与物质层面的威胁。无论是动物还是法术构装体，都可能将雕像误认为潜伏在屋顶、伺机扑击的守护猛兽。这些建筑特性被称作“怪诞”，以指示其怪物般的外观，但我认为这一术语对其中所描绘的生物相当冒犯。所幸，凡人或许已开始改以“石像鬼”来称呼这些雕像——这个词与“gargle（漱口/咕噜作响）”同源，起初只用于指代一种既是怪诞之像、又能从建筑物排水的雕像。

被称为石像鬼的人们，将守护雕像的矫饰倒转了过来：他们是把自己伪装成雕像的人，而非被雕刻成凶猛人形的雕像。不必多说，他们更偏好“石像鬼”这个名字，并认为被称作“怪诞”轻则缺乏体贴，重则怀有恶意。正如他们所酷似的雕像一般，石像鬼是守望者，守卫并保护某人或某物，常常是他们筑巢栖身的地点。石像鬼与雕像融为一体的能力，是一种强大的防御能力与伏击策略，足以对付疏忽之人；但那些不与社群和睦共处、选择猎杀邻里而非守护的石像鬼，很快就会发现周遭那些无生命的石像鬼雕像被砸成瓦砾，令其失去一切藏身之处，也剥夺了他们的奇袭优势。毕竟，若一个社群里仅剩下看起来像石像鬼的雕像都是活的生物，那么无论他们多么擅长定格不动，他们的存在都将变得显而易见。反过来说，与本地石像鬼关系牢固的社群，可能会选择雕塑数以百计的诱饵，协助当地的守护者守卫夜晚。

不幸的是，石像鬼的外貌——它映照着当初促使原始雕像具备护符性质的同一种原始恐惧——使社群畏惧石像鬼，且很难迅速信任他们。这是残酷的讽刺，也引出了“先有鸡还是先有蛋”的问题：“究竟是我们称为石像鬼的族裔先出现，还是最初的建筑石像鬼先出现？”抑或这是并行演化，只在该族裔选择采用这种建筑特性的通用名称时才趋同，也许是为了进一步混淆视听，帮助他们在众目睽睽之下隐匿。唯有神祇与来自远古时代的其他长者存在，才知晓石像鬼何时在这个世界第一次苏醒之谜的真相——他们的心被镌刻于石中，并在那一刻首次跳动。

——艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
Carved in Stone

It’s the ultimate vanity of mortalkind that causes us to carve graven likenesses in our own image, leaving them behind for all posterity, until they too are consumed by the sands of time. But not all statuary is a pure exercise in vainglory and narcissism. Sacred iconography allows mortals an attempt to reduce an incomprehensible infinity into a familiar form that we anthropomorphize, deluding ourselves into believing we can understand.

On the other hand, statues of dangerous beasts have served for millennia as symbols of protection, warding against both mystical and physical threats. Animals and spell constructs alike might mistake the statue for a guardian beast lurking on the rooftop and waiting to pounce. These architectural features have been called grotesque, indicating their monstrous appearance, but I find the term quite offensive to the creatures depicted therein. Fortunately then, perhaps, mortalkind has begun to refer to these statues instead as gargoyles, a word that shares an etymology with “gargle” initially used only to refer to a grotesque that also served to drain water from the building.

The people known as gargoyles flip the conceits of protective statuary on their heads: they are people who disguise themselves as statues rather than statues made to look like ferocious people. Needless to say, they prefer the name “gargoyles,” and consider being called a “grotesque” to be insensitive at best and actively malicious at worst. Much like the statues they resemble, gargoyles are sentinels who defend and protect someone or something, often a location in which they make their lair. Gargoyles’ ability to blend in with statues is a formidable defensive ability and ambush strategy against the unwary, but those gargoyles who don’t live in harmony with the community and choose to hunt their neighbors rather than protect quickly find the inanimate gargoyle statues in the area smashed to rubble, robbing them of any hiding places and stealing away their element of surprise. After all, if the only remaining statues that look like gargoyles in a community are the living creatures, their presence becomes obvious no matter how well they freeze in place. On the flip side, a community with a strong relationship with their local gargoyles might choose to sculpt hundreds of decoys to assist their local protectors in defending the night.

Unfortunately, gargoyles’ appearance, mirroring as it does the same primal fears that led to the talismanic property of the original statues, leads communities to fear gargoyles and be slow to trust them. It’s a brutal irony and raises the chicken and egg question: “Which came first: the ancestry we call gargoyles or the first architectural gargoyles?” Or was it a parallel evolution, converging only when the ancestry chose to take on the common name of the architectural feature, perhaps to further obfuscate the situation and assist them in hiding in plain sight. Only the gods and other elder beings from ancient times know the true answer to the mystery of when gargoyles first awoke in this world, their hearts carved in stone and beating for the first time.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Gargoyle Ancestry.name: 石像鬼族裔 Gargoyle Ancestry
Gargoyle Ancestry.text:
Bound by honor, duty, and kinship, gargoyles are nocturnal guardians who maintain vigil over that which they hold dear and leverage their terrifying reputation to strike fear into their enemies.

According to folk tales and ancient legends, gargoyles are monstrous statues given life who maintain vigil over the buildings they’re magically bound to. Stone by day and terrifying beasts by night, they devour and consume trespassers. This is, of course, an entirely false narrative built around a few kernels of truth. Gargoyles themselves initially perpetuated stories like these, but they’ve long since entered the cultural zeitgeist and become accepted as fact. So then, what is a gargoyle?

Gargoyles are nocturnal, and many have flesh that looks rough or rocky. They’re incredibly patient and calm and can become still at a moment’s notice, slowing their breathing and heartbeat to a crawl and becoming statue-like. Canny and clever gargoyles leverage this to their advantage, filling their homes and the surrounding region with stone facsimiles of themselves and their clutchmates, and claiming each is a gargoyle at rest. Of course, only the most talented of gargoyles can truly turn their flesh to stone, and they certainly don’t sleep that way.

Above all, gargoyles are guardians. Each clutch and clan is bound to a specific person, structure, place, community, or object—not through magic, sorcery, or fell curses, but through vows, duty, and honor. This is a gargoyle’s “ward”—that thing which they would give their life to protect.

In defense of their wards, gargoyles train as warriors and spellcasters, and leverage their fearsome reputation to strike terror into their foes. After all, to a gargoyle, the best way to defeat an enemy is to ensure they’re too scared to fight at all.

How did gargoyles come to be this way? Peel away the tall tales spread by gargoyles to distract and confuse those who seek them and their wards harm, and what’s left are a few disparate narratives of possible origins. Some of these legends claim that gargoyles are tied to other stony guardians or protectors of wards, making them kin of lampad nymphs, sphinxes, or kami associated with earth and stone. Other origin myths claim that gargoyles were originally humanoids who gained their stony nature and monstrous appearance as part of a pact with a powerful entity—those who ascribe to these myths typically point to clutchswapped gargoyles, who look no different from another humanoid ancestry, as supporting evidence. A third tale tells of carved statues built as metaphorical protectors for an important building (which varies depending on who tells the tale) who were wished to life when the building was under attack by the desperate need of those within.

Most gargoyles don’t pay much heed to origin stories that each tie them to another sort of creature instead of allowing them to be their own people, independent of others. They pass down a variety of origin stories, but in all of them, gargoyles were always their own people. One that’s popular among several gargoyle clans tells of the nameless people who would come to be called gargoyles wandering the land aimlessly, looking for a home. They met with fear and prejudice until they found a place and felt a longing to protect it. They came to call it home, and the other people living there came to rely on the gargoyles as protectors, slowly building bonds of friendship and understanding. With that understanding came others who treated gargoyles with contempt or took them for granted, and they assisted an attacking force by spreading the gargoyles’ strengths and weaknesses. This special place fell to the invaders and the gargoyles were forced to flee, spreading across the world and finding new wards. According to this story, the memory of the betrayal is why gargoyles started spreading false rumors to confuse even their allies as to their own natures.

If you want to play an honorable sentinel, driven by duty to protect your ward, who leverages darkness and fear as weapons, you should play a gargoyle.

You Might...

  • Value the safety of your ward over that of yourself.
  • Spread rumors about yourself or your clutchmates, in order to build a fearsome reputation or public persona.
  • Keep a small collection of items that you personally value for the joy they bring you.

Others Probably...

  • Assume you turn to stone when exposed to daylight.
  • Trust that you can handle yourself in a fight.
  • Fear you.

Physical Description

The typical gargoyle is a humanoid, with rocky flesh and a variety of so-called “monstrous” features, from sharp claws and pointed fangs to wide lashing tails and curling horns and—their most iconic feature—the wide wings of a bat or demon. However, few gargoyles possess all these features, and some possess none at all! Each gargoyle is different and unique.

Gargoyles have smooth flesh, like polished marble, which they can transform to a rough, rocky texture by flexing. This ability enables them to imitate a variety of statues and rock types. In terms of coloration, grays and browns are by far the most common flesh tones, but muted shades of blues, purples, greens, reds, yellows, and other colors are possible. A gargoyle’s flesh is typically unblemished—dirt slips off swiftly, rarely scars, and is entirely hairless except for the hair that (usually) grows atop their head. Like many predators, gargoyles have sharp claws and fangs. Sometimes, they instead have talons, beaks, or an elongated snout.

Nearly all gargoyles have wings. Most have the broad wings of a bat, which they can fold and unfold and drape around themselves like capes and cloaks. Others have wing flaps stretching from the sides of their torso to the underside of their arms. Regardless of wing type, a gargoyle’s wings are built for catching the wind and gliding, not for true flight. They are, however, excellent climbers, which enables them to easily reach the high places they so adore.

Despite the prevalence of wings, not all gargoyles have them. Most wingless gargoyles are quadrupedal, rather than bipedal, or else number among the so-called “clutchswapped”—gargoyles who possess no “monstrous” features at all and can easily blend in among humans, elves, and other similar humanoid ancestries.

Many gargoyles possess horns, although the type can vary dramatically. The most prominent, though not especially common among gargoyles, are a pair of large horns located on the sides or crown of the head. These can be ridged or smooth in texture; forward, backward, or side pointing; and straight, curved, coiled, or even corkscrewed in shape. Most horned gargoyles lack these large horns and instead have a small crown, crest, or ridge of horns. For some, this crest of horns starts along the brow ridges, and for others, it’s higher up, at the peak of the forehead near the start of the hairline.

Perhaps the least common gargoyle feature is the tail, which is broad, tapered, and varies from 2 to 5 feet long. Usually, a gargoyle’s tail is smooth, but some have reptilian ridges (much like a dragon or dinosaur) or are tipped in a club or sharp tip. Regardless of appearance, a gargoyle’s tail is used to aid in balance—though some are strong enough that they can be used as a weapon.

Gargoyle Adventurers

Gargoyles are natural predators, and many become fighters or rangers. Those who excel at acts of subterfuge, stealth, or rumormongering, instead lean toward bards or rogues. Gargoyle adventurers tend to gravitate toward classes that favor order and hard work, such as champion, monk, magus, and wizard. Some talented gargoyles have an innate connection with the forces of earth, stone, and wind, and are likely to become kineticists or elemental avatars—such gargoyles are known as worldspeakers, and are incredibly well-respected and honored among their kin.

Gargoyle backgrounds include those that speak to their duties as well as those that speak to their personal interests. Some popular backgrounds include animal whisperer, artisan, artist, emissary, entertainer, farmhand, herbalist, hunter, laborer, martial disciple, nomad, pilgrim, scholar, scout, and warrior.

Society

Gargoyles are community-minded and charitable, with a stern or stoic countenance and strong work ethic. Most willingly perform acts for the good of the clutch or community without thought of recompense or needing to be asked. They’re proud and brave and always ready to leap to the defense of their ward or those in need.

Gargoyles lay eggs, usually in groups of 2 to 4, which are then left to incubate in a safe, enclosed area called a rookery. All gargoyles in a clan lay their eggs in the same rookery, making it a communal space. Gargoyle eggs are hard and heavy, look carved from rock, maintain their internal temperature, and don’t need attention or care. Thus, gargoyles lay their eggs and then leave them, and they don’t act as parents. All the eggs in a rookery hatch every few years, regardless of how long they’ve been gestating, with the exact time and reason for their hatching a mystery. Gargoyles emerge from their eggs infantile but entirely capable of vocalizing, crawling, walking, and fine motor skills. All gargoyles hatched at the same time are part of the same clutch, with gargoyles considering their clutchmates their immediate family. A gargoyle’s clutchmates are simultaneously their siblings, teammates, rivals, and dearest friends. The clutch is the most integral relationship in gargoyle society.

Shortly after a clutch forms, one or two gargoyles will usually find the clutch through instinct or accident and become mentors to the clutch. While love can develop between mentors and their associated clutch, the relationship is more akin to that of a teacher rather than family. As the members of a clutch grow older and develop diverse interests, the mentors’ job ends, and the members of a clutch engage with new mentors—senior individuals outside the clutch who have the skills or talents they want to develop within themselves. However, the bonds between clutchmates are strong and subconscious, and gargoyles always return to their clutchmates in times of need, friendship, or longing.

Often, multiple clutches live in the same location, forming a larger, broader gargoyle community called a clan. While the members of a specific gargoyle clutch are fixed, the clutches within a clan can shift and change over time. Usually, this occurs when a clan becomes too large for its home or territory, causing several clutches to splinter off and form a new clan.

Gargoyles are natural defenders and guardians who stand sentinel over their ward. Sometimes, a gargoyle has a ward they protect on their own, but it’s far more common that a clutch all work together to defend the same ward.

Usually, a gargoyle’s ward is their home, perhaps a structure like a grand castle or an ominous clocktower, a natural location such as a majestic mountain peak or a waterfall, or a neighborhood, such as an entire dockside district or a small town. Other times, a gargoyle’s ward is a site of significance, such as a church or temple, an ancient ring of standing stones, a magical portal to the realm of the fey, or a silent graveyard. Sometimes, it’s a group of people, a family bloodline, or a specific individual—not so much a community, but the people within it. Occasionally, it’s an item or object, such as a cursed blade, a magician’s spellbook, a divine relic, a family heirloom, or a dangerous invention.

Regardless of the form a ward takes, a gargoyle is bound to defend it and takes this duty very seriously, often putting their ward’s safety above their own personal safety or even that of their clutchmates. This binding isn’t magical or unbreakable—it’s a vow of protection uttered with solemnity by a gargoyle, a clutch, or a clan to which that gargoyle chooses to dedicate their life. It’s a responsibility upheld with tenacity, bravery, and honor. The act of protecting one’s ward is known as one’s “vigil.”

Sometimes, a ward is maintained temporarily—such as a gargoyle that vows to keep vigil over a child until they’re grown and will eventually have their term of service come to an end. But it’s far more common that a ward is maintained forever; that is, beyond even a single gargoyle’s lifetime, on through clutch after clutch after clutch—the Eternal Vigil.

Gargoyles speak responsibly—their word is their bond, and they’re careful not to lie, make promises they can’t uphold, or say anything they will later regret. They rarely hurl insults and tend toward boasting about themselves or acting menacing, rather than slandering others, when in a verbal altercation. A gargoyle maintains their vows throughout their life—and often beyond, picking up the unfinished vows and duties of their clutchmates and adopting them as their own as a sign of respect and remembrance. The one exception to their honesty is the fabrication of false tales and spreading rumors, which they consider tactical storytelling.

One legend of old is that gargoyles are creatures of shadow and darkness, which is true. They’re nocturnal, and though they have no problem functioning during the day, they have the advantage at night—they have excellent night vision, and it’s easy for them to hide or pose as a statue in areas of poor light.

Years of living with other gargoyles have taught most to be practical and tactical. They plan communally and work well in a group. They’re passionate and determined, brave and heroic—yet they often come off as stoic or stern. It’s not that gargoyles are serious, it’s that they’re still. They move with intention and purpose, wasting no motion nor expending energy unnecessarily. To fidget, twitch, or even sway is unnatural to a gargoyle—all things they must do, they do intentionally. Only gargoyles aiming to better blend in among other societies adopt such affectations. To a gargoyle, any moment of stillness is a moment of repose.

Because gargoyles dedicate so much of their time and lives to the defense of their ward, they hold personal interests dear to their hearts. Most gargoyles pick up a talent or hobby they excel at, which tends toward the frivolous. Additionally, they maintain a small personal collection of items and objects—usually portable and handheld—which they keep for the joy it brings them. Sometimes, this collection consists of mementos meant to trigger fond memories. More often, it’s simply a mixed collection of items that spark joy. Rarely is a gargoyle interested in a specific topic, and their collection reflects this entirely—perhaps a collection of tiny toy wagons, polished stones, whistles, or objects shaped like rabbits. Whatever the contents of a gargoyle’s collection, a gargoyle cherishes it throughout their lifetime.

Gargoyle Settlements

Gargoyles are communal and community-minded, with a strong focus on defense. They tend to construct large complexes that contain all aspects of a community within a single building, rather than a traditional settlement. These buildings are invariably built of stone in a prominent location, with numerous defensive measures incorporated, such as walls, trenches, portcullises, mazes, secret chambers, and other structures. Thus, a gargoyle settlement is often a castle, fortress, large tower, bunker, or other similar structure. Gargoyle settlements incorporate countless statues—all in the likeness of fearsome and terrifying gargoyles. Sometimes, when secrecy is required, they instead live in caverns and chambers built into the walls of a cliff or mountainside, hiding their homes and defenses inside the stone, and using height to their advantage.

Gargoyle Groups

Gargoyles don’t divide themselves into units or groupings beyond that of clutch and clan. Yet, socially, they have the most commonalities based upon the types of wards they maintain vigil over. These unofficial groupings are detailed below.

Bulwarks

A bulwark gargoyle is one whose ward is a specific site where the gargoyle doesn’t personally live. Usually, these are sites of cultural, magical, or religious importance or are sites of tactical significance, useful in defending a broader region. From lighthouses, graveyards, leylines, and planar bubbles to fortresses, watchtowers, and bridges—a bulwark’s ward is incredibly diverse, as are the interests driving them to form such vows and maintain such vigils. Bulwarks tend to make their vows out of a sense of duty and selflessness—the site they defend isn’t their own, nor does maintaining it help them personally. They’re interested in the greater good, the wider community, the bigger picture, and the forgotten or neglected past. Bulwarks are the most likely to adopt the wards of their clutchmates upon their passing and make new vows of their own, adding new responsibilities to their existing ones over and over again. More than most gargoyles, bulwarks are bound by duty and burdened by responsibility.

Claimants

A claimaint gargoyle is one whose ward is a material item or object. Often, their vigils serve one of three purposes: to protect an item of value or importance, to prevent others from claiming a dangerous or cursed item, or to safeguard a foul or cursed item until a definite method to destroy the item can be ascertained. Few gargoyles would admit openly to being a claimant, but that doesn’t mean that claimants are rare—they’re not. The first step to protecting an item is to ensure no one knows you have it in the first place. This simple truth makes claimants among the most deceptive of gargoyles. While they avoid outright lies, they are prone to simply omitting anything they’d rather keep secret. Some claimants are sedentary, building layers upon layers of defenses around their ward. Others remain on the move to keep their ward far from prying eyes and grasping hands. Regardless, claimants tend towards self-glorification, with those who protect an object of value or significance proud of what they possess and the honor of their vigil, and those who safeguard a dangerous item proud that the world is saved through their actions. A claimant’s ward is usually passed down through generations of clutches—a ward inherited. Occasionally, claimaints undertake grand quests—often to claim and add another item to their ward or to destroy a cursed item once thought indestructible. The result of such quests are usually riddled with hardship but deeply fulfilling—for those who survive the ordeal.

Clutchbound

A clutchbound gargoyle is one who is sworn to maintain vigil over their own clutch or clan. As this vigil is instinctive and effortless, it’s the most common ward among gargoyles—an oft-wordless vow made from a place of unconditional love. Clutchbound gargoyles don’t consider their vigil a burden nor a sacrifice—it’s as natural to them as breathing or stillness. The relaxed nature of their vigil often leaves a clutchbound with more free time, making them more likely to pick up hobbies and try new things. A clutchbound’s vigil is more fluid than most, as they’re bound to protect all members of their clutch but not to always be alongside every clutch member. So long as they’re actively protecting a member of their clutch, they’re contributing to the defense of their ward and maintaining their vigil. This guardianship means that members of a clutch can even temporarily leave the rest of the clutch for an extended time, only to return later. Leaving is most common in clans, when a clutch temporarily leaves for a purpose, such as a seasonal hunt or to deal with a threat, then returns to the rest of the clan later. Or in a clutch, when a few members leave to indulge the interests of their members, then return when that interest has been pursued—such as traveling to a larger town so the clutchmates can study at a magic academy or learn a trade. Clutchbound gargoyles have the least responsibilities and burdens, but also suffer the greatest heartbreak when they fail to protect their ward.

Clutchless

A clutchless gargoyle is one who never had a clutch. Usually, this is because their egg was stolen, lost, or failed to hatch on time, resulting in a gargoyle that hatches without any others of their kind. Most gargoyles consider being clutchless the saddest thing that could possibly occur to a gargoyle, for they never formed the most important bonds of their lives. Thus, gargoyles tend to consider clutchless poor unfortunates, someone pitiable, whom they go out of their way to welcome. Clutchless tend to think the opposite—never part of a clutch, they consider it no different from any other family or group of close companions. As most clutchless are jovial and make friends easily, they usually have friends and found family in abundance. This causes them to see the gargoyle’s obsession with the clutch as weird and close-minded. Clutchless tend to get along best with non-gargoyles, and travel far and wide. While they still have an instinctive need to guard and protect, they don’t usually do so with the same singular obsessiveness as most gargoyles; instead, they protect who and what they want to, as it suits them. They maintain a varied vigil rather than an eternal one.

Desolates

A desolate gargoyle is one that has lost their clutch. Usually, this is because a gargoyle is the sole remaining survivor of some great tragedy, and the desolate is truly that—alone and in deep mourning. Desolates can also include any gargoyle who is excommunicated from their clutch. Excommunication is extremely rare, for even a gargoyle despised by their clutch, or despises their clutch in turn, is still accepted and tolerated. To suffer excommunication, a gargoyle must do something truly heinous—perhaps the murder of several of their clutchmates or the wanton destruction of a rookery. Regardless, desolates tend to be stoic, somber, brooders. As time passes, they can become isolated or bitter. Many are obsessed with vengeance or are so deep in mourning that they become more statue than gargoyle.

Guardians

A guardian gargoyle is one whose ward is a living being. Often, this consists of everyone living in the same area as a gargoyle. For example, if a gargoyle lives in a large castle or a small town, they might vow to defend all the other residents of that castle, or the other citizens of that town, thus defending the people in a community, rather than the structures built around them. Sometimes, a gargoyle instead vows to maintain vigil over a specific group of people, such as the descendants of a particular bloodline, the members of a religious or magical order, or the nation’s ruling nobilities. Rarely, a gargoyle’s ward is a singular person—perhaps an orphan in need, a religious leader, a law enforcement officer, or a monarch. Usually, such wards are maintained for a limited time. Rarer still are those gargoyles who vow to protect a non-sapient living being, such as the animals of the forest they live. Regardless of the ward, guardians are friendly and sociable and are prone to acts of spontaneous heroics. They’re compassionate and charitable and care for people, even if they don’t know them. This caring nature also makes guardians the easiest for others to manipulate and take advantage of.

Nested

A nested gargoyle is one whose ward is their home. While this could be a specific structure or location, it could also be a much larger area, such as a neighborhood, town, or swath of territory. Regardless, nested gargoyles spend their lives defending their physical home. They tend to be territorial isolationists and don’t like visitors snooping around. If a nested shares their home with other non-gargoyles, which is common when a gargoyle considers an entire neighborhood, town, or region as their ward, these feelings tend to be less pronounced, and instead, they lean more toward paranoia. Regardless, nested are dug in and stuck in their ways and are always alert for brewing trouble or other disturbances. They’re the least likely gargoyles to travel beyond their home—though it’s not unheard of as long as another is maintaining the same vigil as many threats are better dealt with beyond one’s home. Nested can be considered rigid, even to other gargoyles, are slow to trust, and incredibly stubborn.

Pursuants

A pursuant gargoyle is a gargoyle who has lost their ward and is striving desperately to recover it somehow. While their situation isn’t necessarily their fault, they’re seen as the failures of the gargoyle world. Under their watch, a vigil was broken, a vow sundered, or a guardianship ineffective. Or perhaps their ward was lost—an item stolen or misplaced, a person kidnapped or lost, a structure conquered or destroyed, or a gargoyle driven from a site or region. Not all guardianships are successful, and gargoyles consider this loss a severe failure. Pursuants have felt the sting of loss and the shame of failure deep in their core and live only for redemption—to reclaim their ward and, with it, their honor. Pursuants tend to be serious and single-minded, utterly fixated on their objectives. They’re worriers and struggle with feelings of shame and inadequacy. Yet, to achieve their goals, they usually must put on a brave face and rally supporters. Usually, pursuants seek the help of their clutchmates or other gargoyles, but sometimes the aid of outsiders is also required.

Stonemasons: The Unsung Gargoyle Hero

Gargoyles frequently craft impressive structures out of stone—from the ubiquitous stone statue of a gargoyle to the magnificent castle, complete with curtain walls, crenelations, barbicans, and keeps. The creation of these items and edifices is a miracle in itself, and yet, gargoyles don’t place much reverence upon those who perform these tasks.

To a gargoyle, crafting a statue or a castle isn’t a great skill, it’s one of many necessary steps in the protection of their ward. Thus, most gargoyles learn to shape and build with stone, just as they learn to walk, talk, and snarl. Individual gargoyles build what is necessary, and when something big needs to be built, they all join together to see it through.

Beliefs

You can use the following tendencies to guide you in choosing an alignment if you’re playing with alignment in your game, or to assist with characterization for your gargoyle characters if not: The stereotypical gargoyle is lawful good. Their word is their bond and their honor. The breaking of vows and the abandonment of tradition is not their way—even if the gargoyle was the first to be betrayed by those with whom they kept faith. Gargoyles are slow to trust, and once their trust is broken, it can’t be won back. Nevertheless, even a loss of trust won’t stop a gargoyle from their duties—many would choose to protect someone they don’t trust—even someone who caused them pain or harm—if it serves to fulfill their duties.

Gargoyles tend to worship deities that understand the importance of kinship and bonds, who promote an emphasis on duty, honesty, honor, and vows, or who encourage altruism, heroism, and self-sacrifice. In the world of Alacar, this includes Tova the Father, Jata the Champion, and Wera the Reaper, among others. While gargoyles train as warriors and spellcasters and resort to battle when necessary, they much prefer a conflict avoided and a fight ended before it began. This makes it rare that a gargoyle worships a deity of battle. Clutchless are much more varied in their faith, instead worshiping deities who match their natural interests and inclinations.

Popular Edicts protect your ward, uphold your vows, embrace the night

Popular Anathemas abandon your ward, betray a clutchmate, intentionally lie

Names

Gargoyles don’t begin life with a name. Instead, for the first few years, they’re referred to by various descriptors, such as clutchmate, little one, red, loud, or babbler. In time, as they become an adult, a gargoyle selects their own name. Sometimes this name is a title, moniker, or descriptor. Sometimes they name themselves after a place they favor or protect. Sometimes they select a name solely because they like the sound of it, or the emotions it evokes, and sometimes they select a name that’s entirely unique.

Clans and clutches are typically named after a gargoyle’s home, city, or community. If a clan is ousted or moves, the clan is temporarily nameless while searching for a new home. When they find that new home, their clan name changes again, becoming reflective of their new home.

Sample Names

Avalain, Bailey, Bower, Bulwark, Castleheart, Crag, Darragh, Davona, Eldson, Firebrand, Golgath, Ironwing, Morag, One With Shadow, Renway, Rides The Wind, Rook, Silverthrush, Una, Weildra.

Clan Names

Castle Ulbroch, Clocktower, Dockside, Kingfisher Isle, Mount Yelvern, Nilbog, Violet Woodlands.

Gargoyle Heritages

Choose one of the following gargoyle heritages at 1st level. Gargoyles of all heritages (unless otherwise noted, such as clamberlimb and clutchswapped) can have all sorts of “monstrous” features—from wings, to tails, to fangs. These don’t grant any additional benefits except as defined by your heritage, but some feats might have a requirement based on your gargoyle features. For instance, if you’re a clamberlimb or clutchswapped gargoyle, you don’t have wings and can’t take a feat that requires wings; if you’re a firmwing gargoyle, you always have wings, and so you could take such a feat; and if you’re a gargoyle with another heritage, you can decide whether you have wings or not, which determines whether you can take such a feat.

Clamberlimb Gargoyle

Additionally, you can alter the coloration of your skin to match any appearance of nearby stone, enabling you to better blend in with your surroundings or imitate a statue. While adjacent to stone or rock (such as gravel, a boulder, a marble statue, a basalt gravemarker, or a stone wall) you can spend a single action to match your coloration to that rock. This grants you a +2 circumstance bonus to Stealth checks when near rocks of a similar composition or coloration. This coloration lasts until you next choose to spend an action to change it.

Sentinel of Stone

When roleplaying a gargoyle, put yourself in their perspective as a sentinel of stone, sworn to guard something or someone come what may. By holding fast to this duty, you can help forge your gargoyle’s broader identity: what would they sacrifice in order to uphold their oaths and keep their vigil? What else, if anything, is important enough to them that they would be willing to accept even a small risk to their ward on its behalf?

Ancestry Feats
