As Changeable as the Tides.name: 如潮汐般多变 As Changeable as the Tides
As Changeable as the Tides.text:
摘自艾瑟里娅·尼克斯的档案
如潮汐般变幻

一个人的情绪会受到许多事物影响：一天中的时间、周遭温度、久拖不愈的感冒、此人最近是否吃过一顿饱饭、他们是否刚与朋友或家人发生过情绪化的互动，诸如此类。当然，我们之中有许多人已经学会如何解读面部表情与肢体语言这些细微线索，来理解这些情绪并据此行事。嗯，大多数人如此。而其余的人则只能在尽力处理自身情绪的同时，尽可能在他人情感那危险的浅滩间航行。

多数人会认为，怪物的一项决定性特征是不理性。“你无法与怪物讲道理！”他们这样说，却没意识到他们自诩的理性，即便在最好的人身上也脆弱得像肥皂泡。一个倒霉的英雄或许会从情绪出发采取行动，结果不小心弊大于利。“怪物毫无理由地杀戮，因此必须被阻止！”他们高喊着，仿佛他们所谓的杀戮理由就真的有什么不同。为领土而战的战争，其合理性不比两只野兽为一片共同的猎场而角力更高。

这种伪善的一个明确例子，便是相当多的文明与狼人类的互动方式。他们常常相信，狼人类在满月到来之前都以社会中“正常”成员的面貌生活；而当满月引发无法控制的嗜血冲动浮现时，便会在本质上接管该个体的人格。诚然，有些狼人类确实是狡诈的杀手，并以自身的变形为乐；但这一比例实际上并不比犯罪世界中暴力犯的比例更显著。大多数狼人类只是想过自己的日子，而不想因为那不过是他们一部分的东西而被追猎。可以说，既然狼人类知道月亮何时可能让他们失控，并能据此提前规划，他们反倒比那些仅仅因为刚刚踢到脚趾就对邻居发作的人更“理性”。

我曾听过短语“如潮汐般变幻”被用来形容某个性情反复无常之人——不能被信任去承担重要任务。但潮汐却是世界上最可预测的现象之一。海面在岸边有规律地涨落，以至于水手与博物学者都依赖它，并对潮汐研究得足够透彻，知道它何时涌入、何时退去。月相亦是如此可靠，以至于有些文化据此设计了他们的历法系统。若能把所有将一个人从愉快变为愤怒的因素都如此轻易地绘制成图并加以规划，那该多好？若所有人都能像狼人类那样洞悉自身人格，那该多好！

— 艾瑟里娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物的倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
As Changeable as the Tides

A person’s mood can be affected by many things: the time of day, the ambient temperature, a lingering cold, whether or not the person has recently eaten a full meal, if they’ve recently had an emotional interaction with a friend or family member, and so on. Of course, many of us have worked out how to interpret the subtle clues of facial expressions and body language to understand these moods and act accordingly. Well, most of us. And the rest of us deal with our emotions while navigating the dangerous shoals of other people’s feelings as best we can.

Most would consider one of the defining characteristics of a monster to be irrationality. “You can’t reason with a monster!” they say, not realizing that their vaunted rationality is as flimsy as a soap bubble in even the best of people. A hero having a bad day might choose to act from an emotional place and accidentally do more harm than good. “A monster kills without reason, and so must be stopped!” they shout as if their so-called reasons for killing are somehow different. Wars fought over territories make as much sense as two wild animals locking horns over a shared hunting ground.

One concrete example of this hypocrisy is how quite a few civilizations interact with werecreatures. They often believe that a werecreature appears as a “normal” member of society until the full moon causes an uncontrollable bloodlust to surface and essentially takes over that individual’s personality. While it’s true that some werecreatures are devious killers who revel in their transformation, this percentage is actually no more significant than the percentage of violent offenders in the criminal world. Most werecreatures simply want to live their lives without being hunted for what is merely a part of them. It could be argued that since a werecreature knows when the moon might cause them to lose control and can plan accordingly, they are more “rational” than those who lash out at their neighbor because they stubbed their toe only a moment ago.

I’ve heard the phrase “as changeable as the tides” used to mean someone with a mercurial disposition—one may not be trusted with an important task. But the tides are one of the most predictable phenomena in the world. The sea rises and falls on the shore so regularly that sailors and naturalists rely on it and have studied the tides enough to know when they come in and go out. The same is true of the moon’s phases, which are so reliable that some cultures have designed their calendar systems based on them. Would all factors that turn a person from pleasant to angry be so easily charted and planned for? Would all people gain the same insight into their personalities as werecreatures possess!

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Werecreature Ancestry.name: 兽化怪族裔 Werecreature Ancestry
Werecreature Ancestry.text:
A werecreature’s natural appearance is that of a hybrid between a humanoid and a specific, usually carnivorous, animal. Werecreatures can shift between that form, a fully humanoid form, and an entirely bestial form at will, though many prefer to remain in their humanoid shape to move undetected among other ancestries. However, when a full moon rises overhead, some werecreatures are compelled to transform into their hybrid forms and carry out bloodthirsty rampages. This behavior had led to other ancestries fearing and mistrusting werecreatures.

Though some believe them to be the victims of a terrible curse, werecreatures born with their abilities aren’t much different than druids and other ancestries who have the ability to alter their shapes. Some werecreatures turn away from this natural ability and never fully embrace it, eschewing their full animal forms in order to focus on qualities that help them blend in with other humanoids. Most of those who travel this path have heard stories of the mistreatment of their werecreature ancestors or currently reside in an area where their persecution is ongoing. Others might pride themselves on blending into society more easily than their wilder cousins.

Werecreatures who allow themselves more freedom (or aren’t as likely to be looked down upon by their neighbors) revel in their animalistic sides and consider what others might call the curse of the werecreature a gift instead. They think nothing of belting out a howl, snort, or growl in mixed company. They might let their hair and nails grow long and tend not to wash in the days surrounding the full moon. On those nights, they run through the wilderness with abandon and hunt to their hearts’ (and bellies’) content. The average villager might consider this attitude evil or hedonistic, but those werecreatures don’t care.

If you want to play a character who has a deep connection to a primal fury—whether they embrace it or fight against it—and has the ability to take on a bestial form at will, you should play a werecreature.

You Might...

  • Find yourself more at home among wild beasts than with the trappings of civilization.
  • Feel a temptation to release your hybrid form on the nights of the full moon.
  • Hide your true nature for fear of being persecuted.

Others Probably...

  • Might not even know about your bestial heritage.
  • Consider you a dangerous animal that must be put down before passing your curse onto others.
  • Appreciate your ability to hunt like a predator.

Physical Description

True werecreatures have varied appearances, depending on both their humanoid form and heritage. When they pose as a humanoid ancestry, they are practically indistinguishable from a typical member of that ancestry. A dwarven wereboar will likely appear short and stocky, and an elven weretiger will probably sport the pointed ears associated with elves. As true werecreatures grow older or spend long hours in their hybrid forms, traces of their nature begin to seep into the appearance of their humanoid forms. That dwarven wereboar’s body hair grows more bristly, and their nose begins to turn slightly upward. That elven weretiger’s canines grow a bit more pronounced and their eyes reflect more light in the dark, giving them the impression of glowing. Most werecreatures become adept at hiding these subtle changes with a touch of makeup, clever use of attire, or minor illusions.

Many werecreatures consider their hybrid form to be their true appearance, no matter how much time they spend in their humanoid form. These shapes combine humanoid features and animal aspects related to their heritage. While werecreature hybrid forms can sometimes be mistaken for other ancestries—wererats and weretigers can occasionally be confused for ratfolk and catfolk—true werecreatures usually have a wilder and untamed mien. A true werecreature’s hybrid form also reflects features of similar animals found in the regions where the werecreature was born. Through some primal connection to nature filtered through their curse, this can lead to a werecreature looking distinctively different from their parents if their distant ancestors were originally from a different climate. Werecreatures can also take on animal forms, but like druids, who possess similar magic, these forms are difficult to distinguish from natural fauna.

Werebear hybrid forms have fur that ranges from black to tawny brown in temperate regions, with some werebears in colder climes sporting white and gray fur. The length of their fur also varies by climate, with longer fur helping the werebear keep warm while trudging through snow and sleet. Werebear snouts hold teeth made for tearing flesh, and their claws allow them to tear through bark and climb trees.

Similarly, wereboar hybrid forms have varying lengths of fur, with many having a “mane” of longer hair that runs down their backs. Their fur can be brown, black, or dark gray, and a few wereboars display a mottled pattern of darker spots along their flanks. Most wereboars have a single pair of tusks, but some grow a small secondary pair behind the first, which helps them skewer their prey better. Unlike other werecreatures, wereboars have hoofed feet that leave distinctive tracks.

The hybrid forms of werecrocodiles have green, brown, or dark gray scales. Their toothy snouts are narrow and elongated, and their eyes are as black as a starless night. The webbing between a werecrocodile’s fingers and toes helps them cut through the water swiftly. While most werecrocodiles have long reptilian tails, not many can use them to attack their prey.

Beyond its gray, brown, or black fur, a wererat’s hybrid form is typified by hairless feet, hands, tail, and snout. These areas also vary in skin color, from pink to beige. There is a higher percentage of albinism in wererats than in other werecreatures, leading to white fur and red eyes. Urban wererats try to keep their fur as clean as possible to stave off any accusations of it bearing disease-ridden fleas.

Weretiger’s fur features its feline kin’s familiar black or dark brown stripes across a body of orange, often with a white belly and muzzle. These stripes help a weretiger in their hybrid form move concealed through the shadowy brush until they are ready to pounce with their powerful legs. Weretigers in snowier regions might have completely white fur with only the faintest of dark gray stripes. Like many werecreatures, weretigers have tails in their hybrid forms, which they use for balance. They also have long whiskers and expressive cat-like eyes.

The hybrid forms of werewolves are among the most varied due to the many different wolf species found throughout the world. Their fur can be white, brown, gray, black, or even rust-colored. The fur’s coloration might be uniform across the body or darken or lighten around the snout, along the back, or on the limbs. A werewolf’s fur grows shaggier in colder seasons and sheds in warmer seasons, though this change seems to occur during the transformation between hybrid form and humanoid form. Werewolf tails are generally bushier than those of other werecreatures.

Werecreature Adventurers

True werecreatures have any number of reasons to join a party of adventurers. They might be on the run, accused of brutal crimes they didn’t commit. They might be exiled from their werecreature family associated with the fury wilding for refusing to partake in bloody sacrifices of innocent townsfolk. They might simply be looking for a place to fit in. Born with their double-edged curse and gift, a werecreature adventurer might otherwise be searching for a way to embrace their bestial nature. Due to their physical prowess, werecreatures often take on martial roles in a party. Barbarians, fighters, monks, and rangers are not uncommon career paths. Many wererats favor the way of the rogue, and some werebears devote themselves to being a deity’s champion. Though not impossible, werecreatures become spellcasters less often, especially classes like wizard involving extensive scholarship.

Society

As an ancestry, true werecreatures don’t have a single, cohesive society. Rather, the outlooks of individual werecreatures are shaped by their upbringing. Some choose to remain hidden among other populations, never revealing their true identities unless forced to do so by circumstances. They tend to live within the local laws, always seeking the safest outlets for their more bestial tendencies. Some isolate themselves from the larger world (and other werecreatures) out of a sense of self-sacrifice, fearing that they might hurt innocents during the nights of the full moon. Still, others form communities of similar true werecreatures and either use their natural talents to take what they want from surrounding areas or live simply in concordance with nature. These different points of view are represented in groupings called wildings. Apart from family members and fellow members of the same wilding, most werecreatures have difficulty forming trusting relationships, especially with members of other ancestries.

Heritages

While there are as many potential forms of werecreature as there are predators in the animal world, several heritages of werecreature are more prominent than the rest. Six of these heritages of true werecreature are described in more detail below.

Werebear

Werebears tend to live solitary lives in their native wilderness, serving as guardians of sacred sites or protectors of rare and endangered species. Many true werebears consider the ferocity they can tap into not as a well of uncontrolled anger but as more of a source of righteousness. Once a werebear finds a cause to champion, they often devote themselves entirely to it, sometimes even forsaking family (unless that family also dedicates their efforts to the same cause). A werebear stoked to anger in defense of their cause is a sight to behold, as their hybrid form is burly and imposing, and their claws can easily tear through armor. Luckily, most werebears are more reactive than active and try not to seek out violence. However, a werebear might find it necessary to take a fight to a foe instead of suffering repeated incursions into their territory. At all other times, werebears can be reasonably sedate, preferring to forage for berries and fish in between long periods of rest. Like their ursine kin, a werebear can hibernate through unfavorable seasons, but if disturbed, they vent their wrath on anyone unlucky enough to be at hand.

Werebears frequently reside in cold to temperate forests and mountainous areas that are replete with caves.

Wereboar

It may be a bit of a joke to refer to wereboars as “pig-headed,” but the description is often apt. They can be quite stubborn when they set their minds to a course of action and might react violently to those who suggest that what they are doing is foolhardy or dangerous. Because of a wereboar’s short temper, those of a calmer nature in the small rural communities where they live tend to avoid or sometimes even ostracize them. However, after someone stands up to a wereboar’s obstinacy, they often gain the wereboar’s respect and can talk their new friend out of trying something too reckless. While wereboars enjoy fighting and drinking, many of them are actually at their happiest when tending to their farms or raising livestock. Their tastes in food and fashion tend to be simple and rustic. Many are vegetarians, with roots, mushrooms, and berries forming the mainstay of their diets. Those who do eat meat avoid pork products, roasting fowl, or fish with ample sides of vegetables. Wereboars appreciate the work that goes into growing or butchering one’s food and are quick to lecture those who take their meals for granted.

Wereboars prefer to dwell on the fertile plains and foothills of most climates, though some live along rivers and seasides.

Werecrocodile

One of the more unusual aspects of the iconic true werecreature heritage is that werecrocodiles differ from their cousins because their hybrid forms are more reptilian than mammalian. This has led to the occasional debate on whether werecrocodiles are part of the same ancestry as other werecreatures. The physical changes that werecrocodiles undergo and their allergy to silver are the same as those of wererats, werewolves, and the like. This seems definitive enough proof for most, but some still assert that werecrocodiles should be classified differently; some scholars prefer to use the term “therianthrope” for werecreatures, but that name implies they are all mammals.

For their part, this academic deliberation is utterly irrelevant to werecrocodiles, who are among the most violent of true werecreatures and most likely to be members of the fury wilding. Though they tend to get along with one another most of the time, werecrocodiles are incredibly territorial. Once they claim an area as their own, they use aggressive displays of dominance to ward off others, whether they are werecrocodiles or other creatures. When that fails, a werecrocodile is quick to battle, using their powerful jaws to tear enemies to pieces. Some bands of werecrocodiles take to piracy of the waterways they frequent, amassing small hoards of stolen goods before bringing them to nearby towns disguised in their humanoid forms. After selling these items, the werecrocodile criminals spend their ill-gotten gains on evenings of debauchery.

Werecrocodiles can usually be found near waterways in warm climates, basking on the banks of rivers until they sense prey approaching.

Werecreature Packs

Some true werecreatures are born into large werecreature families or seek out others of their kind for strength or protection. Such groups can be referred to as “packs,” though they need not consist of werecreatures of the same heritage or remain together at all times. Many packs comprise small encampments or villages of nothing but werecreatures, living simple lives. Some such communities remain in their humanoid forms for safety, except during the full moon. Other packs are similar to guilds or communities within larger settlements, with true werecreatures of varying professions watching out for one another to avoid persecution. Rarely does a pack of furies run wild through the wildnerness in their hybrid forms, terrorizing the innocent and ruling the night.

Wererat

Though most natural scholars believe that werewolves are the most populous true werecreatures, others argue that wily wererats hold that title in secret, as they can blend in with or live beneath the populations of large cities to avoid notice. Some wererats also try to pass themselves off as ratfolk, though the discerning eye can spot the differences between the rodent-like forms of ysoki and hybrid wererats. For starters, wererats tend to be taller and their legs usually have less fur. The offense ratfolk take from any comparisons to wererats stems from the underhanded and devious acts perpetrated by some groups of wererat furies and evangelists. Many wererats enjoy skulking in the shadows when not passing themselves off as ordinary citizens. Some take on the mantle of thief or assassin, and the most ambitious of those quickly rise through the ranks of their respective guilds… sometimes by stabbing their fellow guild members in the back, either figuratively or literally. True wererats who wish to defy this stereotype work to better the community in which they live, sometimes even fighting against organized crime or by engaging in vermin control in the city’s sewers. The irony of this career choice is not lost on them.

Wererats usually take up residence in major metropolitan areas, either above or below ground.

Weretiger

Many weretigers focus on acquiring strength, as they consider themselves the apex predators of their environment, whether in the wilderness or within a social hierarchy. Weretigers are fast and ferocious when on a hunt, quickly running down their prey and granting them quick deaths. Similarly, when confronted with an intellectual debate or a high-society entanglement, weretigers waste no time delivering an astute line of reasoning or cutting remark that leaves their opponents in metaphorical tatters. So convinced are they of their superiority that some weretigers live openly and constantly in their hybrid forms, considering it an easy way to show their status. Other times, urban weretigers keep their true ancestry a secret to lull their foes into false senses of security; such weretigers are masters of espionage, with a public-facing job such as a shopkeeper or bartender and a secret career selling the information they gather. In any case, true weretigers who live in cities sometimes struggle to keep their natural aggression in check and often find clandestine outlets for their more feline urges, usually far outside of the settlement’s borders.

Weretigers frequent hot and humid jungles, as well as urban areas in and close to that particular terrain.

Werewolf

When most people think about werecreatures, they picture an afflicted werewolf: an individual stricken with a terrible curse that causes them to undertake bloody rampages in the light of a full moon. They picture a werewolf’s lupine snout, clawed hands, furry haunches, and slavering jaws. And they attribute an insatiable bloodlust to such a figure, similar to that of a starving wolf. While some werewolves fit that gruesome stereotype, so many more are content to live peacefully with their neighbors. However, even the more sociable werewolves must usually keep their true ancestry to themselves for fear that unwarranted vigilante justice will be visited upon them and their families. Werewolves of any temperament might choose to live alone or with a family of like-minded folk. Due to their lupine tendencies, werewolves who form a pack quickly select a leader among their eldest or strongest. Werewolf leaders, often called alphas, hold their positions until they are otherwise proven not fit to serve (usually after making a series of drastic mistakes) or voluntarily relinquish their power to another member of the pack. Rarely, when a pack grows too large, a small group of younger werewolves splits off to form their own pack and seek their fortune elsewhere.

Werewolves can be found just about anywhere, from crowded metropolises to remote forests far from civilization.

Wildings

In addition to heritages, families, and packs, werecreatures organize themselves philosophically into wildings based on their feelings toward their double-edged gift and curse. Five of the most common wildings are animal kin, assimilators, evangelists, furies, and gifted.

Animal Kin

Most true werecreatures who have a preference for one of their three forms often consider their hybrid form or their humanoid form to be their “true form,” but to animal kin, it’s their animal form that speaks truest to their soul.

Animal kin often get along better with animals of the kind associated with their heritage than they do with humanoids, and they frequently become animal druids, beastmasters, or other occupations that allow them to get deeply in touch with animals. Humanoids are confusing and hide behind veils of deception, while animals are honest with their desires. To an animal kin, life is simpler in their animal form. When not forming groups with animals of their heritage’s associated type, animal kin werecreatures work best with other creatures who understand their philosophy and don’t ostracize them for it. They can be a great asset to an adventuring party who can accept them for who they are.

Assimilators

Where animal kin are tied to their animal side, assimilators have a strong connection to their humanoid side. Whether they truly identify with their humanoid side deep in their heart or came to be an assimilator out of practicality in order to fit in, assimilators generally spend as much time as possible in their humanoid form and hide the fact that they’re a werecreature from all but their closest confidantes.

Assimilators often go out of their way to appear as “normal” humanoids so that others won’t suspect they’re werecreatures, which means that an investigator on a witch hunt for assimilator werecreatures might do well to start with the most suspiciously ordinary-seeming members of the community. When assimilators join adventuring parties, it often takes a while for them to choose to open up to their fellow adventurers about their true nature. Eventually, either their traveling companions will fully earn their trust, or an emergency will force the assimilator to deploy their hidden abilities. At which point, if their party members accept them, the assimilator is likely to forge an even closer bond. And if not, there are often other adventuring opportunities for an assimilator.

Evangelists

Evangelists, along with gifted, believe that being a werecreature is a wondrous gift rather than a terrible curse. However, unlike gifted, evangelists believe that this gift should be generously shared with as many other people as possible. To most evangelists, even those who don’t see how wonderful the gift of the werecreature is now will surely understand once they’ve experienced it for themselves!

Evangelists seek out anyone with the appropriate spiritual compatibility to share their gift and then either make them an offer or do so unilaterally. As a result, most afflicted werecreatures come from intentional conversions by evangelists (with many of the rest coming from survivors left by furies accidentally). Evangelists who know how to respect boundaries and autonomy can sometimes get along with groups of non-werecreatures. They’ll still continue to expound upon the glory and wonder of being a werecreature, attempting to convince their companions to change their minds. But at least they won’t convert their humanoid allies into werecreatures by force.

Wolfsbane

Wolfsbane is a deadly ingested poison, but it’s said to cure those afflicted with the curse of the werecreature if they survive the damage it causes to their body. Of course, such a remedy is ineffective for true werecreatures, as their gift and curse is integral to their being. As a result, most true werecreatures avoid wolfsbane with the same fervor that they shun silver. It is, after all, a potent toxin.

However, some foolhardy werecreatures have taken to tempering doses of wolfsbane with large amounts of soporific mushrooms and other chemicals to create a powerful drug known as “wolfspeace,” which induces a death-like slumber. They use this concoction during the nights of full moons to sedate themselves, keeping them inactive during their most aggressive state.

Furies

To furies, the true essence of being a werecreature is the wild and reckless fury of the full moon. Everything else is artifice; the full moon’s light simply reveals the inner truth that members of other wildings might attempt to hide on other nights. But to a fury, every night is like a full moon, and the exultant euphoria of the frenzied hunt never ends.

Furies are one of the main reasons other werecreatures need to hide their true nature, with evangelists, who wish to spread their blessing, being the other. After all, it usually only takes one fury going on a bloody rampage on a full moon’s night before villagers become terrified of every werecreature they see. For their part, furies aren’t all serial murderers, and a fair number of them do understand restraint, at least a little bit. These furies unleash their beasts at all times, but they turn their hunt toward worthy prey rather than helpless victims. Adventuring parties willing to overlook an incredibly loose cannon can’t ask for a more enthusiastic combatant than a fury. And once a fury sees them as fellow pack members working together on a hunt, they’ll be loyal for life.

Gifted

To gifted, the term “curse of the werecreature” is prejudicial, and they prefer it to be called the “gift of the werecreature.” While evangelists often share this nomenclature for their werecreature nature, the difference between the two wildings is that gifted believe that this gift makes them special, and so only the chosen few deserve it. While evangelists might stoop to sharing the gift with even the unwilling, gifted generally refuse, even if the prospective afflicted werecreature begs them for it.

Gifted who are willing to entertain these supplicants often ask them to complete multiple challenging trials to prove themselves, and only then will they receive the gift, along with the gifted werecreature’s blessing. Gifted who do choose to pass the gift on often feel a deep connection to those who complete their challenges, as well as a sense of responsibility that this new werecreature they inducted uses their powers responsibly. Gifted, more so than any other wilding, are likely to stay in their hybrid form openly most of the time, as they take it as a point of pride to show off their gift. While evangelists also find great beauty and strength in their hybrid forms, they’ve learned that it can be easier to convince people if they start off in a humanoid form, so they use whatever form is best to reach their goal. Many gifted believe that with great power comes great responsibility, so they are likely to join adventurers on a worthy quest in order to show off the power of their gift.

An Ancient Curse

The so-called “curse of the werecreature” has many names across countless societies, and it’s sometimes been called by names that don’t make very much sense in retrospect. Some early scholars named the condition “lycanthropy,” derived from the roots lykos, meaning “wolf,” and anthropos, meaning “man,” after they first studied a werewolf, even though werewolves are far from the only type of werecreature. It’s both a disease and a curse that werecreatures can spread through their bite, but as an ancestry, true werecreatures are considered carriers more than they are the afflicted. The transformation caused by the light of the full moon isn’t unavoidable for some true werecreatures, though even those individuals feel the pull of their hybrid form on such nights, like an itch that begs to be scratched. However, no one can pinpoint the origin of this curse for certain. There are many theories, and all of them speak of events that occurred in the mists of time, some calling them the world’s first curse.

The most commonly told origin story is that a bestial demon lord originated the curse and then spread it to the mortal world through their minions. Attacks from terrible, twisted animalistic demons occurred all over, seeding the different strains of the disease across a multitude of areas in the distant past. The afflicted transmitted the disease to other mortals until one day, true werecreatures were born among the populaces. No demon lord has taken credit for this feat, which casts significant doubt on the tale’s veracity. Demon lords are usually exceptionally proud and shameless. Proponents of the story believe that the originating demon lord must have a good reason for hiding their involvement or that they might have been slain in the meantime by a jealous rival.

Another tale involves a circle of druids who were being driven deeper and deeper into the wilderness by the encroachment of the first cities. In a moonlit ritual, they invited the spirits of the local predators to inhabit their bodies, granting them the strength and ferocity to fight back. These proto-werecreatures tore the villagers, who were once peaceful neighbors, limb from limb. When the druids reawakened to their senses, they regretted what they had done and tried to revoke their incantation. However, their pleas for mercy were met with the indifference of nature. The animal spirits had tasted the blood of those who would hunt them and destroy their homes, and that taste left them with an insatiable desire for more. And so, as the story goes, on the nights of the full moon, these enraged spirits find those they can possess and continue to wreak their vengeance.

One lesser-known theory popular among scholars of esoteric lore lays the blame for the curse at the feet of a powerful fey, possibly a sidhe lord, who was visiting the mortal realm in the earliest days of mortal civilization. The fey was exploring our world in the guise of a harmless creature—some tales say it was a rabbit, other stories mention a songbird—when they were wounded by a mortal hunter. The mortal’s ancestry varies in the telling, though usually the ancestry matches the storyteller’s audience, and in some variations, the hunter is the ruler of a kingdom who was hunting for sport rather than a professional hunter plying their trade. Before the hunter could finish off their prey, the fey reverted to their true form—a pale being that glowed with the full moon’s light—and placed a curse upon the hunter that altered their outer appearance to match their “inner beast.” This curse has lasted for millennia and spread beyond its original victim, such is the potent magic of the faerie realm. Other variations of this tale replace the fey with a spirit or deity visiting the mortal world.

All of these different stories share themes of people of various ancestries giving into their wilder sides, whether on purpose or not. And many of them frame werecreatures as figures to be both feared and pitied. Most true werecreatures find all of these tales distasteful and would much prefer to be treated as an ancestry like any other, without the need for some tragic, angsty origin story.

Werecreature Archetypes

Werecreatures, particularly those of the animal kin wilding, often choose archetypes like beastmaster that connect them to animals, especially the type of animal matching their heritage. Werecreatures who abandon themselves to the impulses of their gift, particularly those of the fury wilding, might take any available archetype meant for afflicted werecreatures, doubling down on their abilities in exchange for giving up some of their control.

The Meaning Of Silver

One major facet that all true werecreatures have in common is their allergy to silver. The very touch of this precious metal sickens them and wounds made by weapons made of silver smoke and bubble, causing intense pain. Some werecreatures train their bodies vigorously to overcome this allergy, either by spending hours touching uncovered silver or by investing in complex alchemical tinctures. Eventually, they overcome this aversion to better hide their true nature or to eliminate a weakness that their enemies could use to their advantage. But even werecreatures don’t fully understand why this allergy is tied to their bestial nature.

Like the origins of the curse itself, there are many theories about its connection to silver. The most widely accepted, though, is almost poetic. Silver is often associated with the moon due to their similarity in color. Moonlight can be described as “silvery,” and deities related to the moon often hold the color silver as sacred. While werecreatures tend to be more dangerous under the light of the full moon, this is also when they have the weakest control of their bestial nature. Afflicted werecreatures tend to lose any semblance of compassion and reason, and even true werecreatures who eschew the fury wilding feel a greater desire to frenzy than on a normal night. Through its primal nature, moonlight speaks directly to a werecreature’s body. As silver and the moon are metaphorically linked, the metal has a thaumaturgic connection to moonlight solidified, and so silver’s touch causes a werecreature’s body to rebel against itself on a fundamental level. The transformation that a full moon causes to a werecreature’s form is reflected and amplified onto each section of a werecreature’s body that is in contact with silver or bears wounds made by silver weapons. The werecreature’s skin rages in the same way that the moon provokes in the mind. This, understandably, causes a werecreature pain, as well as nausea with repeated, long-term exposure.

Though the mere sight of silver doesn’t cause the same reaction in a werecreature as a mirror or religious symbol might engender certain undead creatures, a werecreature still might flinch a bit upon noticing a foe brandishing a silver weapon. This type of reaction is typically due to painful memories of past encounters with silver, rather than any sort of physical response. Such a werecreature tends to either avoid the silver-wielding enemy at all costs or ferociously attack that person to the exclusion of all others, hoping to rip the hated metal from their hands before it can cause too much damage.

Beliefs

If you’re using alignment in your game, true werecreatures can have any alignment, depending on their personal outlooks. Some werewolves tend to be chaotic due to their connection to the untamed wilds of nature. Werebears and wererats, on the other hand, generally follow a code, whether that be the laws of a settlement or their own personal values. Even so, many wererats enjoy bending those laws to their favor. Wereboars, werecrocodiles, and weretigers can be even more selfish, preferring to do what is best for their own interests without caring for others’ principles. Many members of the public assume that werecreatures are evil, likely due to the sensational folktales that portray them as murderous hunters of non-werecreatures. While there is a grain of truth behind the origin of many of these stories, they represent only a fraction of true werecreatures’ motivations, and a werecreature is just as likely to be a pillar of their community. Religious werecreatures are devoted to nature deities and those associated with the moon. On Alacar, this includes the Eld as well as any of the elemental or seasonal Siblings. Some werecreatures of the fury wilding who believe that a demon lord granted them their gift choose to worship the demon lord they assume to be the originator, and those of the gifted wilding might worship animal spirits, fey, or even the gift itself.

Popular Edicts get in touch with your inner beast, respect the phases of the moon, trust your pack and let them trust you in return

Popular Anathemas fail to be true to your inner self, betray a packmate, fight other werecreatures with silver

Natural vs. Afflicted Werecreatures

To those who don’t understand the difference, a werecreature is just a werecreature, but there’s a fundamental difference between a creature of another ancestry afflicted by the curse of the werecreature through a werecreature’s bite and a true or “natural” werecreature born as a member of the werecreature ancestry. To a true werecreature, this is their true nature and fundamental state of being. For an afflicted werecreature, the transformation is an incursion into their life force, injecting a foreign bestial aspect into their mind, body, and spirit. As a result of these differences, true werecreatures tend to have better control over themselves, even on a full moon (though that control usually isn’t perfect). A true werecreature’s nature, whether one believes it a gift or a curse, is part of who they are, not a temporary condition that can be cured. For an afflicted werecreature, being a werecreature would work mechanically more like an archetype a character gains later in their career (similar to the ghoul or lich archetypes), rather than an ancestry.

Names

Werecreatures tend to take names that are common for their humanoid form in order to blend in better within their communities. They might even scour the gravestones of nearby cemeteries for inspiration, and careless werecreatures have accidentally given themselves the full names of deceased beloved relatives of the local townsfolk. If presented with this oddity, the werecreature usually tries their best to play it off as a coincidence in the moment, and then typically abandons that area shortly afterward due to fear of the response after raising the locals’ suspicions. Those who live in werecreature communities take names that are evocative of their physical features or their temperaments. Often, these names are passed down over several generations to honor the family’s ancestors.

Sample Names

Bitterpaw, Bounder, Fangrin, Firestripe, Growlmoon, Redpelt, Shadowlurk, Snapjaw, Stormclaw, Whitemane.

Werecreature Heritages

Natural werecreatures come in many forms relating to all manner of beasts. Choose one of the following werecreature heritages at 1st level. If you choose a versatile heritage, you can still choose an animal to determine the appearance of your hybrid and animal form, but you don’t gain any of the heritage abilities for that heritage, and you don’t qualify for feats with that heritage as a prerequisite.

Tracking Lunar Phases

With a werecreature, it can be useful to track the phase of the moon to determine when the full moon will occur. To save tracking, the GM can also roll randomly to see when it will be.

Ancestry Feats

The following feats are available to werecreature characters. Ancestry feats with a special line that increases their frequency under the light of the full moon allow you to use the new frequency only under the light of the full moon (not indoors or underground where the moon isn’t visible, unless a window or a shaft allows the moonlight to shine). The new frequency applies immediately under the full moon, and this might or might not allow you to use the feat again right away. For example, if you have the Frenzy of the Moon feat once per day and you used it 30 minutes before the full moon rose, you could use it again after another 30 minutes, as long as you were under the light of the full moon. On the other hand, if you used it an hour or more before the full moon rose, you could use it again right away.

The Sins of the Parent.name: 父辈之罪 The Sins of the Parent
The Sins of the Parent.text:
来自艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯的档案
父母之罪

思考这一道德困境：当神祇把凡人看得如同蚂蚁般微不足道，并以如此不成比例的方式表达祂们的不满时，我们又怎能将神祇视为公正？那些不佯装自己可被理解或可被共情的神祇，从某种意义上说，反而更为诚实。祂们是我们无法理解的、神秘莫测的存在。因此，尽管拉弥亚的命运或许会令那些对神祇抱持温馨或安慰式想象的人震惊与痛心，但对那些更深入探究神祇动作的人而言，这本不该令人意外。

让我明确无误地说：若我们接受关于拉弥亚最可信的起源传说之一，那么第一位拉弥亚——那位逃避对其神祇职责的女祭司——无疑有错。至于以将她变形为拉弥亚并在其心智中灌注难以言说的恶意的方式来诅咒她，从而进一步将她推向施虐与堕落，是否在道德上正当，这是我立场坚定的问题。然而，我也承认，理性的哲学家对此可能各执一词。

就我个人而言，我认为这一动作不仅在道德上令人发指——因为它可能影响第一位拉弥亚以及她可能捕食的无辜受害者——也同样带有对半人马以及其他具有相似身体形态的生物的歧视。那种杀戮冲动无疑是一种诅咒与惩罚，但为何牛马式的躯体形态会被视作惩罚，除非有人也认为半人马同样受了诅咒？那个神祇（其身份仍有争论）怎会仅因其不同，就对如此美观的形态抱持这般轻蔑？

话虽如此，我承认反方观点——尤其是在第一位拉弥亚的案例中——确有其道理。加入神职体系，本质上是一种神祇与其牧师之间的契约，使神祇拥有相当大的空间去奖赏、惩戒并向追随者施加义务。在该起源传说中，人们普遍认为第一位拉弥亚（其名或许就只是“拉弥亚”）违背了这项神圣誓约。她犯下过错，是一种傲慢之举，将自己置于其神祇之上并背叛了神祇的信任。我不认为有人会否认：这是亵渎之举，理应遭受某种形式的惩罚——哪怕是极端的惩罚。

我真正划下底线之处，在于她子宫中尚未出生的孩子们——他们毫无过错，却同样受到了诅咒。父母之罪不属于孩子，而这些无辜者，如同第一位拉弥亚的受害者一样，与她的过犯毫无关联。尽管如此，随着诅咒持续侵蚀他们、驱使他们的心智走向恶毒的念头，他们仍被迫付出价格。

神祇是残酷的。神祇是不公的。但仍有希望！即便在最悲惨的时刻，我们也能找到最大的希望。确有拉弥亚反抗诅咒及其冲动，过着自己的生活，证明神祇无法夺走她们的命运，也无法把她们变成她们拒绝成为的模样。每一位这样的拉弥亚都不尽相同。仅举数例：有的拥抱拉弥亚形态的美与身份，而有的连这一点也拒绝，将其视作自己希望克服的诅咒的一部分；有的憎恨并蔑视那位她们认为不公惩罚其先祖的神祇，而极少数则希望信仰与忏悔能改变那位神祇的心意。也许有一天，无论携手与否，她们将解除诅咒并移除神祇的强迫，使所有拉弥亚真正获得自由！

— 艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物的倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
The Sins of the Parent

Consider the moral quandary: how can we perceive the gods as just when they view mortals as insignificant as ants and express their displeasure in such disproportionate ways? Those deities who don’t feign knowability or relatability are, in a sense, more honest. They are enigmatic beings beyond our comprehension. So, while the fate of lamias may shock and dismay those who harbor cozy or comforting notions about the gods, it should come as no surprise to those who delve deeper into their actions.

Let me be unequivocal: if we are to accept one of the most plausible origin stories for lamias, then the first lamia, the priestess who shirked her duty to her deity, was undeniably at fault. Whether it was morally justifiable to curse her by metamorphosing her into a lamia and infusing her mind with unspoken malevolence, thereby pushing her further into sadism and depravity, is a matter on which I hold a firm stance. However, I acknowledge that reasonable philosophers could hold differing views.

Personally, I find this action not only morally reprehensible due to its potential impact on the first lamia and the innocent victims she might prey upon, but also discriminatory towards centaurs and other creatures with similar body shapes. The murderous impulses were undoubtedly a curse and a punishment, but why would a tauric body shape be deemed as such, unless one considered centaurs to be similarly cursed? How could that deity (the identity of which is a matter of debate) harbor such disdain for a form so aesthetically pleasing, simply because it’s different?

That said, I concede that the opposing viewpoint, particularly in the case of the first lamia, has merit. The entry into the clergy, in essence, is a pact between the deity and their cleric, granting the deity significant latitude to reward and punish and impose obligations on the follower. It’s widely accepted in the origin story that the first lamia (whose name might have been simply “Lamia”) violated this sacred covenant. She committed a transgression, an act of hubris, placing herself above her deity and betraying its trust. I don’t believe anyone disputes that this was an act of impiety deserving of some form of punishment—even an extreme one.

Where I truly draw the line is the unborn children within her womb who were cursed for no fault of their own. The sins of the parent do not belong to the child, and these innocents, like the first lamia’s victims, had nothing to do with her transgressions. Nonetheless, they have been forced to pay the price as the curse continues to prey upon them, driving their minds toward malicious thoughts.

The gods are cruel. The gods are unfair. But there is hope! Even in the most tragic hour, we can find the greatest hope. There are lamias who defy the curse and its urges and live their own lives, proving that the gods can’t take away their destiny and make them into something they refuse to be. Each of these lamias is different. For just a few examples, some embrace the beauty and identity of their lamia forms while others reject that, too, as a part of the curse they wish to overcome; some despise and scorn the deity they believe to have unfairly punished their forebear, while a small few hope that faith and repentance might change that deity’s mind. Perhaps someday, working together or apart, they will lift the curse and remove the deity’s compulsions, making all lamias truly free!

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Lamia Ancestry.name: 拉弥亚族裔 Lamia Ancestry
Lamia Ancestry.text:
An ancient curse, an ancient thirst, and an ancient grudge.

Deep in the wilderness, where the nightjars nest and the jackals prowl, there is a shrine. It is a very old shrine, built in an ancient style with pediments, columns, and porticos, though the elements have rendered the goddess’s face to mere suggestion. Inside, beneath a collapsed roof, is an inner sanctum, and in that sanctum are four reliefs, each telling a story.

The first relief tells of a great queen or high priestess. She was a favorite of the gods, blessed with wisdom, graced with beauty, and gifted with power, both secular and sacred. In her right hand, she holds a wand tipped with fennel and woven around with ivy, while in her left hand, she holds a knife, still sticky with blood. Upon her brow was a wreath of laurel leaves, with nine stars gazing benevolently down on her rule.

The second relief is badly damaged, and there are only fragments left. The queen has dropped her thyrsus wand and sacrificial knife, and the leaves of her wreath have fallen. A crime has occurred, a violation, something at once unspeakable and unforgivable. Among the few scholars who know of this shrine, there is great debate about the nature of the act. Was it some pollution of ritual, some shattering of divine taboo? Was it hubris to imagine that one so blessed by the gods might, in fact, be their equal? Was it a sin of the flesh, of forbidden and illicit love? Or was it perhaps, in light of what would come, an act of cannibalism, a blasphemous hunger that gave freedom? No one knows.

The third panel shows the queen, her face and hands bloodied (Her blood? Another’s?), before the altar of a temple—perhaps this very altar, in this very shrine. The goddess is present above the altar, but her face has been gouged out, her divine attributes chiseled away, rendering her anonymous to the ages. The queen’s expression is ambiguous. There may be remorse in those cold, marble eyes, or perhaps fury, or fear. Certainly, there must have been hatred. The goddess has raised a hand and, from it, ushers a dreadful mist, a curse given form and shape.

The final panel shows the queen transformed, but she is queen no more. Her crown is long gone, her jewelry torn away. She is monstrous now, her lower body a thing of serpentine coils, but worse is her face. There is a terrible hunger on the former queen’s face and blood upon her lips and teeth, now many and sharp. Around her, other monsters creep, smaller, lesser children of the curse, half human and half beast, and all bearing on their faces that terrible, cruel hunger.

The queen’s name is chiseled beneath the first panel. Lamia.

The queen is dead, in all likelihood—although with such great and hoary monsters, that is never certain—but her curse lives on, and so do her children. Taking their name from their progenitor, lamias are a species of supernaturally altered creatures, half-human, half-beast, cursed with an uncanny hunger for the blood and flesh of sapient creatures. A few of their kind resist their cannibalistic urges, but most don’t bother.

You Might...

  • Loathe the very concept of gods, fate, or destiny.
  • Constantly struggle against your hunger with daily affirmations, displacement mechanisms, or other addiction-treating techniques.
  • Be prickly or insecure in social settings, expecting everyone to view you as a monster.

Others Probably...

  • Wonder if you’re sizing them up for your next meal.
  • Are careful about believing anything you say.
  • Avoid bringing up religious traditions or festivals around you.

Physical Description

Lamias are clearly a supernatural species, even from the first glance. From the waist up, they have the bodies of humans, more or less indistinguishable from those of regular humans. However, these humanoid portions tend to be on the larger side, with all genders routinely hitting the proportions of a six-foot-tall human (though their actual height will vary a fair amount for reasons shortly to become apparent).

Below the waist, lamias have the body of an apex predator from the animal kingdom. In theory, any large, hungry animal is a possibility, and there are relatively well-attested cases of lamias with the bodies of crocodiles and wolves and a long-standing argument exists as to whether harpies are simply a subvariety of lamias. There are even a few accounts of orca lamias. Nevertheless, the overwhelming majority of lamias fall into two categories. The first has the lower body of a great cat, a lion, a leopard, or a tiger. The second has the “original” lamia body plan, with the lower half of an enormous serpent. These serpentine lamias are sometimes called matriarchs in honor of the first queen (even if they aren’t always female), while all other varieties are “merely” lamias.

The animal aspect of a lamia has no heritable component that anyone studying the matter has been able to discover; instead, it seems to have everything to do with where a lamia was conceived. Lion, cheetah, and leopard lamias live out on the grassy savannas where lamias are most common, while the cold mountains may be home to cougars and snow leopards. The exception to the rule seems to be serpentine lamias, which are possible even in places with no great constrictors, though even then, these lamias tend to have the markings and coloration of some local snakes.

Lamias have an average human lifespan, as their curse doesn’t erode their health, and so a lamia in good health can reasonably expect to reach its eightieth birthday. However, the rough lives most lamias lead tend to lead to far earlier deaths.

Society

The central challenge at the core of lamia society is how to live with their hunger. When the long-ago goddess cursed Lamia, she gave the curse two parts—that Lamia and all of her descendants would be beasts without and beasts within. The first part results in lamias’ somewhat idiosyncratic biology, but at the end of the day, a half-human, half-lion creature isn’t necessarily any stranger or more alien than a centaur, gnoll, or xotlxotl.

But it’s the second part of the curse that has real teeth—the hunger. Every lamia is born with what can only be described as an addiction to the blood and flesh of sapient beings. A single sip of blood tastes sweeter than ambrosia; a gobbet of raw flesh has all the savor of the finest feast. The prospect of a heart pulled still-beating from a chest is the dearest-held fantasy of many a lamia, and even a sniff of a wound is extremely likely to start a lamia salivating. Furthermore, the hunger never ever goes away. Every lamia is born with it, and every lamia will die with it, the price of Lamia’s ancient crime, visited upon her descendants a thousand times over.

From this hunger and this curse flow a number of different ramifications. First, lamias have become outcasts among the various peoples of the world. It almost doesn’t matter what kind of society—whether some charming little merchant republic on a seashore or a clan of nomadic pastoralists out on the steppe, none but the strangest or more compassionate of souls are terribly eager to invite someone they expect to be a flesh-eating horror to their home. For this reason, lamias are seen as bogeymen, monsters, or even the embodiments of the dangers of the waste and the wilds. Consequently, lamias are very rarely able to mingle in polite society. It’s not impossible, perhaps, especially with magic and deception, but there isn’t a country where a lamia can “be themselves” out in public without giving other people the heebie-jeebies.

As a result of their hunger and their outcast nature, most lamias have a powerful and honestly quite understandable resentment of the divine. Fundamentally, no matter how vile Lamia’s crimes were, why should modern lamias suffer for it? They hadn’t yet been born when the ancient queen broke her pact with the gods—indeed, their grandmothers had not been born, or their grandmothers in turn, and a dozen generations beyond that as well. But lamias of today are as cursed and outcast as Lamia was, and the injustice is absolutely galling to most modern lamias, from the most violent and aggressive to the most gentle and empathetic (indeed, sometimes the kinder lamias are actually more infuriated by the injustice than their more malicious brethren).

The result is a perpetual tragedy born of a divine overreaction. On the one hand, lamias absolutely have a justified grievance against the rest of the world. They are cursed for a crime they didn’t commit, with no obvious hope of redemption or reprieve. On the other hand, the very nature of that curse—the hunger—means that other mortals are quite justifiably wary of the lamias. One can be sympathetic to a tormented soul while still being uneasy about them eyeing your jugular as if they want to see what prizes are to be found within.

And, of course, here is the nasty little truth—it’s important for us to show compassion to those who suffer, but the scars of that suffering don’t automatically make you a good person. The lamias’ curse doesn’t teach them a valuable moral lesson or encourage them to be kinder to others or anything of the sort. It simply makes them suffer. Many lamias are deeply and profoundly bitter about their lot in life, but in ways that give them little sympathy for other people’s struggles or hardships. Did a farmer have a poor harvest this year? Is someone’s child sick? To many lamias, these are merely petty troubles when compared to the weight of centuries of unjust punishment and endless suffering.

Perhaps as a consequence of all of this, few lamias choose to build up grand societal structures. Few ever build villages or towns, and they rarely form great clans or armies. This has led some non-lamia scholars to speculate that lamias might be as little fond of each other as other mortals are. Typically, lamias live either alone or in small family groups, seldom more than a few dozen lamias (quite aside from everything else, securing enough meat to feed more lamias than that quickly grows challenging without serious infrastructure).

Some lamias do manage to find companionship with other mortals, but they typically only do so with people who are as outcast as themselves. These lamias tend to attach themselves to bandits or raiders or to small wilderness sects worshiping strange entities outside the divine, typically occult or primal beings.

Lamia Adventurers

Most lamias spend at least part of their lives wandering the world—sometimes by choice, other times by circumstance. Usually, adventuring lamias come from wilderness-focused backgrounds, with herbalists, hermits, hunters, nomads, and warriors all being particularly common. For their class, lamias usually incline towards the martial side of things, with barbarian being the single most common lamia class, followed by rangers and fighters. Spellcasting lamia are usually witches, though sorcerers and druids are also semi-common. Cleric, champion, or monk lamias are rarer than hen’s teeth, though, for obvious reasons.

Divisions

As a result of their disparate organization, lamia culture is a slender and fragile thing, a whisper network spread across a wide territory and maintained by travelers who might not last the next attack by dangerous beasts or members of other ancestries. Lamias might not gather formally, but they still talk to each other across their smaller social groups, and from those discussions, they have formed their own divisions, usually over the question of how, precisely, one is to react to their divine predicament.

The Deniers

Think of it logically. Lamias are driven to drink the blood and eat the flesh of all sorts of other sapient beings, with the notable exception of other lamias. However, if there are no other sapient beings around except for other lamias, then the matter… doesn’t come up. Likewise, who cares if you are an outcast and reviled if they’re all over there and you’re over here?

Deniers are lamias who opt to ignore their curse as much as they possibly can by going as far away from everyone else as they can manage. They go to remote forests, deep deserts, or lonely mountaintops and try to scratch away a life there. They usually become pastoralists of some flavor, herding sheep or goats and supplementing their carnivorous diet with hunting. The problem, of course, is that there aren’t many places in the world where you can live a cozy, pastoral life that isn’t already claimed, meaning that Deniers are usually driven to the most barren and miserable territory in the region. If there is some ungodly wasteland to be had, that is usually where one finds Deniers.

Other lamias tend to think of the Deniers as more than a little out of touch, making a futile attempt to ignore reality—and yet. Deniers are, in many ways, the keepers of lamia culture and the only people trying to build permanent lamia communities across the land. And they are occasionally successful at their goal! While most Denier steadings last only a few generations, there are some remote oasis communes where lamias have lived in peace for a thousand years and counting. As such, other lamias laugh at the Deniers, but it’s an uncomfortable laughter because sooner or later, they might need their Denier kinfolk and the support structures or knowledge they can offer.

Deniers tend to be fairly ascetic, hard-working folk as a whole. They live hard lives but believe that denying their curse is worth considerable sacrifice. Though they’d be dreadfully offended at the comparison, many Denier communes bear some resemblance to traditionalist religious communities. Relative to other lamias, Deniers are the least likely to travel, but on occasion, a Denier will set out to secure some aid for their community.

The Furies

The largest faction of lamias by far, the Furies are… well… extremely furious. They are filled with righteous anger at the gods, at other people, and at the very structure of the universe that would allow such an abominable weight to be put on generations unborn. Very well, decide the Furies; if the gods are out to destroy them, then the Furies will seek to destroy the gods.

Realistically, the Furies know that the death of all divinity isn’t going to happen anytime soon (or perhaps at all) and that, in all likelihood, wiping out the divine from the multiverse would have some dire consequences even if it did happen. The Furies are angry, but that doesn’t mean they’re fools. Nevertheless, the Furies can, at the very least, irritate and harass their own tormentors by attacking faith and religion however they can. Some Furies take the direct approach. They attack and defile isolated shrines, accost itinerant pilgrims, or raze remote abbeys. Such Furies often ally themselves with similarly antisocial folk, trading their power and magic for the sheer muscle necessary. Other Furies are subtler. They start sects or infiltrate other societies to start religious schisms or encourage heresies. Some Furies switch between the two approaches as the situation calls for it.

Most people think of Furies when they think of a lamia, which does little for the reputation of the lamia ancestry as a whole. To other lamias, the Furies are sometimes respected, but they are also sometimes reviled. On the one hand, the Furies fight with all their strength and are at least doing something about their curse, even if it isn’t terribly productive. On the other hand, however, their violence and hatred make them difficult people to love, even for those who might admire them.

Furies are most often characterized by their anger and their bitterness above all other emotions. Motivated by sheer, acrid spite, Furies can be energetic, proactive, and aggressive. What they are not, in general, is kind, friendly, or benevolent—very few Furies try to resist their hunger.

The Breakers

What once was done can always be undone. This is the motto of the Breakers. Once, a goddess cursed an ancient queen, and all lamias today suffer for this injustice. But all curses can be broken if one knows how to do it. The Breakers believe it is their job to do so, since all others lack their sheer resolve to persevere, even after centuries of failure.

The Breakers are one of the smallest divisions of lamia philosophy, roughly on par with the Hopeful. Nevertheless, they are well respected and admired among their lamia kin because the Breakers are also the most scholarly and supernaturally savvy of all the lamia divisions. They search out ancient and forgotten ruins, develop new forms of ritual and magic, and seek a practical solution to the lamias’ problems. The exact approach differs from one Breaker to another. Some figure that the curse is a purely technical problem, a question of energies being applied and redirected. Others imagine that any curse must have an escape clause and devote themselves to historical research or transcendental mysticism. Others still imagine what one god can do, another can undo, and so seek out divinities (demon lords, Enigmas, or stranger beings) that might be persuaded to end lamias’ collective plight for a suitable price.

Breakers also serve an essential role as the “connective tissue” of lamia society. Their research encourages them to travel or, at the very least, to communicate with other researchers. They tend to be the ones to wander from Denier commune to Fury bandit camp, bringing with them news and gossip. Breakers also tend to be more connected to non-lamia societies than most, maintaining correspondence with other scientists and scholars (who might or might not know they are conversing with a lamia).

Breakers are typically ambitious, intellectual, and driven lamias. Most of all, though, they are not used to taking “no” for an answer, persevering even after numerous setbacks or failures. Anything in this world can be dealt with and finessed if only the Breakers figure out how.

The Lost

What’s the point? Why should you even bother? If monsters were what the lamias were cursed to be, then monsters are what they might as well become. Why resist, why struggle, why fight against it? Better to indulge your nature and devour. This is the creed of the Lost.

This is quite the tragic mindset, and perhaps the greater tragedy for lamia culture is that the Lost are the second largest of the lamia divisions, almost as numerous as the Furies. However, viewing the Lost as the absence of any specific philosophy might be more accurate, as opposed to the Furies’ choice to respond with anger. The Lost are lamias who decided to embrace their curse and live as monsters, seeking every opportunity to prey on other species. Depending on the resources available to them, this might simply mean falling upon lone travelers and leaving only bones behind, or it might mean raiding settled communities, or even a more complex arrangement akin to a protection racket, where a settlement offers periodic sacrifices in exchange for the lamia protecting them from all other local threats. The Lost tend to get along quite well with the Furies, and mixed groups of Furies and Lost are often found bedeviling other societies.

That said, the Lost are not, as a general rule, terribly ambitious or proactive in their pursuits. It’s just as common for them to simply live their own lives in the wilderness, ignoring other sapient mortals unless an easy meal walks by. They’re not lazy, necessarily, but the Lost rarely see much point in trying to become more than they are.

The Hopeful

So, there’s a curse, a hunger, and a thousand years of misunderstanding (or perhaps a thousand years of understanding too well). These are certainly challenges, yes. Significant challenges. But challenges, as a rule, can be dealt with. We just need to channel our pain and grief productively, and with enough effort, we’ll find a way to get it done.

Smallest and strangest of the lamia divisions, Hopeful lamias are perhaps best understood as an offshoot or alternative perspective on the Breaker outlook. Except where the Breakers try to remove the curse and the Lost embrace the curse, the Hopeful instead seek to manage their curse. Hopeful lamias consider that there is no absolute rule requiring lamias to eat others. Oh, certainly, it’s tempting, it’s an addiction, it’s a serious challenge—but it’s not impossible. People do hard things all the time, and this is more worth doing than most. To that end, the Hopeful practice a number of techniques to try and take the edge off of their hunger, most notably a wide range of displacement activities. It is far better for a lamia to be obsessed with art than with other people’s jugulars, for instance.

There’s certainly an idealistic aspect here—Hopeful lamias explicitly and openly wish to be “good”—but there’s also a practical side to their actions. The goal of all of their efforts is to integrate with larger, settled societies, to be accepted as just another curious species among a thousand others. They want to live in bustling cities, to be able to simply buy food and not have to worry about violence or hunters. It’s a heady image, and it drives the Hopeful in their quest.

Of all lamias, Hopeful are by far the most likely to live in cities or towns or even rural villages filled with people of other ancestries and to have friends and even loved ones of different species. Aware of their ancestry’s dubious reputation, Hopefuls tend to disclaim their lamia identity politely instead of pretending to be some other species, real or fictional. Some use magic and sorcery to appear human, while others simply announce themselves as “liontaurs” or “snakefolk” or some such species. Hopeful who reveal their ancestry are likely to become adventurers in mixed groups of other ancestries, as they feel that their acts of heroism and bravery can help others come to terms with them and see lamias in a different light.

Most Hopeful begin their path as idealists, and thus their philosophy tends to skew young. But succeeding as a Hopeful lamia requires discipline, determination, and a decidedly hard-headed attitude toward oneself and one’s community. Elder Hopeful are among the most dangerous opponents around since, more than anyone else, they tend to plan for every eventuality. And they’re very good at it.

Lamia Sects and Communes

There are two basic types of lamia community. On the one hand, you have Denier communes. These are usually remote, pastoral villages hidden away in the most absolutely inhospitable and unappealing place that can be found. Aside from a well-honed streak of paranoia, these communes aren’t really that different from any other isolated village. Fury or Lost sects, meanwhile, are usually mixed-species engagements, with a lamia cult leader recruiting the lost, the dispossessed, the bitter, or simply the greedy and ambitious. Some lamias conceal their nature from these sects, even going so far as to prop up a figurehead through which to puppeteer the group, though long-established lamia sects usually end up knowing who is really in charge.

Beliefs

As a general rule, lamias skew sharply evil and almost as strongly chaotic. The first is due to the curse and the hunger—while it’s certainly possible to resist the hunger, it’s difficult and wearying, and it’s just so much easier to justify and excuse a certain level of murder and bloodshed. In terms of divisions, most non-evil lamias are found among the Hopeful and, to a lesser extent, among Deniers.

Their anarchic bent, meanwhile, is a result of both their rejection of the gods and organized faith and their outcast nature. Lamias simply aren’t used to working as a group and have a sharp disdain for anyone, mortal or divine, telling them what to do. The exceptions to these generalities tend to be found most often in Denier communities, though even those lamias tend to be neutral rather than out-and-out lawful.

As far as religion goes, the overwhelming majority of lamias aren’t just irreligious—they’re flat out misotheistic. That is to say, they don’t merely fail to worship the gods or deny their power, as an atheist might, but are explicitly and openly hateful of the gods. On rare occasions, a lamia might form an alliance of convenience with demon worshippers or the followers of some other sufficiently antisocial divinity, but this almost never extends to true faith.

That said, while lamias are seldom religious in the conventional sense, some lamias do have a strong sense of what might be described as ancestor worship for their progenitor, Lamia herself. In extreme cases, lamias might even make little shrines or sacrifices to Lamia, though more often, this simply involves viewing the ancient queen as a paragon of all appropriate virtues, such as self-reliance, ferocity, or determination. Other lamias despise their ancestor as much as they do the gods, blaming both Lamia and her divine disciplinarian equally for originating the horrible curse that afflicts them to this day.

Popular Edicts understand your curse and choose how to approach it on your own terms, despise the divine and their unforgivable deeds, hold a grudge

Popular Anathemas forgive the gods for their unfair actions, let someone else decide your fate for you, forget a kindness or a slight toward you

Lamia, the Queen

“She’s still out there. Yes, yes. I know everyone thinks she’s dead. It’s been… what? Three thousand years, give or take? But do you think the gods would let her off that easily, just let her slink away into oblivion after all she’s done?”

“No, she’s still alive. She lives up in the mountains, in a cave as nicely turned out as any palace. She’s changed, grown—I saw her once, and she can smash a building with a single twist of a coil or swallow someone whole in a single bite. And she’s immortal. She can’t be harmed by mortal blades or spells or anything else. The gods’ curse on her and on all of us.”

“Though. If she did die. Then so dies the curse.”

—Urban Legend, Origin Unknown

Names

Lamias tend towards old names, especially ones hailing from their original homeland’s culture. There is a strong tendency among lamia to name their children after their elder kin, especially those who may have recently died. Occasionally, this leads to some confusion among outsiders, who might not realize that the lamia named “Alcippe” is actually the fourth lamia of that name living in an area, rather than a single three hundred-year-old lamia.

Sample Names

Aeson, Aristeion, Cyrene, Empusa, Eriboea, Eric, Hylas, Idaeus Maeonia, Merope, Mormo, Polymede, Timo, Xenophon.

Lamia Heritages

Broadly, all lamias fall into one of two categories, which are mostly separate from their heritage: lamia matriarchs, with the forms of serpents, and hunter lamias, with the forms of various other predatory beasts (usually great cats). Despite the difference in names, lamia matriarchs don’t have any meaningful advantage over hunter lamias beyond receiving a bit of extra respect in lamia communities. Lamia heritages are utterly random rather than hereditary like most ancestries—any lamia can be born from any other lamia—but some heritages are found only among lamia matriarchs or only among hunter lamias.

Blasphemous Lamia
