People Writ Large.name: 大写的人群 People Writ Large
People Writ Large.text:
摘自艾瑟里娅·尼克斯的档案
放大书写的人

我们看待体型的方式很耐人寻味。即使同一族裔内部体型的些微差异，也会让人们在初次印象中就把某些性格特质归因于对方，并产生不同的情绪。比如，稍微高大一些的人可能显得强大、令人生畏、颇具威势。当体型越发走向两个极端时，这种倾向会被大幅强化。

一只特别娇小的生物往往显得可爱，激起人们的喜爱与保护欲。这种生物小到让那些痴迷于对称与外貌“缺陷”的人也更倾向于觉得它们美丽，因为体型差异会让所谓的“瑕疵”显得更小、更难以察觉。就这样，矮小的族裔在与我们互动时会获得显著优势——这与我曾提到宁芙（特征）因其外貌而占优势的情况类似，但在本质上又截然不同。由于人们认为它们不具威胁、珍贵且迷人，它们更可能得到善待，而不是一见面就遭到攻击。

然而，对巨人来说就不那么幸运了：体型更大的生物体验到的恰恰相反。它们高耸的体型会本能地让体型更小的生物竖起警惕的寒毛，即使那名巨人举止彬彬有礼、温和谦逊也是如此；原因在于，当面对大到足以成为潜在捕食者的存在时，多数族裔共有的原始猎物反应便会被触发。正因为它们如此庞大，它们的每一个缺陷与不足都会被放大：对一名特别巨大的巨人而言，一次踉跄就可能像我们折断一根树枝那样压碎一只木桶；它们也可能像我们忽略脚边的一只蚂蚁那样，没注意到脚下的一只小猫。与此同时，它们的每一处不对称都大到让小型族裔无法不去注意；一只小型生物的鼻子可能小得让我们必须眯起眼睛才看得见，而巨人的鼻子却可能大到有人类拳头那么大。因此，那些肤浅到会以此评判他人的人——遗憾的是，这样的人占了相当一部分——难免会对这些令他们恐惧与厌恶的因素产生执念，并因此将巨人视为略显“怪物”。这不过是同一种陈旧偏见的不同口味罢了。

更糟的是，一些巨人社群信奉强权即公理，确实会利用更大的体型与力量去征服周边的小个子族群，似乎“印证”了那种猎物本能；这一切混杂在一起，形成由成见调制的鸡尾酒，酿成一团乱麻。于是，在传说故事里，巨人被描绘成反派或需要被克服的“怪物”的情况，远比它们仅仅被描绘成体型不同的人要常见得多（尤其是与那些看起来像类人但更小的族裔相比）。但这仍不过是确认偏误：他们真能说自己的族裔就没有任何社群会用力量或暴力去压迫弱者吗？当然不能。可他们也没有因此彻底否定自己的族裔，不是吗？正因为对巨人而言，这些轶事会“验证”任何既有观念，所以这些故事对人们的影响力才比本应有的更大。要夺回我们的公正，就需要有意与这种由体型引发的本能对抗，只把巨人看作它们本来的样子：放大书写的人。

—— 艾瑟里娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物的辩护者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
People Writ Large

The way we look at size is fascinating. Even slight variations of size within an ancestry lead people to attribute certain personality traits toward another on first impression and experience different emotions. For instance, someone slightly larger might seem powerful, intimidating, and commanding. This tendency is drastically exacerbated when sizes move further and further toward the extremes.

An especially tiny creature often seems cute and inspires feelings of affection and protectiveness. Such a creature is so small that those obsessed with symmetry and “imperfections” in appearance tend to find them more beautiful as well, as the size difference makes any so-called blemishes” smaller and harder to notice. In this way, diminutive ancestries gain a significant advantage when it comes to their interactions with us, in a parallel but fundamentally different way to the one I mentioned that nymphs do due to their physical appearance. Since people see them as non-threatening, precious, and attractive, they are more likely to be treated well rather than attacked on sight.

Unfortunately for giants, however, larger creatures experience the reverse. Their towering size instinctively raises the hackles of smaller creatures, even if the giant is acting polite and gentle, due to a primal prey response when confronted with something large enough to be a potential predator that most ancestries share. Because they are so immense, their every flaw and shortcoming are amplified: for a particularly huge giant, a stumble could crush a barrel in the same way we might snap a twig, and they might fail to notice a kitten underfoot in the same way we might miss an ant. Meanwhile, their every asymmetry is large enough that smaller ancestries can’t help but notice; while a tiny creature’s nose might be so small we have to squint even to see it, a giant’s could be so big that it’s the size of a human fist. Thus, those shallow enough to judge a person on such a basis—a significant portion of the populace, sadly—can’t help but become obsessed with these factors that scare and repulse them and see giants as slightly more “monstrous” because of them. It’s just a different flavor of the same old prejudice.

It doesn’t help that some giant communities believe in might makes right and absolutely do use their greater size and strength to subjugate smaller folk around them, seemingly justifying that prey instinct, and it all blends together into that cocktail of preconceptions to create a big mess, with tales featuring giants as villains or “monsters” to be overcome far more often than they depict giants as simply people of a different size (especially when compared to ancestries that look like humanoids but smaller). But this is still nothing more than confirmation bias: can they really say that their own ancestry doesn’t have any communities that use strength or violence to subjugate the weak? Of course not. And yet, they haven’t written off their own ancestry entirely, have they? It’s because, for giants, these anecdotes confirm any preconceived notions, so the stories have more power over people than they otherwise would. To take back our impartiality, we need to intentionally fight against the instincts with size and simply see giants as they are: people writ large.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Giant Ancestry.name: 巨人族裔 Giant Ancestry
Giant Ancestry.text:
Giants are large humanoids who grow in stature and strength as their accomplishments mount. Deed by deed, they carve their name into history, becoming powerful living legends

Giants are an ancestry with roughly humanoid builds and large statures, well-known for their strength and intimidating countenance. They’re proud and ambitious, constantly striving to outdo their rivals, surpass their past exploits, and climb the social hierarchies of which they’re a part. They’re canny survivors, and over the ages, they’ve adapted to different environments, elemental affinities, and ecological niches, spawning a wide variety of different giant heritages, each with their own culture, values, and traditions. Despite cultural differences, nearly all giants desire glory, respect, and power.

According to giant mythology, giants are literal living legends—beings incidentally given form by the epic acts of the gods. Every drop of blood spilled in deific wars, every ounce of sweat poured while toiling over a divine creation, every curse of rage, every oath of kinship, every heart-wrenching betrayal—each deific act sent energy into the world from which the first giants sprang forth, forging their own forms from their deific potential through sheer force of will and determination. In short, they believe that they birthed themselves from divine energy and are therefore semi-divine themselves.

But what divine conflicts formed the giants, and from whose blood did they emerge? The answer you’ll receive to that question varies depending on which giant you ask, as it’s been lost to the mists of time. For example, many giants feel that the first of their ancestors emerged during the war known as the Titanomachy, when the god-beasts of old and the dragon deity Talir spilled blood. But unlike the various heritages of dragons, born from Talir’s eggs after devouring each of the god-beasts’ remains, giants have different features and abilities. Surely, then, they aren’t some form of dragon. And yet, they also have distinctly humanoid forms, unlike the ancestry of titans, who share their monstrous features with their god-beast forebears. And so, from which gods were the giants born? Were they ancient gods from the world’s past, long before the New Gods came? Or were they formed from the blood of the Parents Tovah and Lilin themselves, shed when they turned against their firstborn Talir on behalf of mortalkind? If you ask the gathering army of einherjar, giants will someday serve together as the soldiers of the evil ones in some climactic final battle, and yet few giant heritages can agree with each other on anything, let alone come together as some giant army. As living legends, giants have huge personalities, and attempting to define them all in a single way is doomed to failure. Perhaps they arise not from a single possible source but from all of these divine sources at once, and so all the tales about them are simultaneously true? If so, then their humanoid form might be a reflection of some other hidden truth.

Whether giants’ belief that they are born of the divine is rooted in fact or fiction, it’s instilled in giants the certainty that they, as a people and as individuals, have limitless potential. Most giants believe that with enough drive and determination, they can triumph over any obstacle, overcome any adversity, and become something bigger, stronger, and more powerful than ever before—perhaps even mightier than a god. Thus, giants strive to carve their name into the annals of history by deed and daring, and whether they succeed or fail at that challenge, they rise and fall in a blaze of glory. Giants, as a rule, are never content with what they already have. Instead, they’re always pushing harder, reaching higher, and striving for more. More than they once had. More than they could achieve before. More than others expect to be possible. These societal traits mean that most giants are incredibly ambitious social climbers who believe, without a shred of doubt, that they deserve the world.

While many people assume that a giant’s power arises mainly from their size and strength, in actuality, a giant’s true strength comes from their very legend—their deeds, their accomplishments, and their victories. With each triumph, a giant grows bigger, stronger, and more powerful, in turn earning the respect of their peers anew. And yet, simply performing an epic or meritorious deed isn’t nearly enough for most giants—they need to celebrate it, brag about it, and shove their glorious accomplishments in the faces of those around them. They need other people to celebrate them, to laud them, to envy them, and even to fear them. They chronicle their own legend in stories and songs, they mark their victories with trophies and treasures, and they even use their own bodies as a canvas to display their greatest deeds. All who meet a giant are confronted not only by the giant themself in physical form but also by the giant’s past accomplishments hanging around them like a victor’s wreath, both literally and metaphorically.

Giants craft themselves into living legends, piece by piece, one act at a time, and by doing so, they become legendary. If you want to play a proud and ambitious powerhouse who’s determined to forge their own glorious legend, you should play a giant.

You Might...

  • Boast loudly of your accomplishments to anyone who’s willing to listen, or even to the world at large if no one is around to hear of your great deeds directly from your lips.
  • Be ambitious, constantly striving for loftier and loftier goals, or reaching to seize a new advantage.
  • Keep a bag filled with trophies, trinkets, or treasures that commemorate your most memorable adventures, raids, or deeds.
  • Revel in the fear you instill in others.

Others Probably...

  • Are intimidated by your size or strength.
  • Are impressed by your unwavering confidence.
  • Believe you have a short temper, fondness for violence, or both.
  • Think they can fool you, since they believe that someone so large would necessarily be unable to notice the smaller details.

Physical Description

Giants are long-lived compared to most of the smaller humanoid ancestries, with most giants averaging a lifespan of 250 to 350 years. Cloud giants and storm giants tend to live even longer, with the eldest grazing 600 years. Hill giants, on the other hand, tend to live for a scant 100 years—they’re capable of living up to 250 years if they die of natural causes, but they tend to die younger than that due to violence or misadventure.

To say giants are simply “large” or “big” is a gross understatement. At birth, giants are the size of full-grown halflings; by childhood, they’re the size of a full-grown human; and by adulthood, they’re so large that they tower over most other people. For most people, that is quite large. And yet, that isn’t the end of things either. As a giant accomplishes grand deeds, they grow even larger still, with accomplished giants becoming huge by middle age and the greatest giant heroes, leaders, and villains growing to truly titanic proportions—an event not witnessed in generations of giants seeking that goal.

As a rule, giants commemorate and enshrine their accomplishments in the ways that resonate with them best, often in more than one way. They do this verbally using stories and songs but also physically using trophies, trinkets, treasures, and even body art drawn or carved into their very flesh. Nearly all giants maintain a collection of trophies, which they usually carry with them in a bag so that they’re always close at hand. This collection includes pillaged treasures, valuable objects, and trinkets fashioned from (or taken from) the enemies and creatures they’ve vanquished, whether in glorious battle or through some other clever means. Most giants also work trinkets and trophies into their attire in various ways, adding horns and antlers to helmets, fashioning massive claws and teeth into necklaces, weaving jewelry into giant braided cords better suited to their size, and wearing the crowns and circlets of smaller people as rings or earrings. After all, what better message of their glory than to wear the crowns of multiple defeated monarchs as finger rings? This specialized crafting style leads giants to often use the monster parts from beasts they’ve vanquished (see the monster parts system from Battlezoo Bestiary for more details) in order to fashion incredible magic items. On a more personal basis, giants use their own bodies as a canvas to record and display their deeds. The nature of these markings varies by heritage but can include marks, sigils, runes, tattoos, brands, and scars. Whatever best shows off their mightiest victories in a way that those meeting the giant can’t help but see and can’t possibly deny.

Cloud giants are graceful and regal, with skin that tends toward pale or pastel blue. Their hair is light and airy and billows around their heads like a halo of ever-shifting clouds, while their hair ranges in color from white to black but usually tends to be gray. Cloud giants prefer to wear elaborately draped robes in whites and creams decorated with highly detailed gold beading, braiding, embroidery, and lace, and they favor gold jewelry with white or clear gemstones, such as diamonds, opals, crystals, and quartz. They also often mark their bodies with elegant white tattoos that resemble lines of light coiling across their skin. These tattoos are commonly symbolic and include sigils and runes that tally victories or have meaning rather than literal depictions of their deeds.

Fire giants are incredibly militant and are always fully armed and armored. They have a powerful, muscular build well-honed from a lifetime spent waging war and operating forges. Their skin tends toward shades of peach, pink, or pale orange, while their hair is bright orange and red and flickers and dances as if it were made of living flame. Fire giants prefer to wear red or black clothes along with heavy full-plate armor. They’re often heavily scarred, and they wear these natural marks with pride.

Frost giants are worn, weathered, and incredibly intimidating, with skin that varies from blue to gray, along with a mane of white hair. Frost giants prefer to keep their hair and beards long and flowing and then braid them into patterns that convey their prowess. They wear metal armor with clothes that incorporate the fur, skin, teeth, and tusks of slain beasts and enemies. They wield big, intimidating weapons and braid trophies from their victims into their hair and beards. Frost giants favor angular runic tattoos that literally chronicle their deeds and titles alongside “power runes” meant to enhance their strength, fortitude, and ferocity.

Hill giants are heavyset and broad, with skin color that ranges across the spectrum. They have pronounced brow ridges, bushy eyebrows, and fine hair in shades of brown and black. Most are incapable of growing a beard and keep their hair short. As the most successful hill giants typically gain access to the best food and resources, and bully others into doing their own work, most hill giants aspire to broad and soft builds. Hill giants are thick-skinned and heavily scarred, and they value every mark, scar, and bruise. They often make sure to enhance their natural scars with scarification, which they use to falsify bigger deeds and tales. Hill giants favor body piercings and wear trophies as decorations. They craft their clothes from fur, leather, and stolen baubles, repurposing cloaks and blankets as skirts, using necklaces as bracelets, and perching countless hats and crowns atop their heads—even if they don’t fit. To a hill giant, it’s not how an item fits, it’s how many items you can pile onto your body.

Stone giants are tall and slender, with wiry muscles and a slightly stooped stature. They have gray, craggy skin and are entirely hairless. They dress lightly, favoring practical leather attire with fine stitching and embroidery in muted colors. They wear cloaks designed to blend in with their surroundings and often go barefoot. They mark their bodies with geometric tattoos in black ink, which are highly symbolic and are only applied during special rituals.

Storm giants are muscular and vain, with skin that ranges in color from blue to purple. Their hair varies greatly but is always a dark color, typically dark purple, blue, or black. Storm giants have silver eyes that glow when their emotions spike, particularly from anger or passion. They favor loose clothes that cover little of their bodies—all the better to show off their physique and tattoos. Storm giant tattoos are highly detailed and depict their deeds in colorful pictures and images. As storm giants are changeable, they tend to change their hair and attire as whim dictates, and they’re the giants who display the broadest range of styles.

The Source Of Strength

Let’s start by getting the obvious out of the way: giants are big, and throughout their lives, they can get bigger. Big creatures, big muscles, big mass, big strength. Simple, yes?

Not so.

Giants are indeed big, muscular, and heavy, and this does, of course, contribute to their strength and power. But a giant’s strength can’t be explained away quite so easily. No, in my eyes, there is something more to giants. Something else… powerful.

They say a giant is only as big as their deeds. They drape themselves in trophies of battle and stolen plunder. They brag of their accomplishments, crafting songs and tales to further spread word of their exploits. They value their scars and mark their body with tattoos and brands that depict and chronicle their triumphs and grand feats. These prizes and legends are incredibly important to giants culturally, and a giant whose legend or trophies are attacked considers their very soul under direct assault. One could assume that a giants’ trophies are merely decorative and their penchant for braggadocio a sign of their vanity and pride. While possible, I believe that there is something greater at work.

Many scholars posit that giants gain literal strength from their trophy collection and that seizing a giant’s ever-present bag of oddments and tokens can thus weaken them—a highly dubious claim, in my opinion, but a surefire way to anger them, if nothing else. Others think the source of a giant’s strength is more metaphysical than that. That it rests, perhaps, within the legends and stories themselves. Like a leader who draws strength from their followers or a false god who draws power from their believers, becoming closer to true divinity.

I suspect that neither of these is entirely correct. So, what then is the answer? Where do giants get their strength? Is it an act of accomplishment in itself? Their confidence? Their pride? Is their unwavering belief in their own potential and power simply a self-fulfilling prophecy?

Giants believe that they created themselves from deific energy shed by the gods during their exertions. That they are their own makers, in charge of their own fate. I don’t put much stock in this belief. Certainly, religious scholars would disagree.

And yet… perhaps there is some truth to this? A kernel of divine might buried deep in their bloodline, shrouded by time and history? This raises the question: who were the first giants, and where did this so-called ‘divine spark’ originate? What did they do to earn it? Was it given or seized?

A question worth investigating.

Giant Adventurers

Giant adventurers usually favor classes that play to their strength—literally—with barbarian and fighter being most common. Beyond that, giants also have certain preferences based on heritage. Cloud giants often become bards, fire giants frequently become champions, frost giants regularly become rangers, hill giants sometimes become rogues, and stone giants tend to become druids or rangers. In general, giants avoid classes that force them to rely on someone else for power, such as clerics, oracles, and witches. Spellcasting giants are often druids or sorcerers. The most common giant backgrounds are animal whisperer, bandit, entertainer, herbalist, hermit, hunter, nomad, sailor, scout, tinker, and warrior.

Society

Most giants are highly ambitious, always striving to accomplish great deeds, rise above their kin, and become better—bigger, stronger, more powerful, and better respected, with a wider sphere of influence than ever before. They’re proud and boisterous, bold and brave, and always ready to tackle the next obstacle and face the next challenge before them. Yet, giants are also stubborn and foolhardy, so enamored with themselves that they’re prone to underestimating the strength and cunning of their foes. Many giants are eventually brought low by their own self-importance and overinflated egos. That moment of fatal hubris, much like a hero out of legend, leaves open a fatal gap in their otherwise impenetrable defenses, allowing the giant’s foe to strike the telling blow that decides the battle once and for all.

Giants strive to make themselves into a legend, whether directly through deeds or through their mighty boasts after the fact. The claiming of trophies and crafting of trinkets and mementos are an integral part of giant society, as is the art of tattooing, scarification, and body modification. These activities serve not only to commemorate a giant’s deeds but also to craft a giant’s image into something iconic, fearsome, or respected by all. While all giants can, of course, earn glory through strength of arms and heroism, giants of particular heritages can also achieve respect for other tasks that their people value. For example, a fire giant might earn great prestige after successfully forging a legendary blade.

Most giants tend to live on the fringes and frontiers of the world, rather than in metropolitan centers of population. They yearn for space, as the wide open spaces allow them to feel unconstrained and full of possibility. On the flip side, small, cramped spaces tend to make giants feel trapped or boxed in; many giants are claustrophobic, and even those who aren’t tend to grow depressed when kept contained or restrained for too long. When faced with such conditions, most giants either quietly wither and fade away or, more often, loudly rage and rebel against whatever dares to restrict them in that way. As a giant gains respect and power, they tend to branch out into the untamed wilds in order to claim territory or land of their own.

Giants have carved niches for themselves in the wildest and most inhospitable places of the world, evolving and growing just like their legends and tales in order to excel in their environment of choice. While the process is akin to that of animals adapting to their own ecological niches, giants display this growth through story and struggle, rather than the natural selection of evolution. Giant territories rarely overlap across different clans or social groups—they’re just too big, too brash, and too proud to share their land with one another. This isolation means that giants rarely mingle outside their own preexisting social group, save for specific diplomatic or religious reasons, or for large raucous celebrations every decade or so where giants gather to spread their tales to the widest possible audience.

Giant settlements vary greatly in their architecture, social structure, and size based on factors like heritage, resources, and neighbors. However, those few giants who build permanent settlements share significant commonalities with each other, and thus so too do the massive settlements they construct. As befitting the living legends who dwell there, a giant settlement’s streets are wide, its buildings are towering, and its windows are impressive and frequent. Many giant settlements are ruled by jarls—accomplished warrior monarchs who earned their title after challenging and besting the previous jarl in a contest of strength and martial skill. However, that’s not to say that a single powerful giant with poor social and leadership skills can remain as the jarl simply by defeating any challengers in single combat. A jarl must be as accomplished a leader as they are a warrior, lest their rule be cut short by a group uprising. Holding hot-headed and self-important giants in check and unified is a daunting challenge that few giants can accomplish for long. There’s always another challenger striving to claim your holdings and title and to topple your legend to grow their own. Of course, there are exceptions who prove this rule, giant jarls who are so individually powerful that even a group of their fellows can’t hope to defeat them working together and yet so focused on their skill at arms that they don’t tend to politics or maneuvering. What happens in these cases is that the other giants usually stick around to benefit from the prestige of their jarl’s mythic might while deputizing an acting assistant jarl to make the actual decisions. If the powerful jarl’s personality is so loathsome that it outstrips the respect for their might, the other giants might have no other choice but to quietly abandon their home, leaving the jarl as the ruler of an empty ghost town.

Like many people, giants are fond of animals and they tend to keep pets—though their pets are certainly “wilder” than most. Bears, mammoths, dinosaurs, and megafauna are among the most popular, as are dangerous beasts and dragons like griffons, hippogriffs, chimeras, and drakes.

Heritages

Giants of different heritages build drastically different societies, though all share a focus on growing their own tale into an epic legend, each in their own way.

Cloud Giant

Cloud giants are the most political of giants. They prefer an urban lifestyle, and so they build roads and cities suited to their tastes, with grand monuments, amphitheaters, and coliseums. Their constructions favor pale colors and shiny surfaces, making heavy use of marble, limestone, glass, gold, and precious gemstones. The grandest of their cities soar through the sky on a bed of solidified clouds. Many of these settlements are city-states, bound together by alliances and trade. Others are empires, with vast swaths of territory under their control. Sometimes, these nations are peaceful, but other times, they’re raiders or conquerors who subjugate their neighbors and take what they desire by force of arms.

As diplomats, cloud giants are the most likely to reach out to their brethren in peace or with purpose and the most likely to interact with other humanoid nations and empires. They have an interest in trade and new technologies, but they strive to balance new innovations against existing traditions, careful to ensure their lofty way of life doesn’t become warped by industry or unfamiliar ideas. Cloud giants can earn glory through their holdings, civic projects, and hard-won negotiations, as well as through strength of arms.

Fire Giant

Fire giants are hard-working and militaristic. They live near volcanoes, where they stoke the fires of industry, working massive forges that churn out arms and armor with alarming speed. They’re the smiths and crafters of giantkind, but they’re also the most organized. Their communities tend toward hierarchical military empires, where one always knows their place and follows the orders of their superiors. Fire giants work well together, both on and off the battlefield, and they’re highly trained, enduring combat training exercises since childhood and drilling endlessly with their fellow soldiers. As a people, they’re focused on learning new combat techniques, mastering the art of blacksmithing, and finding new ways to dominate their enemies and rise through the military ranks. Fire giants can earn glory through tactical prowess, dutiful service, and by crafting exceptional arms and armor, in addition to strength of arms.

Frost Giant

Frost giants are among the most feared of giantkind and are well-known for those among them who act as cruel and vicious marauders. Most frost giant clans are nomadic, claiming a large swath of territory and launching attacks by land or by sea on the neighbors beyond their borders. They acquire most of their resources through pillage and plunder, making raiding an important part of their survival strategy. Within their territories, frost giants maintain numerous sites of power and significance, including monuments, religious sites, tombs, and “founts”—natural or megalithic sites believed to empower giants who perform the appropriate rites and sacrifices within them and destroy any unworthy giants who attempt to use them. Frost giants have the most tumultuous leadership—jarls rarely rule for long and are constantly fighting off challengers. Frost giants can earn glory through leading successful raids, stealing impressive plunder, and crafting a fearsome reputation in addition to the strength of arms.

Hill Giant

Hill giants are brutish bullies. Like frost giants, they’re raiders and thieves, but unlike frost giants, hill giants are notoriously disorganized and are prone to squatting in the towns and settlements they’ve plundered rather than returning home. They’re typically appalling strategists who work poorly together, prone to rushing in headfirst without thought to tactics, consequences, or the greater ramifications of their actions. They usually put their own personal glory over the collective glory of their clans or communities. To many hill giants, the greatest prize is leisure time—the ability to do absolutely nothing while the people you boss around do everything for you.

Many hill giants are quick-tempered and violent, with a crude sense of humor. Yet, they’re also cowards who flee or surrender when they’re outmatched and often get in over their heads. Dishonest, crafty, and utterly untrustworthy, these hill giants are willing to trick or betray someone to get ahead or get by—even their own allies. Hill giants can earn glory through the spoils they steal and the lackeys they command, in addition to strength of arms.

Stone Giant

Stone giants are the most peaceful of giants. They’re stoic artists, herders, and healers who deeply revere the natural world. They value wisdom, patience, and magical ability—particularly the ability to heal wounds, cleanse bodies, soothe spirits, and divine the future. Stone giants live in communal yurts, longhouses, or caves located along mountain slopes, craggy ranges, and sheltered valleys. They’re sedentary, but they maintain a wide territory they use for herding, grazing, and gathering. They treat their surroundings with respect, always aiming to adapt to their environments rather than alter their environments to suit their needs better. Stone giants are community-minded—they typically get along well with one another and place the needs of their band before their own personal desires. They form bands as large as their surroundings can support and form friendly relations with neighboring stone giant bands. They tend to avoid contact with outsiders, preferring to hide or pass unnoticed rather than invite social interaction. While stone giants are willing to let others pass through their territories, they don’t take kindly to outsiders entering their homes nor to trespassers who harm the environment—such infractions are likely to rouse stone giants to united action or violence. Stone giants can earn glory through displays of knowledge or wisdom, magical feats, and synchronicity with nature, in addition to strength of arms.

Storm Giant

Storm giants are passionate guardians of the sea and sky who bask in the crashing waves and the relentless storm. They live along coastlines, rocky archipelagoes, tropical islands, and underwater. They’re typically benevolent, but even so, most storm giants are driven by their passions and emotions, quick to forge friendships but just as quick to take offense at a slight. This makes their constantly shifting moods a challenge to navigate. Luckily, storm giants tend to forgive and forget, making it easier for them to mend relationships—save for when a storm giant believes they have been betrayed, which can cause them to hold a grudge that lasts for centuries.

Storm giants are impulsive and spontaneous, quick to rush into the fray or to the aid of allies, but often too unreliable to be a constant support. Their outbursts can sometimes cause more trouble than their help is worth. Storm giants can earn glory through impassioned acts, such as a tempestuous love affair or a destructive rage, in addition to strength of arms.

Giant Settlements

The structure of a giant’s home is based upon their heritage and culture. Cloud giants, fire giants, stone giants, and storm giants are sedentary, with cloud giants and fire giants building their own cities and settlements, storm giants preferring an isolated and independent life, and stone giants living in peaceful communities. Hill giants are sedentary but opportunistic, sometimes making their own simple camps and other times squatting in or taking over the homes and towns of other people. While frost giants are typically nomadic raiders.

Rings

Across all giant societies, there are four organizations that welcome only the best and most respected into their fold. Giants who are welcomed into these organizations, called rings, leave their communities behind and are forced to start from scratch at the bottom of the pecking order in their associated ring—a gamble for a giant accustomed to being respected and feared!

Ring Of The Battleborn

To enter the Ring of the Battleborn, a giant must be an exceptional warrior—at the minimum. More than that, they must be a clever tactician and a charismatic leader. They’re the commanders of giantkind who unite giant nations into terrifying armies capable of subjugating the world—or defending it. Battleborn revel in combat but also in tests of will and endurance and in outwitting their foes.

Ring Of The Eternal

To enter the Ring of the Eternal, a giant must be a talented healer capable of both mundane herbalism and magical healing. Among the Ring of the Eternal, they learn the formulas for countless medicines and poultices and powerful rituals capable of animating corpses or bringing the dead back to life. The most powerful of the Eternal attain immortality, touched by neither illness, pain, nor time, and unable to be killed by any means of this mortal world.

Ring Of The Lauded

To enter the Ring of the Lauded, a giant must be a talented storyteller who forged their own accomplishments into a legend—which they need not deserve. Among giants, Lauded are well known as chroniclers and sages who keep the history of all giantkind alive. While this is true, it’s also a lie by omission. In addition to their duties as chroniclers, historians, storytellers, and entertainers, Lauded are influencers and masterminds who achieve their goals from the sidelines. Among the Ring of the Lauded, giants learn to manipulate others to action, bringing emotions to the fore with a song or tale and inciting their audience to dance to their whims. Like a grand puppeteer, Lauded can alter the course of history, pushing giant communities to do their bidding with subtlety and tact.

Ring Of The Godsworn

To enter the Ring of the Godsworn, a giant must be born under an auspicious sign or during a powerful celestial event, which marks them as blessed by the godsblood flowing through their veins. But that’s only the beginning—from there, a Godsworn must distinguish themselves as an exceptional giant in countless ways until it’s clear that the giant has the potential to accomplish the ultimate achievement: becoming a divine being.

Beliefs

Most giants are independent and proud, constantly striving to get what they deserve—which they believe is everything. They tend toward selfishness and callousness but run the gamut from good to evil and from lawful to chaotic. Giants are not particularly pious. Those who do worship gods tend to favor gods that value combat and war or the acquisition of material possessions or power, such as Zatera the Bloody General in the world of Alacar. Others favor gods who encourage freedom and independence, such as Lilin the Mother in the world of Alacar. Cloud giants also favor gods of art and the sky, fire giants also favor gods of industry and order, frost giants also favor gods of sailing and chaos, hill giants also favor gods of deception and violence, stone giants also favor gods of nature and community, and storm giants also favor gods of the sea, sky, or powerful emotions.

Popular Edicts acquire power, boast about your accomplishments, prove your strength, strive for your goals

Popular Anathemas settle for less than you deserve, accept ignominy, be humble

Piety

Giants believe that they created themselves from deific matter or energy, not that they were created by gods. This has caused most giants to consider themselves god-like and therefore related to the gods, not subservient to them. To many, the thought of beseeching a god for aid is as distasteful as asking one’s elder cousin for aid. This means that most giants aren’t very pious. However, giants understand that gods have power—power that they’re willing to share with those who are loyal to them. To a giant near the bottom of the social hierarchy, faith in a god is a tempting tool to ascend the ranks and earn power. These giants often become holy warriors or favored champions of their chosen deity and maintain their piety throughout their lives.

Names

Giants are given powerful, impressive names at birth, and they strive to live up to those names. Most have at least three syllables and favor hard sounds. As they age, giants earn (or choose) new titles and monikers, which they bear with pride. A giant aims to earn as many titles as possible—all the better to carve their name into the annals of history!

Sample Names

Algersdir, Berigmar, Djurundul, Gravaniktor, Krevishkar, Numinelde, Ragnara, Thurvindar, Yurgrinda.

Sample Titles

Boneripper, Dragonslayer, Heartrender, Jarlsbane, The Calamity, The Devastator, The Ferocious, The Mighty, Thunderclap, Worldender.

The Measure of a Person.name: 衡量一个人的尺度 The Measure of a Person
The Measure of a Person.text:
摘自艾瑟瑞亚·尼克斯的档案
一人的衡量

你能说清究竟是谁真正造就了你吗？你能指出你形体与性格的每一个独立侧面，并追溯其渊源吗？你或许会说，你的身体特征显然源自你的父母与其他祖先——眼睛的颜色、耳朵的形状，以及你脚背上有多少毛发。但那些每天锻炼的人，或通过手术改变体型的人又如何？那在喧闹的酒馆斗殴中一次又一次被打断而留下的鼻梁轻微歪斜又如何？你也许会宣称，你的道德完全是你特定成长环境的结果，由你的社群或你的宗教教导而来。但当你在那些软弱的时刻多喝了一杯本不该喝的麦酒时，又该怪谁？而你为什么还把那个显然会带坏你的朋友留在身边？

显然，每个人都是由诸多境遇与考量交织而成的丰饶锦缎。人不该只凭一次交谈就对陌生人妄下严苛的评断。看似是在当下情势里无法做出决定，可能只是因为那个人的成长经历使其在行动之前会先考虑每一种结果。这并不是说你就该原谅那些不断越界的人；有些人会一再以毫无悔意的方式向你展示他们是谁。然而，总体而言，最好还是先给人以善意推定。而这种态度也理应延伸到那些被社会视为“不合常规”的人。

不幸的是，傀儡裔正是这些被放逐者中的一员，而原因仅仅是他们出生时的怪异。由于某种魔法的命运扭曲、魔法事故、纯净之心的愿望，或古怪的实验，这些个体从强大的构装体中诞生——构装体与赋予其行动的灵魂融为一体并获得生命，成为活体血肉与构装体的混合体。在我看来，这是一种真正而自然的诞生，丝毫不比其他族裔所经历的过程逊色或不自然；若说有什么不同，那就是这种奇迹甚至更具灵性与个人色彩。然而，傀儡裔常遭迫害或被误解。不知为何，许多人很难与一个曾是铁制自动机、或泥塑雕像的人产生共鸣。他们为何如此区别对待傀儡裔？是否因为他们对“其曾是无心智的构装体与驱动之力”这一观念怀有敌意，于是认为他们作为如今之“存在”不那么值得尊重？可多数族裔不也是在无心智的卵与恰当的肥沃之物结合时形成的吗。这又有何不同？

没有任何理由要把傀儡裔与其他任何族裔区别对待。我们应将他们视作奇迹，而非将他们妖魔化为存在的异常。但我们太容易因为自以为是的差异而排斥他人。唯有当我们能克服这种偏见，我们才会明白，“人”的真正衡量在于我们的同理心——在于我们理解不同视角之人的能力。若我们做不到，那真正“不像人类”的并不是傀儡裔。

—— 艾瑟瑞亚·尼克斯，空间元素的传令使，怪物的代言人

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
The Measure of a Person

Can you say who truly made you? Can you point to each individual facet of your form and personality and trace its lineage? You might say your physical characteristics are obviously derived from your parents and other ancestors—the color of your eyes, the shape of your ears, and the amount of hair on the top of your feet. But what about the person who exercises daily or performs surgery to change their body shape? What about the slight crookedness of a nose broken time and again in rowdy bar brawls? You might claim that your morals are solely the result of your particular upbringing, taught to you by your community or your religion. But who is to blame for those moments of weakness when you have one more ale than you know you should have? And why do you keep that one friend who is clearly a bad influence around?

Clearly, each person is a rich tapestry of circumstances and considerations. One shouldn’t judge a stranger too harshly based on a single interaction. What might seem like an inability to make a decision in the heat of the moment could be the result of an upbringing that led that person to consider every outcome before acting. This isn’t to say that you should forgive people of constant transgressions; some individuals repeatedly show you who they are without remorse. However, it is generally better to give people the benefit of the doubt. And that should definitely extend to those who society considers to be outside of the norm.

Unluckily, golemborn can count themselves among those outcasts for no other reason than a quirk of their birth. Through some magical twist of fate, magical accident, pure-hearted wish, or eccentric experiment, these individuals arose from powerful constructs that merged with their animating spirits and came to life, becoming an amalgam of living flesh and construct. In my mind, this is a true and natural birth, no less beautiful or natural than the process performed by other ancestries; if anything, there’s something even more spiritual and personal about such a miracle. Yet golemborn are often persecuted or misunderstood. For some reason, many people find it difficult to relate to someone who was once an automaton of iron, or a statue made of clay. Why do they treat golemborn so differently? Is it because of the animus from the idea that they were once a mindless construct and an animating force, and so thus less worthy of respect for the being they have become? But most ancestries were formed when a mindless egg merged with the right fertilizer. How is that so different?

There’s no reason to treat a golemborn differently than any other ancestry. We should accept them as miracles, rather than demonize them as an aberration of existence. But it’s far too easy for us to reject others due to perceived differences. Only when we can overcome this prejudice can we understand that the true measure of a “person” is in our empathy, our ability to understand those of differing perspectives. If we can’t do that, then it isn’t golemborn who are truly “inhuman.”

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Golemborn Ancestry.name: 魔像裔族裔 Golemborn Ancestry
Golemborn Ancestry.text:
Sometimes, when a skilled magical crafter constructs a mindless golem, its frame attracts soul energy and merges with the golem’s animating spirit to become a fully living being known as a golemborn. Alternatively, a golemborn might originate when a sick or dying person’s mind is grafted into a usually unliving construct, forming a fusion of animate and inanimate. A golemborn might be mistaken for a regular golem at first glance, especially when looking at the inanimate portions of their body, though those assumptions are quickly dispelled when that golemborn exhibits emotions and rational thought unknown to a simple construct.

Golemborn aren’t born as much as they are made. Ancient magical rites that produce normal golems are somewhat uncommon, but rarer still are the accidents and experimental techniques that create living, thinking beings out of inanimate material like clay and stone. Golemborn come into the world fully formed, their tall and sometimes rough-hewn frames standing head and shoulders above the average human. Some golemborn see their creation as the wonder that it is, while others are made to believe they are abominations. In either case, whether a golemborn is accepted by society or shunned as an outcast depends on the nature of their creation and the views of their neighbors.

A golemborn might use their sturdy body to fight against the evils of the world after being subjected to prejudice or delve into arcane secrets to strive to create more of their kind, ensuring they will be treated with acceptance. Other golemborn seek out ways to enhance their bodies and become more like their golem “cousins,” able to shrug off most damage and thwart those who would use magic against them.

If you want to play a character who has a manufactured body and a strong connection to magic that sometimes allows them to stand against it, you should play a golemborn.

You Might...

  • Slip unthinking into repeated patterns of behavior.
  • Search for the one who made you, to exact revenge or find a family.
  • Wear veils and heavy clothing to hide your composition.

Others Probably...

  • Wonder, often out loud, about your provenance.
  • Fear the moments in which you go berserk.
  • Appreciate your ability to take a punch or shrug off magic.

How Much is Golem?

Golemborn exist on a spectrum from those whose bodies incorporate many features of other ancestries to those who look mostly the same as golems, except that they are living creatures with functioning lifeforces and biologies within their mechanical outer facades. The artwork in this ancestry is focused mainly on the hybrid golemborn, as the others look much like golems do.

Physical Description

Though their full appearance depends on the material from which they have been crafted, golemborn tend to have vaguely humanoid shapes: two arms, two legs, and a head. Some golemborn are carved with extreme detail to closely resemble another humanoid ancestry and even have parts of their body that have transformed into living flesh… or at least the appearance of living flesh. However, due to their size and composition, even these golemborn are almost never mistaken for their mimicked ancestries.

Other golemborn might have only the vaguest of features, with their facial features nothing more than indentations in their faces, and are clearly creatures of construction. Such golemborn may find it difficult to integrate into some societies, as their seeming expressionless visages might be considered off-putting. A golemborn with an especially constructed appearance and few (or no) visible sections of flesh might pose as a simple, mindless construct in places where golemborn are shunned to avoid needless persecution. Fortunately for golemborn, this is becoming less necessary as more and more areas are growing to understand the many varied ways that life presents itself.

While golemborn run the full gamut of how much of their body appears as flesh, golemborn adventurers often tend toward appearing more as flesh and blood than stone and sand. Perhaps because the unique blend of magic present in a golemborn, which appears as more of a hybrid of humanoid and construct, catalyzes a golemborn adventurer’s growth. That said, golemborn appearing almost entirely the same as “traditional” constructed golems sometimes become adventurers as well.

Golemborn can be comprised of nearly any material that magical crafters use to make golems, with clay, iron, and stone being the most common. Clay and stone golemborn often resemble animated statues with sections of living flesh, though clay golemborn sometimes have a fired, ceramic glaze that gives them a slightly shinier skin than their stony cousins. In addition, a clay golemborn might resemble a deity or other religious figure, while a stone golemborn are more likely to take the form of a soldier or historical figure. Because of the greater difficulty and cost in working the material, iron golemborn are less likely to have incredibly well-defined features, though master smiths can shape metal in the most extraordinary ways. On the other hand, iron golemborn are the sturdiest heritage and can usually be repaired by banging out their dents, while other golemborn might require infusions of new material.

Ice and wood golemborn are considered to be the most primal heritages and often look the least humanoid in their golem parts. While ice and wood can be carved into facsimiles of other ancestries, sculptors who work with those materials are just as likely to incorporate their natural shapes. Ice golemborn might have long, icicle-like limbs or stout iceberg-shaped bodies. Wood golemborn may resemble walking trees with mossy beards and birds’ nests for hair.

Though flesh golemborn are technically made from the same stuff as what most people have throughout their own bodies, they are the most shunned heritage. Most of the living find flesh golemborn too reminiscent of undead creatures (and often mistake the former for the latter) when they see the golemborn’s stitched-together combinations of different creatures’ skin and can’t get past their checkboard look. Although they might resemble undead to the untrained eye, flesh golemborn are created using specific chemical treatments and magic that prevent their flesh from rotting, although a nasty wound might cause them to lose a limb temporarily. Another unfortunate side-effect of flesh golemborn comes from the material used when creating their bodies. Some crafters sometimes assemble their creations from recently deceased locals, such as their faces or other specific body parts, leading to further awkwardness within the community. Usually, such a golemborn has no memories of that person’s life and opts for a kind of self-exile to avoid causing emotional distress.

As golemborn age and become more accepting of their ever-changing bodies, older golemborn often wear their battle scars and traces of repairs proudly. Some consider it a rite of passage to have a limb or large section of their torso replaced with newer materials. Most adventuring golemborn have to do this themselves or rely on a trusted companion; they are rarely repaired by their original creator unless that creator is a fellow adventurer. While a few bandages or a healing spell will also repair a golemborn’s wounds, many village doctors and priests tend to balk at treating a golemborn, likely out of fear or confusion. This usually means a golemborn will have to make do with whatever is on hand, leading to sometimes patchy fixes. However, it doesn’t take much to get most golemborn to tell the stories behind each chip or dent on their ever-changing bodies.

Rarely is a golemborn the result of using experimental surgical and magical techniques to save a dying person’s life or to bring them back from death. Unlike other golemborn, who run the gamut in their appearance, these golemborn always feature large swaths of their heritage’s material merged with living flesh. Perhaps one leg is made of clay, while the other is flesh and bone. Flesh golemborn created in this fashion are difficult to distinguish from completely fabricated flesh golemborn, though the latter may have more obvious patches of once-necrotic skin. The material still usually makes up more than half of the golemborn’s form; having only a single iron prosthetic hand doesn’t make someone a golemborn. In addition, more often than not, the process alters the previous person’s mind and soul, with the new golemborn not remembering anything of their previous life. This can lead to heartbreak or anger on the part of the golemborn’s creator, who might have expected to have been reunited with a loved one.

Golem Manuals

Regular golems are often constructed through the use of complicated, arcane formulae and detailed construction designs known as golem manuals. Sometimes, a spellcaster following one of these books makes an unforeseen error that results in the creation of a golemborn. These “mistakes” aren’t usually replicable, no matter how detailed the notes the creator takes in the manual’s margins about their process. Some golemborn keep these tomes with them, adding their own observations and thoughts, sometimes even binding more pages onto the spine. These personalized golem manuals become more akin to diaries than instructions as the golemborn owner works through their feelings about their creation.

Society

As rare as they are, golemborn don’t often form their own societies and are sometimes forced to live outside of civilization. Some dwell in remote caves, while others find crumbling ruins to inhabit, fearing that someday they might have to protect themselves against hostile interlopers who see them as a monster. Some wander across the countryside from village to village, resting outdoors and giving rise to local myths and urban legends. Lucky golemborn find a home, usually within a small community or group of adventurers, where they are accepted for who they are, regardless of their origin. Some of these individuals make it their life’s work to seek out others of their kind to take in or to start a family by creating a golemborn descendant of their own.

If there is one driving force common to most golemborn, it’s the desire to understand the mysteries of their creation. Some seek this knowledge out of sheer curiosity, others believe it will help them to fit in better, and a few simply want to replicate the process. These golemborn believe that if they can grasp the reasons behind their being, they can finally be at peace with themselves. Of course, this existential longing isn’t unique to golemborn, and when they meet others going through the same thing, they are quick to commiserate. This can often lead to a lasting bond and a promise to work together in their respective metaphysical quests.

Golemborn communities, where they exist, are small. They often consist of fewer than a dozen individuals, sometimes of the same heritage and sometimes not. Once they find one another, these golemborn settle in a remote location, far from prying eyes. Residents of a golemborn community are fiercely loyal to each other and quickly rally to defend their homes and way of life. However, while they are wary of outsiders, they aren’t immediately hostile, as they understand the pain of being forsaken by civilization. A visitor must usually provide a good reason for their presence, without which they are politely asked to leave.

Golemborn villages are recognizable by their larger-than-usual homes, built for most golemborn’s tall statures. These dwellings have only one story, as it is inconvenient to construct stairs and ceilings that can bear the weight of heavier golemborn made of iron or stone. Central to these communities are arcane libraries that collect tomes about golem construction and workshops that act as hospital-forges for golemborn with damaged or worn bodies. In times of danger, the golemborn of the community also use these buildings for protection.

Molds

In addition to heritages, golemborns can usually be considered part of one of three general molds, depending on their outlook on their origins and their thoughts about their future.

Architects

Architects pride themselves on building communities for the future of golembornkind. Whether those settlements are populated solely of golemborn or integrate several ancestries, golemborn architects usually ensure that the community is open and welcoming to all. Those who have been ostracized by the rest of civilization are especially welcome in such places, though those who are on the run from the law after committing heinous crimes are not.

Golemborn architects often begin their work by finding a suitable location for a settlement, sometimes one that is a bit removed from its neighbors. An area that is near enough to natural resources and fertile land allows it to become self-sufficient over time, reducing the need for merchants and other travelers to interrupt the residents’ solitude. These communities aren’t necessarily hostile to outsiders, but they tend to prefer to be left alone. Many citizens have troubled pasts and have difficulty trusting others. This presents a problem for the architect who is looking to populate their new settlement, and it sometimes takes weeks of negotiations with each potential resident to convince them to move in. Architects are nothing if not persistent, however, and they usually have superior powers of persuasion on top of their organizational skills.

Some golemborn architects stumble across an already-established settlement that is experiencing hardships of its own and pitch in to help solve the problem. This usually garners them the residents’ trust, allowing them to mold the community into something they can all eventually be proud of. Occasionally, some residents grow resentful of this former outsider bringing in other outcasts; these individuals usually either change their minds after a personal incident or leave the settlement to live alongside those who share their views.

A golemborn architect might not seek a position of authority within their community, but those who do usually have the unwavering support of their neighbors and serve with humility and effectiveness. Those who don’t hold such positions are still considered trusted advisors and community leaders. Golemborn architects keep the best interests of the people in the forefront of their minds whether they have power or not. They know all too well how such power can be abused.

Unusual Materials

Occasionally, golemborn emerge formed of similar but different materials than precisely the ones listed in the heritages. In those cases, the easiest way to build such a golemborn is to choose the most similar heritage among the existing heritages and adjust the physical description of the golemborn accordingly. For instance, a particular stone golem might be formed out of a geode, with chunks of colorful crystal emerging from the stone, or from an unusual type of stone with a distinctive appearance, such as obsidian. On the other hand, an iron golemborn might be made of a different sort of metal than iron, such as bronze or tin. These aesthetics never provide mechanical benefits or precious materials, though at the GM’s discretion, a golemborn-based character could use a 5th-level ancestry feat to have their fist unarmed attacks be silver, or a 13th-level feat for adamantine.

Progenitors

Those golemborn who spend their time and energy duplicating the process that created them are known as progenitors. They seek magical and engineering knowledge to craft more of their kind, often in an attempt to form a family that they would otherwise be lacking. Many consider this no different than a biological entity’s drive to reproduce, though a golemborn’s creation requires more skill, care, and luck. Some golemborn progenitors don’t necessarily think what they are doing is akin to bringing a child into the world; they merely want to better understand their own creation by replicating the process.

Unfortunately, not all progenitors are successful. A few fail to create any sort of animate construct, fruitlessly sculpting away at stone, clay, or wood to create humanoid statues but never instilling them with the necessary magic. Most of these golemborn give up after a few attempts to focus their efforts on artistic expression, and they typically excel at it. Many other progenitors are able to fashion working golems that sadly don’t attract the stray souls that would turn them into golemborn. The procedure’s cost in time and gold is prohibitive enough that these progenitor golemborn create only two or three golems before running out of funds. A scant handful of golemborn are actually able to become a “parent,” though there are no clear reasons why they are successful when others are not.

Some failed progenitors believe that the process of their creation is truly random, while others think that the act of trying to create a golemborn actively drives away the required soul. According to these golemborn, those who have succeeded are lying about their original intentions and were simply lucky. Some progenitors, whether successful or not, become researchers later in life, attempting to learn more about why they achieved the results they did.

Researchers

Golemborn who seek to uncover knowledge about others of their own ancestry and discover the true reasons behind the origin of their existence are called researchers. Researchers often visit a multitude of libraries for their research, searching for any tomes that describe the process of golem manufacture and the history of other constructed beings. Once they exhaust the lines of inquiry possible in their local settlements, they begin to travel the world, questing for hidden depositories of knowledge and sellers of rare books. They go to great lengths to acquire the information they seek, which can sometimes lead them into dangerous situations.

Even golemborn researchers have yet to truly understand the nature of their own ancestry, but that doesn’t stop them from looking further and deeper. It is said that perhaps a golemborn who does unravel this mystery undergoes an immediate apotheosis into another humanoid ancestry, forgetting everything about their previous life and beginning anew. In this way, the knowledge remains secret, which some believe is the design of the god or other powerful entity responsible for the creation of golemborn. However, many think this theory is nothing more than a cautionary tale to deter golemborn from investing so heavily in their research that they forget to engage with the world around them.

Memorial Statues

When a golemborn takes their last step, becoming akin to a statue and memorial to their life and accomplishments, what holds them together and prevents decay is the lingering magic which once animated their body. This is independent of the golemborn’s lifeforce, and so a golemborn might very well die with a great deal of magic still swirling around them. Effectively, what this means is that the longevity of the golemborn’s statue form depends on the strength of that magic, which is related to their power as a golemborn but could also be influenced by whether they died of natural causes or went all-out in a battle, expending all their magical energy as they did so. While the magic lasts, the golemborn doesn’t decay or erode, but when it’s gone, they rapidly decay and crumble to base materials.

A Golemborn’s Lifespan

Golemborn don’t age in the same sense as most other living ancestries do. They are mortal, certainly, but their bodies don’t naturally grow larger over time. A newborn golemborn is essentially the same size as one near the end of their lifespan, and—except in extreme cases—a golemborn’s features don’t change with the passage of time. This often leads many to believe that golemborn live forever until they are destroyed, much like a golem or other construct can function indefinitely. However, this is not the case. While they have longer lifespans than humans, golemborn do eventually die. After about two centuries, the soul and lifeforce that animate a golemborn lose their connection to the golemborn’s physical body. The golemborn perishes, and their form becomes inert. Their bodies usually revert back to their physical underlying components without the life force powering them, leaving their body like a statue or living monument to themselves, while other times, their bodies simply crumble to pieces or dissolve to dust.

Many golemborn believe that the knowledge of their own mortality will come to them days, sometimes even weeks, before the actual event occurs. Their predictions aren’t exact, but they often come close enough to allow the golemborn a chance to tie up any loose ends and set their affairs in order beforehand. Skeptics aware of this tradition claim that golemborn have a fixed lifespan, so the timing of their end is something that can be easily calculated by anyone with access to the right variables and equations. Some older golemborn insist that it’s more than finding the answer to a math problem; they explain it as a gentle and gradual quieting of a noise inside their heads they never realized they were hearing. They know that once the sound stops, their time is up. Depending on their place of origin, golemborn have many different names for this sensation: the slow silence, the peace at the end, and the final stillness are just a few examples.

It is considered polite to leave a golemborn’s “corpse” wherever it died, and those golemborn who achieved a high status in life are sometimes honored by their neighbors and their descendants, who treat their body as a memorial statue. Sometimes, a plaque or other signifier with the golemborn’s name is attached or erected nearby. Of course, some dead golemborn might rot or melt away over time once the magic holding them together has dissipated; these golemborn are usually buried or allowed to simply vanish. Though some might find it morbid, it isn’t unheard of for a golemborn to deconstruct a dead golemborn of the same material and use that material to repair or upgrade their own body. This is usually done in only the most dire of circumstances and with the utmost respect, with the belief that they might carry on the dead golemborn’s legend and legacy. The still-living golemborn often offers a small prayer of thanks to the departed soul or builds some kind of marker where the dead golemborn stood.

Large Player Characters

Many golemborn are Large, which means GMs must take certain considerations into account when designing adventures that include them. The following are reminders about Large characters: Golemborn can go most places Medium characters can go, though they take up more room: 2 squares long by 2 squares wide and 2 squares high. Though a Large ancestry doesn’t necessarily have a longer reach, their height still allows them to Strike a creature that is 10 feet above the ground (usually a flying creature), since their top squares are adjacent. A Large PC provides cover (not lesser cover) to Small and smaller creatures, and when moving through an area that is less than 5 feet wide must treat each square as difficult terrain. Finally, a Large character need not pay more for equipment that is sized for their frames.

The Seat Of Emotions And Thought

Unlike normal constructs, many of which are mindless or built to serve a specific purpose or creator, golemborn think and feel for themselves. These emotions are considered to come from the soul and life force their body drew in during their creation and not anything purely physical within their body. This is one of the many mysteries surrounding a golemborn’s life. How do they think when they might not even have a brain? How do they feel when they might not even have a heart?

Many golemborn believe that their animating spirit is what gives them all the aspects of a true person. Much like how a leshy is a being of pure primal life essence given form through plant matter, a golemborn’s identity arises from the magic that animates them. And that power can sometimes shape a golemborn’s personality, giving them traits that are often associated with the material they are made of. In a way, the entirety of their body becomes the source of their thoughts and emotions.

A clay golemborn shaped in the image of a deity or a deity’s herald could find themselves drawn to the worship of that god or, alternatively, driven away from it. Another clay golemborn, tempered by fire, might burn with a particular passion and fixate on a singular project. A third clay golemborn might have awoken alone, first seeing a passage from a holy text inscribed upon their body and treating those words as sacrosanct, basing their entire worldview upon it.

Being built from the corpses of other living creatures, a flesh golemborn might harbor an unhealthy fascination with death. Another flesh golemborn who was animated by lightning could exhibit a similarly volatile personality, suddenly lashing out in a destructive rage without much warning. A third flesh golemborn might take to the healing arts to study whatever maladies took the lives of their component bodies.

The frigidness of an ice golemborn’s body might infect their heart, turning them callous and unfeeling toward the plights of others around them. Another ice golemborn might derive wonder from the fact that their form is incredibly shapeable and strive to turn themselves into a work of art. Yet another ice golemborn could decide to dwell only in shadow, fearing the warmth of the sun.

An unbending iron golemborn could be considered stubborn and set in their ways. Another iron golemborn might have designs etched onto their plates with acid; these patterns might be a mystery to the golemborn, and the discovery of their meaning might drive them to study the occult. A third iron golemborn might consider it their duty to protect others, throwing their metal parts in harm’s way because they’re sturdier and more easily repaired than mere flesh.

A stone golemborn’s voice might have a deep reverberating tenor, like the sound of rocks grinding together, lending the golemborn’s words a gravitas that commands respect. Another stone golemborn might be slow to act on a decision due to a mental inertia from pondering every possible outcome. Inspired by the veins of ore and deposits of gemstones found in rock, yet another stone golemborn could adorn their statuesque bodies with shiny metals and sparkling jewels and judge others on their own attire.

A towering wood golemborn could take their composition seriously, appointing themselves a steward of nature and punishing all who defile it. Dismayed that the wood of their body can no longer grow, another wood golemborn might cultivate flowers, vines, and moss to take root within their form to feel more like a living, changing being. A third wood golemborn might smooth down the rough edges of their exterior, varnish the wooden portions of their torso and face, and don flowing robes to hide their true nature for fear of being misunderstood.

In essence, a golemborn’s mind and soul are truly no different than those of any other living, sapient creature. They are shaped by their personal experiences, though most golemborn’s histories are fraught with calamity and trauma that could lead them to have extreme personalities—either cold and withdrawn or full of endless rage. Of course, many golemborn fight back against such stereotypes and cultivate a manner that helps those around them feel more comfortable. Others argue with good reason that they should be accepted as who they are, regardless of their origins.

Beliefs

Many believe golemborn have no strong feelings about morality and ethics, but like anyone else, a golemborn’s personal outlook is heavily influenced by their individual circumstances. A golemborn who was raised as part of a family—perhaps as an adopted replacement for a lost child—might exhibit compassion and empathy as cornerstones of their personality, while a golemborn who has been shunned by society might become wicked and cruel, wanting no more than to avenge every slight. A few golemborn who see all sides of a society, from its best to its worst, opt out of such dichotomies and decide to look out only for themselves. They aren’t always completely unmoved by others’ plights and might decide to help those in need if the situation warrants it.

Those golemborn who seek a higher power usually venerate deities associated with magic and crafting. Most religious myths tell of a deity who formed one ancestry or another out of clay, dirt, or a similar substance, and some golemborn see their own creation as an echo of that divine act in the modern world. Many of these golemborn seek out divine knowledge that will aid them in creating more of their own kind, while others search for those who have already been created and help them find their own places in the world. Evil golemborn might even consider themselves divine and form cults of personality to manipulate fellow golemborn with weaker wills.

Popular Edicts seek to understand your nature and the meaning of your existence, create so that something of yours will be left behind, come to terms with yourself

Popular Anathemas destroy wantonly that which you do not understand, reject fellow golemborn or those forsaken by civilization, stick your head in the sand to avoid the deeper questions

Names

A golemborn might be given a name by their creator, which depends on the creator’s preferences but might often arise either from a holy text or from the creator’s personal family tree. Those golemborn who were rejected by their creators or who never knew them must find their own names, sometimes inspired by their physical features or their supposed function. A few wander namelessly for a time and are eventually named by those they travel with, usually for their companions’ benefit. In short, golemborn names range in history and form.

Sample Names

Azareal, Clayfoot, Elshaih, Guardian, Jania, Keeper, Rimesoul, Steelheart, Tallow.

Golemborn Adventurers

Golemborn, who are looking for ways to better understand their nature, often easily fall in with a group of adventurers. They might be standoffish at first but going through shared adversity helps them to open up and share their feelings. Most golemborn can withstand a lot of punishment and even weather magical effects, so they tend to fill the more combat-oriented roles in a party—barbarians, champions, fighters, and monks are common. A golemborn highly affected by or interested in exploring the magic of their creation might become sorcerers, witches, and wizards. Apart from the occasional ice or wood golemborn, they don’t often gravitate toward more nature-oriented careers.

Golemborn Heritages

Your golemborn aspects are determined by the material that was used to construct you. Choose one of the following golemborn heritages at 1st level.
