Swarmblood Ancestry.name: 虫群血裔族裔 Swarmblood Ancestry
Swarmblood Ancestry.text:
The uncanny feeling of movement pulsing beneath your skin, the knowledge that anything could wriggle inside your skull while you sleep, the fear of a parasite making your body its home, stealing your vitality for itself. Swarmbloods are an unenvied few, humanoids whose bodies belong not only to themselves but who share their skins with an infestation with a thousand minds of their own.

Linked as intrinsically to the cycles of the moon as they are to the strange, symbiotic creatures that live inside their bodies, a swarmblood is most accurately described as multiple creatures who live in a state of harmony: a humanoid host body and an unusual symbiotic swarm, called a “brood,” that lives inside that body, carrying out many processes and functions for the composite swarmblood that would normally be the role of various organs and fluids. While the brood often resembles a swarm of small animals like rats, crows, or bees, these creatures are a single collective bonded to each other and to their host by a weak telepathic net. Broods can’t survive independently; just as they serve a vital function in the host’s body, so too do they require the host in order to live. Once a swarmblood is born, the bond normally can’t be severed, and attempting to do so would, at best, kill both the host and the brood.

New swarmbloods are usually born when a swarmblood brood branches off into a new host. This donation weakens the swarmblood slightly for a time, so it normally happens to save the life of the new swarmblood’s host due to organ failure or a similar malady that inheriting the brood can fix. There are also tales of the majority of a brood passing on from a dying host into a new willing host, causing the previous host and the remaining brood to die but some of their memories and legacy to live on in the new host. Both of these processes involve a large number of members of the brood, occur fairly quickly, and require a willing participant. Swarmbloods usually can’t reproduce with other ancestries, but they can sometimes have children with other swarmbloods, regardless of the type of humanoids their outer bodies appear to be, through a process that involves a mixing of their two broods which can produce a child with a brood that look like chimeras between the two animals in the parents’ broods.

While these three methods are fairly benign, rumors abound of another possibility that some powerful swarmbloods with particularly sturdy broods can manage. Such a swarmblood, the legends say, could break off a single member of their brood from the collective. Then, that single member would crawl inside another humanoid, wriggling beneath the skin and laying numerous small, jelly-like eggs and quickly returning to their host body before the separation kills them. After that initial deposit, the legends say that over the following month, the brood’s eggs begin to hatch under the light of the changing moon. As the brood grows, they slowly consume their host’s organs, making space inside the body to continue to grow and reproduce… all while the host is still alive. After a full lunar cycle, the infestation is complete: host and brood united as both many and one by their swarmblood state. While some believe this rumor is unreliable, especially the connection to the moon, which could be simply based on distorted stories of werecreatures, many swarmbloods attest to the sense of peace and solace their brood feels when they are bathed in moonlight.

Whether performed quickly to save a life or slowly by a powerful swarmblood infesting another with eggs, the transformation into a swarmblood is painful and often frightening, and the process of choosing a new host for the brood requires great prudence and care. While the curse could heal those with weak or dying organs, a host with a frail enough constitution might not survive the change; the stress on their body could kill them during the process, especially the slower month-long infestation, which requires time for the brood to mature. Understanding both the advantages and drawbacks of living with an infested brood, most swarmbloods value consent above all else in the process, which is part of why unwilling infestations remain a legend. Even those who choose this path voluntarily might not be prepared for the pain of the transformation or the unnerving changes it brings about.

If you want to play a character with a horrifying secret hiding under their skin, who has a swarm of tiny creatures inside of them and at their command, or to explore the trauma of undergoing a frightening transformation and suddenly sharing a body with creatures who have never known another home, you should play a swarmblood.

You Might...

  • Think of yourself and your brood as a collective (we) rather than an individual (I).
  • Identify any group of people you regularly associate with as a community with collective interests, seeking out actions and solutions that maximize the benefit to the whole group rather than the individual.
  • Focus on keeping your swarm fully under your skin and out of sight when dealing with others who don’t understand you.

Others Probably...

  • Avoid your company, finding you or your presence creepy and discomforting.
  • Misunderstand the connection between you and your swarm if they are even aware of it.
  • Worry you might be a zombie or other type of undead creature or a parasite likely to infect them.

Swarmbloods and Body Horror

The swarmblood ancestry has some descriptions that could be disturbing or nauseating for some players, so talk to the other players in your group about playing a swarmblood and you can potentially tone down some of the visual descriptions or change which creatures constitute your brood if spiders are a problem but mice aren’t.

Physical Description

Swarmbloods can be difficult to tell apart from other humanoids of the same type as the host. Excepting those times when a few members of the brood make themselves known by leaving the host body to crawl across their face or scuttle along the ground, the infestation leaves few physical tells. Some swarmbloods might have strange, hard lumps under their skin that wander across their bodies, or others might find that the fine details of their features, like the angle of their cheekbones or lengths of their fingers, can sometimes shift from one day to the next. In extreme situations, typically involving a swarmblood who suffered from major external wounds or burns before their infestation, portions of a swarmblood’s outer body might be composed of specialized members of the brood that appear, while lying still, to be normal pieces of flesh, scales, feather, or whatever their host’s outer body normally looks like. In those cases, there are always thin telltale lines in their flesh where the brood members connect with each other, and those portions of the swarmblood’s outer body can detach like any other brood members, leaving behind a hole revealing more of the brood crawling underneath.

Most swarmbloods are comfortable around animals that humanoid societies have deemed macabre, like spiders, insects, rodents, snakes, and bats. Their condition also leaves many of them accustomed to the feeling of something constantly crawling under and over their skin, and they are rarely frightened or disgusted by branches that suddenly brush across their arms or unexpected creatures making their way up their legs. Still, some never get used to the feeling of their brood’s constant motion, forever unable to find peace or stillness within their bodies. These swarmbloods might move their bodies in strange and sudden tics, struggling for a sense of comfort that never comes.

Swarmbloods who live among other humanoids can develop reputations for being creepy or dirty, though it can be challenging for the swarmblood’s neighbors to articulate exactly what they find off-putting. Many have only vague and half-forgotten recollections of the time they spotted a cockroach crawling up a pant leg or a wriggling creature inside a stranger’s ear. These little odd moments are easy to rationalize away as a mistaken puff of lint or loose hair when someone thinks about them too hard, but they nonetheless leave behind an uncomfortable feeling that something must be wrong with the swarmblood.

Society

Swarmbloods are a diverse group originating from many other ancestries and a wide variety of different backgrounds. They are united only by the symbiotic brood they carry with them. There is no single society shared by all swarmbloods; despite the changes wrought by the infestation, most simply carry on living something akin to their old lives, remaining in their homes and continuing to adhere to the societal norms of the civilization into which they were born. For many, these community and societal connections are even dearer to their hearts than before they became a swarmblood as they become more in tune with the community around them. Many swarmbloods quickly realize that the way they support and are supported by their local communities is a parallel to their brood and host body on a larger scale. It’s common for the transformation to shift a swarmblood’s perspective to favor collectivism, though these changes are usually minor.

Though less common, some swarmbloods uproot their lives entirely following their transformation, moving away from other ancestries to live among other swarmbloods of similar broods. Others might move on from their old lives for other reasons, perhaps because they feel disconnected from their families or after being rejected by a community that discovered their transformation.

Newly infested swarmbloods can’t always cope with the changes, even if they understood theoretically what the process would entail. Even after making the decision, it’s not unheard of for a swarmblood to attempt to reverse the process. These new swarmbloods often must accept their brood by themselves before they feel ready to return to their swarmblood progenitor and learn from them, though not all accept their situation. Some never return.

On the other hand, the rare few swarmbloods who are born to two other swarmbloods typically have the fewest problems adapting, as they’ve had the mixed brood as part of them since before they were even born. They tend to have the strongest tendency to identify with their brood of any swarmbloods.

Hives

While swarmbloods rarely build societies together with other swarmbloods, they do share several schools of thought, which swarmbloods have come to call hives. Depending on who you ask, the term “hive” might be short for hivemind, with the idea that they aren’t physical hives but rather collectives of swarmbloods who think more alike on various philosophical topics. The following are the most common hives among swarmbloods.

The Covenant

For most swarmbloods, passing a brood on to another person and creating more swarmblood (or choosing never to do so, even to save a life) is among the most meaningful and serious choices of their lives. Outside of the rare conception between two swarmbloods, finding a new host is usually the only way for a brood to reproduce. The covenant are a hive of swarmbloods who take the relationship between those who share a common brood as a familial bond between them all.

To a coven seeker, their coven is a swarmblood family, a tightly connected network of infested all created from and connected by a shared, single brood that proliferated across each one of their bodies. Members of a coven are all siblings to each other, though elders are acknowledged as such and treated with respect by those newly infested. Among the covenant, there is a strong belief that when a new swarmblood is born, it is their progenitor’s duty to teach the new younger sibling about the transformation and their brood before ultimately presenting them before the coven so they can know their family and appreciate the totality of their brood.

Most covens live together in tightly knit communities, whether they are within swarmblood enclaves of larger cities or in small, secluded villages founded by the coven where they can live quietly and in peace. From the coven’s perspective, their way of life represents the true core of swarmblood society, and those who choose to live outside their norms are a minority group of rebels and outcasts. Members of the covenant maintain that all swarmbloods become members of their brood’s coven when they receive the gift of the brood, even if they aren’t aware of that coven’s existence or never meet their siblings. They take the fact that swarmbloods with a common brood can sometimes feel a minor symbiotic connection with each other across their bodies as proof of their claim. However, this position causes great frustration for those who reject the idea of covens, especially the scattered.

Swarmblood Adventurers

Swarmbloods can come from all walks of life before their curse, but many only feel the call to adventure after they become swarmbloods. Those with the animal whisperer background might feel a stronger connection to the creatures of their swarm, while shattered swarmbloods who leave their communities after their infestation might have the nomad or hermit background. Because those with the sturdiest fortitude fare best in their transformation, many swarmbloods have a high Constitution score and choose classes that benefit accordingly, such as kineticist, barbarian, or champion.

The Hidden

The hidden are the largest hive, representing a plurality of swarmbloods, and possibly even a majority. Hidden simply continue going about their old lives, remaining in their same town and hiding their brood as best they can, or staying with the same adventuring party if they were infested over the course of their adventures. Despite the difficulties of concealing the curse from all but a trusted few, many swarmbloods are able to maintain this unsteady equilibrium for years, or even their entire lives.

Because most laypeople don’t know that swarmbloods exist, concealing their transformation is often easier than most new swarmbloods expect it to be. It’s rare for anyone without an extensive knowledge of unusual magical physiologies to connect a sudden increase in the local pigeon population to someone within the community who recovered from organ failure, and even if they did, it would seem implausible to others. However, the longer a swarmblood remains in one place, the greater the risk of discovery becomes. All but the most careful of hidden, along with those who manage to become accepted for who they are, are eventually exposed. This usually all but forces them to move and start a new life every few years, and this need for a transient life makes it challenging to form satisfying connections in their communities. Of course, this is all the more frustrating given their increased affinity toward such societal connections.

The Scattered

To the scattered, the swarmblood transformation is a curse. It’s common at first for new swarmbloods, even though they rationally accepted the transformation, to find that part of them rejects the melding with their other selves within the brood. These swarmbloods flee from their progenitor for a time, and while many ultimately return seeking guidance and support, not all do.

Those who come to despise their swarmblood state are part of the hive known as the scattered. Needless to say, compared to those who voluntarily became swarmbloods, or those who were infested involuntarily, are especially likely to follow this hive’s philosophy. On the other hand, the rare swarmbloods born of two other swarmbloods are almost never scattered.

The scattered are openly antagonistic to the covenant and any covens they come across, and they loudly proclaim their transformation as a terrible curse to any who they trust and know their secret. It’s common for the scattered to feel afraid, confused, and alone, and to blame their progenitor swarmblood for the pain of their transformation. That doesn’t mean that most scattered seek to rile up angry mobs to wipe out other swarmbloods. While they might have antipathy or resentment for their progenitor, they more often see covenant swarmbloods as confused victims in desperate need of the guidance and help that they stubbornly refuse to accept. Meanwhile, since most swarmbloods who initially recoil at their state eventually return to their progenitors for guidance, covenant swarmbloods often think of scattered as “simply going through a phase.” Even though neither means to be actively antagonistic, both hives are oversimplifying the others’ lifestyles, and it’s a common cause of friction between swarmbloods and occasionally even open conflict.

Other scattered are simply angry. Ultimately, they are often truly angry with themselves for choosing to agree to the transformation, but they often deflect that anger outward as a self-preservation mechanism, justifying their actions by rationalizing that their progenitor undersold the pain of the transformation and the unease of their new life while overselling the benefits, misleading them into making a huge mistake. Some remain outraged for the rest of their lives, and this outrage defines their relationship both to their own brood and to all other swarmbloods. Those who despise their swarmblood nature the most posit a different reason for the name of their hive, a secret meaning that they consider to be the true one: they are scattered not because they are foolish lost souls but because they will someday find a “cure” for the swarmblood curse, scattering the broods of all swarmbloods, and returning sovereignty over their bodies. They believe any swarmblood who opposes their cure to be brainwashed and are determined to provide it to all, whether they want the cure or not. Unfortunately for these scattered, they have yet to make any breakthroughs on a cure, but as with most things magical, all it takes is one genius mage or lucky experiment to change the world.

The Seekers

Sometimes, new swarmbloods feel that the transformation has changed them too much to remain in their old lives, reject the familial lifestyle of the covenant, and resentment of the scattered. They instead feel sheer wonder of becoming swarmblood and wish to seek their own path through the world. These rare few call themselves the seekers.

Seekers are ever curious and willing to experiment to help them find the place that feels right for them. As such, they often become wanderers or pursue an adventurer’s life. Others might move to a remote hermitage for a time where they can live in solitude with their brood without fearing the day they are discovered by their neighbors, giving them a chance to quietly reflect on a journey of self-discovery until they’re ready to move to the next phase of their life. Sometimes, a seeker finds what they were searching for, either on the road or within themself, allowing them to put down roots. Other seekers consider this a joyous occasion and the culmination of the seeker’s path. They sometimes call those who have succeeded “the found” to indicate this momentous milestone, but they use this as a term of respect, and not as a way to isolate these seekers by elevating them. To other seekers, the found are ultimately still seekers, and they might later discover that there’s another stage to their journey they have yet to begin.

Beliefs

Swarmbloods come from many backgrounds and consequently have many perspectives on life, and nowhere is this more exemplified than in the diversity of attitudes toward reproduction. For instance, swarmbloods of the covenant believe that finding new hosts willing to become swarmbloods is necessary for their collective survival, something both good and imperative for them to do. On the other hand, the scattered more often resent receiving the transformation and believe convincing another to accept the infestation to be unethical, even if it would save their life. Other swarmblood hives often fall somewhere in the middle, perhaps longing for the connection of family or feeling a deep connection to the brood’s desire to create a legacy while avoiding an active search for new hosts either to help remain hidden or because they prioritize seeking out their own path.

Swarmbloods are usually no more or less religious than other humanoids of the same ancestry as the host, though their slight tendency to collectivism also lends itself to a collective religious faith. Though the deities worshipped by swrambloods are diverse and often influenced by their homelands and backgrounds, some faiths do attract more swarmblood followers than others, and those who become devout only after their infestations gravitate toward those deities. The worship of deities associated with the moon, such as Talir, the dragon deity imprisoned in Alacar’s moon, help swarmbloods to connect with the primal or mystical nature of their being. These lunar swarmbloods often build kinships with lycanthropes among these faiths out of their shared experiences of being transformed into something more monstrous. Deities of community, civilization, and cities, like Tovah the Father or Castili the Marquis of Civility on Alacar, are also popular. These faiths recontextualize the swarmblood’s brood as the inhabitants of their body, much like the swarmblood inhabits a city or wider community, and they also help the swarmblood maintain their humanoid connections with the people around them. Deities of nature and animals, such as Than Dat and Chira on Alacar, are also popular, particularly among pest swarm and wingswarm swarmbloods whose broods take on the shapes of holy animals.

For those who were devout prior to their infestation, it’s common to remain with the same faith even after the transformation. The infestation rarely prompts religious swarmbloods to lose their faith or move to a new church. When it does happen, it’s primarily followers of deities whose doctrines are incompatible with life as a swarmblood or with the philosophy of their hive.

Popular Edicts protect and preserve your brood, find and cherish family where you can, pass on a legacy you can be proud of

Popular Anathemas prioritize the few over the many, refuse to accept that a person has many facets, make a unilateral decision that changes another’s life without their consent

Swarmblood Settlements

The largest swarmblood settlements are formed by the most populous and influential covens among the covenant. They form enclaves carved out within huge metropolises and remote villages sequestered between mountain passes where siblings of a single brood can live together.

A coven with a brood of small crabs lives in the docks district of Kraken in the Indigo Isles, where they operate the fishing boats of the East Pearl Fishery. Across the world at the summit of Kenovar Mountain, a wingswarm coven of a white dove brood lives in the peace and seclusion of the village of Rochaven, descending the mountain once each year with a wagon filled with small wicker goods to sell.

Names

Though not all swarmbloods feel compelled to choose a new name, some do, and these names often either reflect their new lives as a collective of many minds or take after the creatures of the swarmblood’s brood. A collective noun normally used for a group of the animals in a swarmblood’s brood are also popular choices, such as Mischief for a swarmblood infested by rats. Among the covenant, it’s customary for a progenitor to bestow a new name to a swarmblood child they infest once their transformation is complete, though those who flee the transformation might not receive this new name until their return. Some particularly self-loathing scattered who think of themselves as accursed monsters rather than people also change their names, choosing deprecating monikers like Sack or Intrusion, or adopting the cruel jeers of suspicious layfolk for a new name.

Swarmbloods who keep their birth names often find it helps them to feel grounded in their previous identities, and it’s typical for those swarmbloods from hives other than the covenant to have names that are customary among their ancestry or homeland.

Sample Names

Finley, Flock, Gossamer, Hookbeak, Intrusion, Mischief, Plague, Plume, Quill, Sack, Silkwing, Vagabond, Whisker.

Swarmblood Heritages

Each group of infested interacts with their brood in slightly different ways, depending on the different types of creatures that make up their brood. Choose one of the following swarmblood heritages at 1st level.

Aquatic Swarm

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-living-legends-pf2e.yol-heritages.Item.WYDftEvZKxvk13f9]{Aquatic Swarm}

Inside your body, a host of small aquatic creatures such as fish, eels, plankton, or mollusks make their home. The water in your body is enough to support your brood even while you are on land, but they thrive when your body is fully submerged in water. You gain a swim Speed of 30 feet, but your land speed is only 20 feet. In addition, you gain the amphibious trait, and like all creatures with the amphibious trait, you can breathe both water and air.

Crawling Swarm

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-living-legends-pf2e.yol-heritages.Item.EVi4VtpUSNkrLR1E]{Crawling Swarm}

A swarm of crawling insects, spiders, snakes, or small geckos live within your body. Your brood usually takes refuge beneath your skin, but they sometimes crawl out through your orifices and creep across the outside of your body. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-living-legends-pf2e.yol-actions.Item.5NnBbxblFe25R4tt]{Creeping Crawlers} reaction.

Pest Swarm

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-living-legends-pf2e.yol-heritages.Item.fcWPLCqMepquwwu2]{Pest Swarm}

You share your body with a colony of rodents, weasels, or other terrestrial vermin. You gain imprecise scent with a range of 30 feet. This means you can use your sense of smell to determine a creature’s location. The GM will usually double the range if you’re downwind from the creature or halve the range if you’re upwind. In addition, you gain a +2 circumstance bonus to Perception checks to Seek a creature or object within the range of your scent.

Vespin Swarm

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-living-legends-pf2e.yol-heritages.Item.p7NKUVW26m1vp7pZ]{Vespin Swarm}

Your body contains a swarm of arthropods with stingers, such as wasps, hornets, scorpions, or bees. When threatened, members of your brood slip out of your mouth or nose and sting your enemies. You gain a stinger unarmed attack that deals 1d4 piercing damage and 1 poison damage; the piercing damage is the unarmed attack’s weapon damage die, but if you have multiple damage dice (such as from a striking rune), the poison damage increases to 1 per weapon damage die. Your stinger is in the brawling group and has the finesse trait.

Wingswarm

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-ancestries-living-legends-pf2e.yol-heritages.Item.RcmVsZXDQXmvs5Uk]{Wingswarm}

Scores of flying creatures, such as bats, birds, or moths live under your skin and behind your eyes. When you fall, your brood vigorously flaps their wings to carry you aloft. You take no damage from falling, regardless of the distance you fall.

Before the Infestation

During the transformation into a swarmblood, the brood consumes their host’s organs and takes their place inside their body. While a swarmblood might superficially maintain the appearance of their previous ancestry, they do not keep that ancestry’s physiology and consequently are no longer members of that ancestry for most purposes. If your swarmblood preserved their connection to their previous ancestry’s culture or abilities following their curse, consider choosing the Adopted Ancestry general feat to represent that part of your character, especially via the Resurgent Host fe

Ancestry Feats

The following feats are available to swarmblood characters.

One or Many.name: 一个或多个 One or Many
One or Many.text:
出自艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯的档案
一或多

没有人是一块顽石般的整体。我们在思考自己时似乎本能地理解这一点。我们每一次前后不一的行为，都能用处境或心情来辩解。但我们会把别人过度简化，试图将他们广阔而多面的本性压缩成一个标签，以便更容易推理他们或编织一个叙事。我们没有足够的时间或注意力去完整理解身边的每一个人，但这些让我们得以运转、并尝试做出决定的简化漫画，也可能造成巨大的伤害。对于由复合体构成的存在更是如此。几乎每个族裔都有属于复数系统的成员，但很少有哪个族裔比那些确确实实由许多不同的活体生物组成、却又作为一个生物运作的族裔更常见。这些生物拥有所谓的蜂巢思维，且能以许多不同形态出现——行走之虫、蜜蜂妖蜂巢，或虫群血裔。

对虫群血裔而言，整体大于部分之和；其格式塔能够做到任何类人生物族裔能做的事，而其内部的个体虫群生物则会在需要时浮现出来提供各种益处，就如同我们的血液充满了肉眼不可见的微小结构、各自承担不同功能一般。差别在于，虫群血裔的“内里”要大得多，且更像那些通常在世界里独立生活的超小型动物。这使许多人以戒备的态度对待虫群血裔；当他们以“正常”的方式理解动物、并期待以那样的方式遭遇它们时，要让他们把“一个人肚子里有数百甚至数千只动物”这件事想明白，就困难得多。即便有些人能够理解蜂巢思维的概念，仍会轻视动物，因此想当然地认为：一个由许多动物构成其内在的生物，必然更偏兽性或缺乏智慧。这绝非事实——事实上，据说虫群血裔比其他族裔更擅长多任务处理，能同时处理数条思绪线，但这种偏见依旧挥之不去。

要彻底理解虫群血裔，我们必须愿意将他们视为由众多构成的三维存在，正如每一个活着的生物都可以是那样。我们必须抛弃那些启发式与刻板印象——这些简化让我们能够迅速下判断，却阻碍我们真正理解他人深处的丰沛。仅凭第一印象或快速分析、把人归入某个类别，并不存在所谓“好人”或“坏人”、“圣人”或“怪物”。只有人——以他们辉煌、美丽且杂乱无章的复杂性存在。虫群血裔也没有那么不同。我们，所有人，都是多重的。

—— 艾瑟瑞娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物的倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
One or Many

No one is a monolith. We seem to inherently understand this when thinking about ourselves. Our every inconsistent act is justified by circumstance or mood. But we oversimplify others and try to shrink down their expansive multifarious nature into a single label so that we can more easily reason about them or create a narrative. We don’t have the time or attention to understand every person around us in full, but these simplified caricatures that allow us to function and attempt to make decisions can also do great harm. Even more so for beings who are composites. Almost every ancestry has members who are plural systems, but few more so than ancestries that are literally composed of many different living organisms and yet function as one creature. These creatures possess what is known as a hivemind, and they can appear in many different forms—a worm that walks, a mellifican hive, or a swarmblood.

For a swarmblood, the whole is greater than the sum of its parts; the gestalt is capable of anything a humanoid ancestry can do, while the individual swarm creatures inside can emerge as necessary to provide various benefits, in the same way that our blood is full of tiny structures smaller than the eye can see, each with their own function. The difference is that a swarmblood’s internals are much larger and resemble Tiny animals that normally live independently in the world around them. This leads many people to treat swarmbloods apprehensively; when they’ve come to understand animals in the “normal” way they expect to encounter them, it’s much harder to grasp their mind around a person with hundreds or even thousands of them inside. Even some who can understand the concept of the hivemind still look down on animals and thus assume that a creature composed of many animals on the inside must be bestial or unintelligent. This is far from accurate—in fact, swarmbloods are said to be able to multitask and process several trains of thought at once much better than other ancestries can, but it’s a prejudice that persists nonetheless.

Understanding a swarmblood completely requires us to be willing to see them as a three-dimensional being composed of a multitude, just as every living creature can be. We have to abandon our heuristics and stereotypes, the simplifications that allow us to make snap judgments but prevent us from truly understanding the depths of another person. There aren’t “good people” or “bad people,” “saints” or “monsters,” based purely on our first impressions or quick analysis to sort them into a category. There are only people, in their glorious beautiful and messy complexity. Swarmbloods aren’t so different. We, all of us, are many.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Memento Mori.name: 死亡警句 Memento Mori
Memento Mori.text:
摘自艾瑟里娅·尼克斯的档案
Memento Mori

几乎每个活着的生物都害怕死亡。尽管它并非终极的终点，但死亡中有某种不可知——某种难以言喻且不可避免的东西——使其兼具恐惧与阴森的诱惑。所有这些感受也会投射到一切让我们想起死亡的事物之上，无论是表层的视觉相似，还是更深层的形而上联系。对送魂使而言，这两种联系共同塑造了我们的成见。送魂使往往有着鲜明的外表，并会直接让我们联想到死亡——无论是骷髅般的特征、死神的镰刀，还是其他可怖的属性。

就送魂使而言，这种与死亡的联系是真实的，而非想象出来的，但他们并非值得我们恐惧或憎恨的恶意存在。几乎所有送魂使都直接作为炼狱的代理人工作，但这并不意味着他们是闯入凡世、猎杀并杀死人的游荡怪物。相反，他们通常执行抚慰的任务，温柔地引领迷失的灵魂前往其在来世中应归之处。否则，他们便会出手对抗存在的危险扭曲与威胁宇宙的事物，比如一些更为凶残的亡灵（不过那又是另一摊麻烦事），以及对抗被称为萨基尔的叛徒送魂使。

但即便如此，许多不了解宇宙更深层根基的人仍会畏惧并厌恶送魂使。他们常把送魂使误认为亡灵或其他邪恶生物，一见面便退避或攻击。有时，一名肩负来自炼狱的明显神圣使命的送魂使能与这些凡人讲理。否则，由于缺乏证据、信任或对话意愿，谈判便会破裂，冲突随之发生。

这种局面对某一特定类别的送魂使尤为棘手：他们作为特殊行动人员运作，超出炼狱的直接层级体系。因为某些强大而恶意的存在——例如萨基尔折磨者、远古巫妖，以及其他亡灵主谋——能够提前察觉炼狱的影响并据此筹划，所以对来世而言，雇用少数在常规汇报架构之外行动的送魂使担任特工更为有利。炼狱授予这些“自由特工”特别许可，使其可依自身特权执行任务。这些自由特工并非像萨基尔那样的叛逆者；相反，他们通常得到炼狱的非正式认可来履行职责。他们大多以炼狱认可的方式行动——至少在大多数时候如此。然而，缺乏直接联系使他们得以躲过魔法监视，并为其行动提供合理的否认空间。当然，没有这层联系，当某个无知或迷信的凡人决定一见面就攻击他们时，送魂使可调用的资源也会更少。可悲的是，这会导致这些特工因原本并非敌对的旁观者发起的攻击而出现令人尴尬的高损耗率。这庄严地提醒我们：死亡亦会死。

— 艾瑟里娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物的辩护者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
Memento Mori

Almost every living creature fears death. Even though it’s not the ultimate end, there’s something unknowable—something ineffable and inevitable—that gives death its mixture of dread and macabre allure. And all these feelings apply to everything that reminds us of death, whether through a surface-level visual similarity or a deeper metaphysical link. For psychopomps, both links build our prejudices. Psychopomps often have a distinctive appearance and directly remind us of death, whether it be through skeletal features, reaper scythes, or other gruesome attributes.

In the case of psychopomps, that connection to death is real, not imagined, but they aren’t malevolent beings worthy of our fear or hatred. Nearly all psychopomps work directly as agents for Purgatory, but that doesn’t mean they’re marauding monsters who come to the mortal world to hunt down and kill people. Instead, they usually engage in missions of solace, gently leading lost souls to their rightful place in the afterlife. Either that, or they strike out against dangerous perversions of existence and threats to the universe, like some of the more vicious undead (though that issue is a whole other can of worms), as well as against traitorous psychopomps known as sahkils.

But even so, many people who don’t understand the deeper underpinnings of the universe fear and despise psychopomps. They often mistake them for undead or other evil creatures and retreat or attack them on sight. Sometimes, a psychopomp with an obvious divine mission from Purgatory can reason with these mortals. Otherwise, negotiations break down from a lack of proof, trust, or desire for dialogue, and a conflict will ensue.

This state of affairs is especially troublesome for a particular class of psychopomps who operate as special operatives beyond Purgatory’s direct hierarchy. Because some mighty malicious beings, such as sahkil tormentors, elder liches, and other undead masterminds, can detect the influence of Purgatory in advance and plan accordingly, it’s to the advantage of the afterlife to employ a few psychopomps as special agents who operate outside the usual reporting structure. Purgatory grants these “free agents” special permission to carry out tasks in accordance with their prerogatives. These free agents aren’t rebels like sahkils; instead, they typically have unofficial approval from Purgatory to perform their duties. Most of them operate in Purgatory-approved ways, at least most of the time. However, the lack of a direct link evades magical surveillance and provides plausible deniability for their actions. Of course, without this link, the psychopomp has fewer resources upon which they can draw if an ignorant or superstitious mortal decides to attack them on sight. Tragically, this can lead to an embarrassing attrition rate among these agents due to attacks by those who would otherwise be non-hostile bystanders. A solemn reminder that death too can die.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Psychopomp Ancestry.name: 亡魂引渡者族裔 Psychopomp Ancestry
Psychopomp Ancestry.text:
Ominous in appearance and stern in countenance, psychopomps are guardians and guides for the recently deceased, who strive to maintain the cycle of souls and the sanctity of death.

Psychopomps are beings connected to Purgatory, the realm of the dead where mortal souls are sorted and judged. Resolute and determined, they exist to fulfill their purpose: to usher souls to Purgatory, to safeguard them from harm, and to ensure that death remains final. They combat undead, necromancers, and any others who would prey upon souls, such as daemons and sakhils—fallen psychopomps who prey upon mortal fears and torment the dying and recently deceased.

Psychopomps are an integral part of Purgatory’s vast bureaucracy. They see to their personal tasks with an eye to the big picture, content in the knowledge that their individual efforts are one piece of the collective design that maintains the cycle of life and death. As integral as they are to Purgatory and its functions, Purgatory is likewise integral to a psychopomp. Not only does its existence give meaning to a psychopomp’s long life, but it’s also a very real part of them. A shred of Purgatory is nestled at the core of every psychopomp, providing protection, power, and mental fortitude.

Most psychopomps believe wholeheartedly in their cause. They’re unswayed by the countless excuses touted by the souls in their care, feeling neither pity nor doubt in the face of diplomacy, sorrow, or rage. So devoted are they that few choose to differentiate themselves with much in the way of individualism—they dress and behave more or less as one and rarely allow their hobbies or causes to distract them from work. Psychopomps are beings of balance and solemnity who remain outside the bounds of mortal morality. Justice, mercy, hate, freedom, oppression—such nebulous concepts mean nothing to them. Yet, psychopomps are not devoid of emotion. Psychopomps are proud of their role, confident in their ability to perform it, and determined to see it through.

Psychopomps in Purgatory visit the mortal realm only rarely, most often to collect or guide souls, to destroy undead or their makers, or to soothe the living, as their duties dictate. When active in the mortal realm, they pass unnoticed while performing their duties, often invisible or wearing another’s guise. Yet, some beings (including many sakhils) can detect psychopomps through their innate connection to Purgatory, allowing them to slip away and evade a psychopomp dispatched to destroy them.

To counter these elusive entities, Purgatory permanently severed the ties of some psychopomps, a process commonly known as “the cut.” These psychopomps, forever deployed in the mortal realm, serve as special agents capable of taking such foes unaware. Bereft of their connection to Purgatory, these chosen psychopomps are much weakened, both in body and spirit. More shockingly, free of Purgatory’s otherworldly bureaucracy, they find themselves without direction and purpose beyond the generic ideals that led them to become special agents in the first place. Though their values remain the same and they remain devoted to Purgatory, there remains no one to give them orders, and thus, they’re left to chart their own course into the future. These psychopomps experience individualism, freedom of thought, and countless confusing emotions. Without Purgatory’s rigid mental support, they struggle, feel emotions, and experience firsthand how powerful those concepts and values they once scoffed at, such as justice and intolerance, can be. These psychopomps, called the Severed, are most commonly encountered in the mortal realm.

If you want to play a solemn or morose outsider struggling to balance their purpose against their budding morality and individuality or a character with an intimate connection to death and the afterlife, you should play a psychopomp.

You Might...

  • Wear a mask to hide your face, which you never remove in the presence of mortals.
  • Be aloof and unmoved by the passionate pleas, angry tirades, and sorrowful laments of the living.
  • Despise the undead and all those who create them, going out of your way to put an end to their machinations and purge the world of their presence.

Others Probably...

  • Consider your fascination with death and funeral rituals morbid, creepy, or unnerving.
  • Mistake you for a grim reaper, undead, or other foul being intent on ending mortal lives.
  • Long to ask you about death, the afterlife, and Purgatory, but fear your response.

Physical Description

Psychopomps are solemn and serious, with a stern countenance and rigid bearing. Some are visually tied to themes of death, looking either skeletal or ancient, and many adopt other forms to blend in better. Regardless of their appearance, nearly all psychopomps wear masks. Beyond these commonalities, psychopomps look very different from one another, as determined by their heritage.

Calacas look like humanoid skeletons, but this is not the visage they present to the mortal world. Rather, they dress in fine clothing, covering their bodies from head to toe to impersonate the living. While their exact attire changes based on which culture and society they’re visiting, calacas always dress in finery appropriate to a funeral, usually in black, with a bright bowtie or sash, a large hat, finely polished shoes, and long gloves. Calacas cover their face with a mask decorated like a festive skull and are rarely seen without an antique weapon, typically sheathed at their hip, and a musical instrument, often held in their hands (typically a guitar or fiddle). Calacas have a comforting bearing, acting with compassion and kindness. Their movements are graceful and gentle and their voice is solemn yet joyful.

Much like calacas, catrinas look like humanoid skeletons with a full head of hair. They dress in fine, brightly colored clothes and carry flowers, either woven into their hair, pinned in their attire, worn as long garlands, or carried as bouquets. At once both festive and macabre, catrinas are beings of ominous beauty whose mere presence is calming. They wear vibrant silk masks. Among mortals, catrinas don illusory disguises to blend in among the living.

Morrignas are tall and rigid, looking much like living mortals who have forsaken all compassion and weakness. Unnaturally still and patient, morrignas have the air of a stalking predator or a looming nightmare. They dress entirely in spider’s silk, wearing long robes and wrappings, and a half-mask that mimics a spider web. They have large white wings that look batlike or draconic.

Nosois are the most animalistic of psychopomps in appearance and much smaller than the others as well, resembling a whippoorwill, sparrow, raven, or other small bird wearing a leather plague doctor’s mask. They’re incredibly talkative and animated and rarely remain still for long, even while performing their scholarly tasks.

Like morrignas, olethroses appear more like living mortals than most other psychopomps. If it weren’t for the fact that most olethroses have mothlike wings and that they tend to wear masks decorated with spirals and white silk gowns decorated with numerous bronze mirrors, an olethros could easily be mistaken for a slightly short human. More powerful olethroses can be substantially taller, though these olethroses rarely become Severed.

Shokis are gray-skinned humanoids, ashen and ancient, with leathery, wrinkled flesh and long white hair. Curling ram horns crest their foreheads, and a massive snail shell covers their backs, giving them a stooped posture. They wear loose robes, carry a vast collection of religious symbols and paraphernalia, and—most importantly—lean heavily on a staff capable of entrapping wayward souls. Shokis have a wise disposition and excel at feigning emotions, but they are callous and cold at heart. They rarely wear masks, instead donning shadowy hoods and veils.

Vanths are humanoid skeletons with black feathered wings. They carry a sharp scythe and wear a mask resembling a vulture’s skull. Vanths are serious, stoic, and incredibly intimidating. They rarely speak, instead observing with predatory intensity. Among mortals, vanths prefer to pass unseen rather than disguise themselves, showing their ominous visage only to those foes whose lives they intend to reap.

Psychopomp Adventurers

Owing to their unwavering devotion to Purgatory, psychopomps tend to favor classes that align with divine grand causes. They usually become champions, clerics, oracles, or sorcerers with the psychopomp bloodline. Calacas, catrinas, and nosois tend to become bards, while morrignas, shokis, and vanths often become fighters and thaumaturges. Particularly intellectual psychopomps, such as some olethroses, might become investigators or psychics. Psychopomps rarely train in classes that favor powerful emotion or bombastic behavior, such as barbarians or swashbucklers. Morrignas sometimes become summoners and call upon spider-like eidolons. Common backgrounds include acolyte, barrister, bounty hunter, entertainer, martial disciple, raised by belief, and warrior.

Society

Psychopomps, at their most fundamental, are bound by duty to fulfill Purgatory’s directives by safeguarding the cycle of souls. This drive is so instinctive that even among the Severed, whose very connection to Purgatory has been cut and hollowed out, it lives on. Yet, without the guidance of Purgatory’s bureaucracy, how an individual psychopomp chooses to fulfill this need varies dramatically.

Most psychopomps continue to carry out their trained tasks, with nosois handling bureaucratic and organizational tasks, catrinas calming confused souls, and morrignas slaying undead. However, Purgatory’s lack of support and backing forces them to expand their duties out of necessity. A nosoi must determine what to do with their meticulous notes. A catrina must find lost souls lingering in the mortal world, in denial and at risk of becoming undead. A morrigna must identify and hunt a foe worth slaying. In a broader sense, psychopomps of all kinds must choose to wander and help where they can, to investigate and track down disturbances, or to follow some other course; to work independently or as a group; to operate in the open or in the shadows. These choices, collectively referred to as their “methodology,” compel Severed psychopomps to reinvent themselves. While few psychopomps ever regain all of the same powers they had before the cut, most gain new powers that their unsevered brethren can only dream of possessing.

Regardless of methodology, psychopomps are accustomed to being a part of a greater whole—they crave purpose, community, and understanding. If given the chance, Severed psychopomps choose to work or live together, but their scarcity amid the mortal realms makes this a challenge, as does their instinctive need to carry out duties they have difficulty identifying and prioritizing.

Perhaps the most noticeable and integral difference between Severed psychopomps and their Purgatory-bound brethren is their emotion. Severed live near mortals, and without Purgatory supernaturally bolstering their will and resolve, psychopomps become more empathetic and emotional, and they often come to see the power in concepts they once scoffed at, such as love and justice. This transformation unfolds gradually over the course of mortal lifetimes, manifesting as subtle pangs that elicit a mixture of confusion and wonder. Yet, like it or not, this change occurs all the same, and the hearts of the Severed become a little less hollow, their lives a little fuller, and their missions more personal and impassioned. The oldest Severed would argue that this is their greatest strength, for passion is a stronger power than the duty that once drove them—an aberrant sentiment that newly Severed find deeply off-putting.

Psychopomp Settlements

Psychopomps don’t form their own settlements; instead, they live in hiding on the fringes of (or within) mortal cities. However, they often form communities centered around the home of a sedentary psychopomp who provides an invaluable service to their fellows, such as information, supplies, shelter, or healing. These hubs spring from practicality—for few newly Severed would deign to admit to their emotions—but quickly become a beloved home away from home (even if an individual stays only temporarily). These hubs provide much-needed company, guidance, and understanding to the psychopomps within them, becoming places where stories are exchanged, alliances forged, and the fragile threads of identity and hope are quietly reinforced through shared experience and mutual care.

Heritages

The primary tasks a psychopomp attempts to accomplish to fulfill their duties often depend on their psychopomp heritage. The following descriptions are for the seven most common psychopomp heritages.

Calacas

Social, empathic, and already possessing an understanding of mortal emotions, calacas behave much as they did before the cut—they visit funerals, care for the bereaved, and ensure mourners accept death as final. All this to prevent the living from performing acts of necromantic desperation and meddling with the sanctity of death.

Calacas prefer to rely on their social graces and keen observations to see their tasks fulfilled. They celebrate the lives of the dead and offer mourners comforting words, a soothing presence, timely wisdom, and cathartic songs. Calacas see the beauty in the transition from life to death and cherish the passage of every soul from this world. The longer a calacas is Severed, the more they connect with the words they say and the songs they sing, and the more they grieve alongside the grieving.

Calacas are at once joyful and forlorn, sociable and introspective, peaceful and passionate. They celebrate a life well lived, and so they seek to live a life well lived in turn. They favor peaceful mediation over violence. Each typically possesses two items which they value deeply: an instrument and an antique weapon, both of unknown providence. A calaca who loses their instrument or antique weapon usually becomes deeply aggrieved and goes to enormous lengths to retrieve it. While most are simply objects of great sentimental value and all are essentially broken in another creature’s hands, a calaca’s antique weapon is significantly more likely than most weapons to reveal itself as something particularly special, such as a relic. Their antique weapon’s secret backstory might be something even the Severed calaca doesn’t know at first and that they discover during their adventures in the mortal world.

Calacas typically favor the caretaker or wanderer methodologies, though particularly experienced and forward thinking calacas become excellent influencers, leaving such an impression upon the mortals they help that those mortals spread the calaca’s teachings in turn. They work well with other calacas, with whom they form a musical troupe, as well as catrinas, and they benefit greatly from the aid of nosois.

Catrinas

Catrinas spend a great deal of time among mortals, ensuring that even the most recently Severed understand the basics of mortal interaction and societies. They aim to convince confused and scared souls to accept their death. Usually, this involves easing a mortal through a prolonged illness or debilitating injury. At other times, they confront lingering spirits, such as ghosts and haunts, and encourage them to enter Purgatory. A Severed catrina’s services are dearly needed at the site of sudden or tragic deaths, but their ability to foresee and arrive in time for such events is lacking. Many catrinas choose to attend childbirths, serving as spiritual midwives, as the act of childbirth remains dangerous, and the deaths are always tragic.

Catrinas are calm, well-spoken, and understanding. While they initially perform their vigils out of duty—to prevent spirits from clinging to life and becoming ghosts—Severed catrinas become sentimental and compassionate. While they have difficulty genuinely comprehending the grief that accompanies death, they have a keen understanding of regrets. Thus, they tend to embrace their personal goals, desires, and emotions, often taking up hobbies, enjoying periods of rest, and acting with spontaneity. Catrinas are the most likely psychopomps to abandon their duties entirely and live a life for themselves and their own happiness.

Catrinas typically favor the caretaker, networker, or wanderer methodologies. They work well with nosois and reach out to shokis for aid when faced with stubborn spirits.

Morrignas

As Purgatory’s investigators, morrignas understand how mortal societies function and are accustomed to lurking on the fringes of mortal lives. Unlike other psychopomps, a morrigna who strides brazenly through a mortal settlement undisguised will cause little in the way of a disturbance. Most often, they’re regarded with cautious awe or wary respect.

Morrignas identify, track, and slay individuals who thwart death or interfere with the destined flow of souls. This frequently pits them against the undead, necromancers, death cults, and sometimes even particularly nosy scholars. To perform their duties, Morrignas become investigators, spies, assassins, and bounty hunters. They consider themselves akin to a spider at the center of a web—powerful, subtle, connected, and fully in control.

Morrignas are among the proudest of psychopomps. In a fundamental sense, their duties are challenging, require a bevy of skills to accomplish, and frequently involve them outsmarting others. Less tangibly, morrignas are masters of subtlety and information gathering, which often leaves them the most well-connected and well-informed Severed. This tends to leave morrignas with feelings of superiority. Conversely, it also makes them the slowest Severed to discover their emotions, understand different outlooks, and imagine a life that’s more than duty.

Morrignas rarely seek the comfort of psychopomp communities and consider their relationships with other psychopomps either transactional or hierarchical. They typically favor the gravedigger or undertaker methodologies, but make formidable caretakers and influencers. They work well with nosois and vanths, whom they sometimes attempt to boss around. Occasionally, they team up with other morrignas to take down particularly formidable foes or groups.

Nosois

Nosois are talkative and social, accustomed to working with other Nosois and other psychopomp heritages on a daily basis. They bear the brunt of the emotional damage from the cut, quickly succumbing to feelings of loneliness and sadness. Thus, they often seek out other psychopomps and are quick to establish psychopomp hubs and communities if no others exist. They usually set themselves up as an integral resource for other psychopomps, providing supplies, information, record-keeping, or messenger services, which serve to draw the presence of even the most isolated of psychopomps. Nosois are known for being helpful, cheerful, and easy to get along with. Much like they did in Purgatory, nosois perform the administrative work required to keep a disparate community in contact and functioning. However, instead of serving as permanent assistants, Severed nosois take on leadership roles, organizing missions, dispatching psychopomps to sites of interest, and resolving disputes.

Nosois typically favor the networker or supplier methodologies, which incidentally makes them efficient caretakers. They work well with all psychopomps and revel in the company.

Enemies

The primary enemy of psychopomps are undead and all those mortals and immortals who create, protect, or propagate undead across the endless horizons of the planes and the multiverse. Yet, these are hardly their only foes. Many creatures, notably daemons and hags, prey upon souls, choosing to destroy, consume, or capture them. Others lead souls astray, removing them from the cycle of life and death and transforming them into ghosts and haunts. Perhaps a psychopomp’s most personal foes are sakhils, themselves once psychopomps who abandoned their duties. Now, sakhils take joy in tormenting mortals and preying on their fears—particularly the fear of death.

Olethroses

Olethroses are some of the most powerful psychopomps, but also among the most likely to pursue their own missions by their own independent methods, so it’s not a surprise that some olethroses choose to become Severed, even though they stand to lose quite a bit of power in the bargain. Olethroses consider themselves the guardians of fate and destiny, and they can’t stand it when others meddle with it (except themselves, of course). This makes them rivals of fey norns and other creatures that preside over fate. While other psychopomps are focused on death and the afterlife, olethroses follow the entire cycle of birth and rebirth, including the time that mortal beings are still alive and the time before they’re even born.

When new branches of fate come to be, new olethroses are sometimes born as well, in a form of immaculate conception from other olethroses. This means that they alone among psychopomps have parental and sibling bonds, which makes it easier for them to understand the minds and hearts of mortals in that respect.

Olethroses are unusual among psychopomps in that they rarely plot and battle against undead. That isn’t because they have any less hatred for undead but rather because they’re more focused on handling the schemes of psychopomps’ other great enemies: the traitorous sahkils. Some scholars believe that this was because one of the first great sahkils was originally a powerful olethros.

Olethroses typically favor the influencer, networker, or supplier methodologies, though they sometimes become gravediggers or undertakers specifically targeting sahkils or organizations that support them. Morrignas tend to deeply respect olethroses, leading to especially good cooperation between them, while nosois occasionally find olethroses to be a bit too bossy for their tastes. However, these interactions depend on how a given olethros comports themselves.

Shokis

Once powerful and well respected, Severed shokis have a hard time adapting to life amid mortals. In a physical sense, they lose the most power and abilities during the cut, causing deep frustration and feelings of inadequacy. Many become isolated or bitter.

Shokis are shepherds who lead wayward souls to Purgatory, either through compelling discussion or force. They’re cunning, manipulative, and have extensive religious knowledge. They’re accustomed to working alone, beyond the bounds of society.

Shokis typically favor the wanderer methodology, though some find purpose as a gravedigger or undertaker hunting the undead. They tend to lurk in haunted ruins and crumbling edifices, dealing with the undead rather than the living. They appreciate the company of nosois—though they’re often loathe to admit it—but duty or shame tends to drive them away.

Making a Mortal Life

After the Severing, one of a psychopomp agent’s very first challenges is making a life and home for themselves in the mortal realm. While some psychopomp heritages, such as olethroses and morrignas, might be used to undergoing long-term secret missions away from Purgatory where they had to blend into the world around them, others normally only leave Purgatory to pursue specific tasks related to the souls of the dead. Even for olethroses and morrignas, however, there’s a difference between the artifice in simulating a mortal cover identity and the truth in building something real. Those they’ve fooled over the centuries might not be able to tell the difference, but the psychopomp themselves always can.

Vanths

As Purgatory’s soldiers, vanths are accustomed to fighting undead and other threats out of sight of living mortals, both alone and in units. Highly trained and tenacious, they are a formidable force. However, without someone to give them orders or direct them to a suitable foe, most Severed vanths spend their time on the mortal realm lost and wandering. Typically, they hunt undead openly in the wilds, exploring ancient ruins and forgotten tombs, and stalk city streets in secret, searching for undead in graveyards and listening for leads on their next destination or target.

Vanths learn quickly not to appear to mortals, as their appearance inspires dread, and most mortals confuse them with grim reapers or other undead. Thus, many vanths live a lonely life, watching mortals but only interacting with other psychopomps.

Vanths typically favor the caretaker, gravedigger, undertaker, or wanderer methodologies. They frequently train and form combat squads with other vanths and appreciate the information passed on to them by nosois and morrignas. While it’s difficult to tell due to their grim countenance and lack of communication, vanths enjoy the company of nosois, since the smaller avian psychopomps’ incessant chatter makes a vanth feel more like part of a community.

Methodologies

The method by which a Severed psychopomp accomplishes their goals in the mortal realm is their methodology. The following are the seven most common methodologies.

Caretaker

Caretakers are sedentary guardians who select a region to serve as their territory and then spend their entire lifetime protecting it. Usually, a caretaker adopts a settlement and its environs as their territory, but a minority instead adopts ancient ruins or regions of wilderness. Caretakers often become emotionally attached to their territory and its occupants. They take pride in their territory’s accomplishments, mourn its hardships, and celebrate its prosperity. With time, caretakers broaden their definitions of “protection,” safeguarding not only the sanctity of life and death but also the well-being of their territory and its occupants.

Gravediggers

Gravediggers are similar to undertakers, save that they select an organization, cult, or faction to dismantle rather than an individual. Sometimes, these hated factions are dispersed across nations and continents, but it’s more common that they’re local to a smaller region—usually a city or nation. Gravediggers are clever investigators, following leads and clues to scour every trace of a group’s existence. Often, their success identifies another group requiring the gravedigger’s attention.

Influencer

Influencers understand that Severed psychopomps are at a distinct disadvantage—they’re few, isolated, and weakened by the cut. Thus, influencers sway mortals into aiding their cause. Whether by hiring mortals to serve as trusted agents, manipulating mortals from the shadows, or inspiring mortals to action, influencers have a reach and impact much greater than other Severed. However, the unpredictability of mortals sometimes causes an influencer’s efforts to backfire.

Networker

Networkers gather, collate, and share information with their fellow Severed, making them an incredible boon to their fellows. Since networkers are often sedentary, other psychopomps often seek them out for their assistance and advice, making them a hub for psychopomp activity and community. Many networkers serve as dispatchers, dividing Severed into units and assigning them missions.

Supplier

Much like networkers gather information, suppliers gather material goods and supplies, then disperse them to psychopomps in need. This commonly includes anything from armor and weapons to cover identities and potent magic. Suppliers are sedentary and hide in plain sight amid large settlements. They usually maintain several properties that serve as storage houses, businesses, and safehouses. Their aid is integral to other psychopomps, causing psychopomp communities to spring up around them.

Undertakers

Undertakers set their goals high, identifying a powerful individual they’re determined to destroy, then bending all their will and effort to follow through. Typically, their targets are immortal tyrants, devious necromancers, dangerous fiends, and powerful undead, such as liches, banshees, and grim reapers. After accomplishing their goals, undertakers tend to drift for a while, performing cleanup duties and wandering until they either adopt another methodology or select another target.

Wanderer

Wanderers travel across the world wherever their whims dictate, solving any issues they come across and fighting any evils they discover along the way. Brave and multi-talented, wanderers run the gamut from bold and independent to lost and aimless and can be found anywhere from ancient ruins and isolated wildernesses to crowded cities and rugged frontiers.

Beliefs

Most psychopomps are methodical, resolute, and stoic, ready and willing to perform their duties through the ages without pause or oversight. Newly Severed psychopomps often tend toward cold aloofness and neutrality. Over time, as they explore their emotions and come to understand mortal concepts of morality, they tend to slide closer toward good or to other alignments, depending on their experiences.

Psychopomps believe in preserving the sanctity of life and death. First and foremost, their devotion is to Purgatory itself. Those who worship gods tend to favor gods of life and death, like Aoz and Zoa in Alacar, or, later in life, gods of wisdom, self-discovery, and whatever causes they individually cherish.

Popular Edicts destroy undead, maintain the sanctity of life and death, defend spirits from predation

Popular Anathemas create undead, resurrect the departed, prevent a soul from moving on

Purgatory

Purgatory, the land of the dead, is the plane where dead souls go to be judged and sent to their final reward or punishment in the afterlife. At least, that’s how things are supposed to work. Souls that intentionally or accidentally elude Purgatory’s grip contribute to the erosion of this process. And so while many psychopomps exist to fulfill Purgatory’s function, others hunt those who would break the cycle.

Names

Psychopomps typically possess a single name, most often one that has two to three syllables and favors hard consonants. As Severed psychopomps begin to understand their emotions and embrace their individuality, however, they tend to select a new name for themselves. This appellation serves as recognition that they’ve entered the next chapter in their lives and that the psychopomp they were before the cut no longer exists.

Sample Names

Azuhail, Barveigh, Darazahk, Gorescu, Kesseret, Naxan, Pompom, Rozarin, Traviri, Urdek.

You Are What You Eat.name: 人如其食 You Are What You Eat
You Are What You Eat.text:
出自艾瑟里娅·妮克丝的档案
吃什么就成为什么

饮食往往是将我们区分开来的因素。这或许有些讽刺，因为对养分的需求又如此将活着的生物团结在一起。即便在同一族裔之中，那些食用在其他文化中令人作呕的物质或生物的人，也可能发现这种负面的反应会外溢。而试图强行劝说他人改变饮食习惯，通常都会遭到鄙夷——即便是那些伪君子也一样；他们若遇到冒犯自己感受的饮食，也会做同样的事。

就我个人而言，我不会试图告诉别人该吃什么。但许多类人生物确实存在一种偏见：他们认为，为了烹饪而大规模屠宰动物以及其他有感知但不具理性的生物，是完全可以接受的；而对具理性的生物做同样的事，在道德上则是错误的。我发现这条泾渭分明的界线难以下咽——人们总喜欢把事情变成二分法，而不是视作一条连续的光谱时，通常都会如此。这也是具理性的生物在明知不具理性的生物没有足够智力为自己辩论的情况下，所乐于采用的一种方便分类。不过施放一个“与动物交谈”法术，你就会发现它们依然拥有丰富的精神生活。

我为何要对食物与饮食习惯如此咏叹？这段话是我将要讨论某个族裔的前言；它们常被误解，部分原因正是它们的饮食与起源：食脑者。顾名思义，它们以大脑为食，而且大脑中的智性活动越多，它们就越觉得美味。考虑到它们的饮食，人们称它们为怪物也就不足为奇了——这触发了也许是“厌恶”与“道德愤怒”的完美组合。

我并不是在说，那些猎杀类人生物以获取其大脑，或将其当作牲畜饲养的食脑者值得称赞。但也有其他食脑者会以大多数人认为更合乎伦理的方式来满足饥饿——只要人们能越过“吃大脑”这件事带来的震撼。例如，一些食脑者是延迟满足的大师；它们会寻找可能的天才，并出钱让其接受最好的教育以确保天赋被培育成长，作为交换，则获得在其死后收割其大脑的权利。另一些则利用食脑者从完整大脑中获取印象的能力，提供数种相关的死后服务。食脑者调查员可以通过吞食受害者的大脑来获得谋杀案的关键线索；食脑者灵媒可以为逝者的挚爱亲属带来释怀；而食脑者馆藏者则会接纳大脑以纪念那些否则将被遗忘的人。

如果我们能越过它们的饮食，我们就会看到：食脑者的存在本身就不太可能，几乎称得上是奇迹——至少也是传奇般的事件。通常，当脑鹦鹉螺附着在某个生物的头骨上时，它们会杀死宿主，并操纵其不死的身体。但食脑者则是在两者改为以共生方式联结、合而为一时形成的。这意味着食脑者的本质有一部分来自另一个生物，而那个生物可能来自某个你更容易承认为“人”的族裔。并且，随着大脑的印记成为食脑者的一部分，食脑者心灵中非鹦鹉螺的那一部分往往会对其决策过程施加强大的影响；若我们选择与它们交流而非试图猎杀它们——触发它们的战或逃反应——这种影响会更为明显！因此，若我们想理解食脑者，以及它们那能为我们的社会做出贡献的独特能力，我们就必须先从审视我们对饮食的看法、尤其是对食用大脑这件事的看法开始，以求得新的视角。

—— 艾瑟里娅·妮克丝，空间元素的先驱，怪物的倡导者

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
You Are What You Eat

Diets have often been a factor that separates us. That’s perhaps ironic because of how much the need for sustenance unites living creatures. Even within the same ancestry, those who eat substances or creatures that inspire revulsion in other cultures might find that the negative reaction bleeds through. And attempts to forcefully persuade another person to change their diet are usually met with disdain, even among hypocrites who would do the same for diets that offend their sensibilities.

Personally, I don’t try to tell other people what to eat. But there’s certainly a bias among many humanoids toward the idea that mass slaughter of animals and other sentient but non-sapient creatures for the purpose of cuisine is completely acceptable, while the same behavior toward sapient creatures is morally wrong. I find that stark dividing line hard to swallow, as usual when people try to make things into a dichotomy instead of a sliding scale. It’s also a convenient categorization for sapient creatures to employ when they know that non-sapient creatures don’t have the intellect to argue either way. But cast a speak with animals spell and you’ll see that they still have a rich mental life.

Why am I waxing poetic about food and diets? This is preamble for my discussion of an ancestry often misunderstood in part because of their diet and their origins: cerebrophages. As the name implies, they eat brains, and the more intellectual activity in the brain, the better for them in terms of taste. Given their diet, it should be no surprise that people call them monsters, as this triggers perhaps the perfect combination of disgust and moral outrage.

I’m not saying that cerebrophages who hunt and kill humanoids for their brains or raise them as livestock are admirable. But other cerebrophages find ways to sate their hunger that most people would consider more ethical, after looking past the shock of eating brains. For instance, some cerebrophages, masters of delayed gratification, seek out potential prodigies and offer to pay for them to have the best education to ensure their talents are nurtured, in exchange for the right to harvest their brain after their death. Others take advantage of a cerebrophage’s ability to gain impressions from an intact brain to provide one of several related post-mortem services. Cerebrophage investigators can gain crucial clues to a murder by consuming the victim’s brain; cerebrophage mediums can provide closure to the loved ones of the deceased; and cerebrophage curators accept brains to memorialize individuals who would otherwise be lost.

If we could just look past their diet, we would see that the existence of cerebrophages at all was so unlikely as to be something of a miracle, or at least a legendary occurrence. Normally, when a brain nautilus attaches to a creature’s cranium, they kill the host and puppet their undead body. But cerebrophages are formed when the two instead symbiotically link to become one. That means part of a cerebrophage’s essence comes from that other creature, which might have been from an ancestry you more easily recognize as a “person.” And with the brains’ imprints becoming part of the cerebrophage, the non-nautilus portion of the cerebrophage’s psyche often exerts a powerful influence on their decision-making process, even more so if we engage with them rather than seek to hunt them down, triggering their fight or flight response! Thus, if we want to understand cerebrophages and their unique abilities to contribute to our society, we must first seek perspective on our views about diets, and particularly the consumption of brains.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Cerebrophage Ancestry.name: 噬脑者族裔 Cerebrophage Ancestry
Cerebrophage Ancestry.text:
脑鹦鹉螺是一种微小而聪慧的异怪，拥有恶毒的生态习性：通常，当一只脑鹦鹉螺附着在某个生物的身体上时，该生物便注定会痛苦地死去——脑鹦鹉螺会吞噬其大脑，并操控其尸体去追猎猎物。但在极少数情况下，脑鹦鹉螺会对某个有智慧生物的大脑产生反应，使二者融合为一种完全不同的单一生物，拥有崭新的身份与族裔，只保留原本两种生命形态的记忆、情感与洞见的片段与闪回。这些神秘的新存在仍会渴求大脑，因此得名“cerebrophage”，亦即“食脑者”。

食脑者渴求有智慧的大脑。它们可以通过大量吞食非智慧生物的大脑来满足这种饥饿，但以这种方式维持食脑者所需的大脑数量极其庞大，因此这很少是可行的供养手段。正因如此，大多数食脑者仍以传统方式满足饥饿：吃有智慧生物的大脑。它们不需要太多：每周一个有智慧的大脑最为理想；在紧急情况下，它们最多能撑到一个月，只要不要每次都等那么久。相较之下，它们若要每周维持自身，就得吞食将近100个小型或更大的非智慧生物的大脑。这并非灰质体积的问题——事实上，那些出于绝望或道德良心而走这条路的食脑者，根本无法在体内容纳相当于100个大脑的量，往往在进食后会把大部分脑浆吐出来。决定营养含量的，是大脑中的精神本质，或心灵能量。以同样的道理，某些格外聪明且具心灵力量的生物的大脑，甚至可能让食脑者维持长达一个月。

因此，食脑者的饮食很可能是一片纠结的道德泥沼……但当然，也有相当数量的食脑者选择“戈尔迪之结”的解法：想吃什么就吃什么，后果见鬼去吧。鉴于食脑者以其心灵能力闻名，而那些吞噬无数有智慧大脑者只会愈发强大，与这些食脑者的遭遇，使该族裔背负起阴沉的名声：强大的食脑主谋，把其他族裔当成不过是长了腿的餐点。

另一方面，也有一些食脑者，其脑鹦鹉螺之外的“另一自我”影响更强，或道德底线更坚韧，这些食脑者会寻求能让它们与其他族裔和平共存的解决方案。但它们在这条路上往往遭遇挑战：谁会愿意在自己人中接纳一个食脑者？这些温和的食脑者要么找到愿意将其视作个体而接纳的社群，要么作为四处漂泊的旅人或冒险者生活，要么伪装其真实本性以融入其中。

若你想扮演一个以鹦鹉螺为头、因双重本性而拥有复杂精神生活的心灵食脑者，你就该选择食脑者。

你可能会……

  • 发现不同的精神状态、智识专长或记忆，会在某个生物的大脑中呈现多样而美味的风味。
  • 偶尔经历与脑鹦鹉螺或与之共同构成你食脑者自我的另一有智慧生物相关的幻象或感官闪回。
  • 练习精炼你的心灵能力，以对付你的敌人。

其他人多半会……

  • 担心当你与他们对视时，其实是在掂量他们的大脑是否适合作为一顿饭。
  • 以为你聪慧绝伦或是强大的心灵能力者。
  • 一旦他们认识到你是大脑方面的专家，就会来向你寻求心理建议。

外形描述

食脑者通常看起来隐约具有人形，长着类似鹦鹉螺的头部，并配有无数触手。然而这并非保证；这取决于与脑鹦鹉螺形成共生结合的生物。例如，一只独角兽与一只脑鹦鹉螺可能形成一种隐约马形、鹦鹉螺头的食脑者，带有蹄子与微驼、近乎四足的姿态。即便在更不寻常的案例中，食脑者也总会有两只手或其他类似的附肢，能够以同样方式握持物体。

食脑者倾向穿着宽松长袍或其他朴素的肥大衣物，而非时髦或华丽的高级定制服饰。不过这并非铁律，食脑者也有各种各样的时尚感官。

社会

食脑者是由意外的共生互动而诞生的独特个体，因此它们彼此之间没有真正的繁殖方式。也因此，它们的选择要么是作为个体生活，要么尝试加入另一个或多个族裔的社会，要么四处寻觅以找到并与其他食脑者一同生活。

在这三种可能之中，多数食脑者选择独居生活。这比学习与他人相处要简单得多，而且对那些想要猎杀有智慧生物并吞食其大脑的食脑者而言尤为理想。这些独居的食脑者有时会征服并雇佣其他生物作为爪牙与帮凶，尤其是那些食肉者。毕竟，食脑者只需要吃大脑；其余部分可以留给下属。这也能帮助食脑者处理其心智中非鹦鹉螺部分遗留的孤独感——那部分也许曾渴望同伴。

加入他者社会的食脑者，是从社会常态角度看第二常见的类型。它们往往难以融入，但随着时间推移，最终能说服邻里相信它们无意伤人，并且它们提供的服务使其成为当地社群的珍贵成员。冒险小队与其他战斗同盟亦然——它们往往会欣赏食脑者洞见与心灵能力所带来的贡献。

现实中最少见、却最富传奇色彩的，是那些彼此寻觅、意图在某处建立殖群的食脑者。一旦聚集了如此多的食脑者，为它们供食所需的大脑数量将高到几乎不可避免地会发生不对劲的事。毕竟，那些较为温和的食脑者密室提出的策略，也许足以让一个食脑者在其他族裔的大型聚居地中找到足够的大脑，却远远不足以供养大量食脑者。面对只能成为更大规模的牧养者或屠杀者这两种选择，前者是唯一不会不可避免地引来武装士兵或冒险者洗劫殖群的选项——但这同样是多数有智慧生物既恐惧又厌恶的局面。

食脑者冒险者

食脑者冒险者通常会选择能让它们运用大脑来探索与战斗的职业，例如施法者、调查员、发明家等。心灵术士一直以来都是多数食脑者的最爱。它们很少选择成为其他武斗职业，尤其是野蛮人。荒野中独行的食脑者往往会拥有类似隐士的背景；但当它们融入社会并试图找到大脑来源时，依其密室而定，它们可能成为占星师、簿记员、侦探、伪灵媒、占卜师、猎人、秘法图书管理员、学者、魔法学生或殡葬者等——仅举数例。

密室

鉴于它们孤立且随机的“诞生”，食脑者往往拥有丰富的内在生活，进而对周遭世界形成极其多样的哲学。完整的哲学过于复杂，难以彻底分类；但食脑者社会的某一单一侧面至关重要，足以自成分野：它们如何获取食物？各种观点与策略可归纳为较少数的生活方式，通常称为“密室”。下文描述六个最著名的密室。

屠夫

屠夫通过让自己塞满来自较低智生物、难以下咽的残渣，以避免吞食有智慧存在的大脑。为达成此事，它们每天需要十多个足够大的动物大脑，最好更多；即便如此，久而久之也可能出现营养缺乏。通常，这样的摄入量要求一种围绕狩猎或屠宰动物而运作的生活方式，例如“屠宰”这一职业，亦为其同名由来。一旦当地人能克服其外貌，一名愿意以折扣提供服务的食脑者屠夫便可能成为社群支柱；即便人们发现自家屠夫在吃掉所有动物的大脑，也未必会真的介意。同样地，食脑者猎人与捕手也可能以一种仍能出售动物其余部分作为食物、皮毛或其他材料的方式取出并吞食大脑。

其他食脑者常带着怜悯看待屠夫——它们必须把如此多的生命投入到进食之中，却从未体验过一顿满足的餐食。至于屠夫自身，并非所有屠夫都将其饮食视为绝对：若它们遇到新鲜的有智慧生物尸体，这些弹性的屠夫愿意取用其大脑。相反，正统屠夫甚至不会考虑此选项；它们相信，一旦品尝过有智慧大脑，就会难以回到动物大脑那令人不快的味道与不满足的口感——就像彻底戒掉一样困难。

图书管理员

图书管理员是一类提供特殊临终服务的食脑者：它们吞食亡者的大脑，以将其中部分记忆归档留存，成为献给其所吞噬者人生的活体图书馆或博物馆。有些仅是储存知识；另一些则加以运用，例如吞食谋杀受害者的大脑，并利用所获取的记忆来协助调查凶案。由于并非所有食脑者在进食大脑时都能体验记忆，图书管理员密室的可能成员数量受限，且属于最小的密室之一。然而，图书管理员拥有巨大的影响力，因为每一位图书管理员个体都是隐秘学识、冷门事实与他人带进坟墓的秘密的宝库。对多数图书管理员而言，保存知识与满足对大脑的饥饿同等重要，尽管它们有时会把求知推得过远。其信念往往使它们在生命可能受威胁时极端谨慎——毕竟它们若死去，储存自他者的所有知识与记忆也将随之消失。为避免此命运，一群强大的图书管理员正合作创建心灵档案，使它们能在灾害发生时保留所获记忆的副本。不过它们仍缺少完成该项目的关键——无论是一种新理论还是一种稀有试剂——因此可能需要请来一些可信的年轻图书管理员来协助达成目标。

部分图书管理员会与另一密室的界线变得模糊，通常是殡仪师或资助者；它们除图书管理员的身份外，也兼任其他密室成员。其中最糟的一些也是屠杀者，痴迷于为其内在图书馆获取特定知识而不计代价。这些图书管理员屠杀者会追踪持有其渴求新知的有智慧生物，继而跟踪并杀死对方以取其大脑、获得该知识。其他图书管理员通常会否认这些行为，并认为那些食脑者不过是玷污图书管理员密室之名的屠杀者。

殡仪师

人人终有一死。冒险者有时会在致命地城的深处丧命，除了就地掩埋或为防止其化为不死而火化之外别无选择。但在人形生物社会中，多数人会在更可控的环境中死去，使得根据其文化与当地根源进行丧葬仪式成为可能。即便没有怪物袭击、战争、谋杀或瘟疫，多数城市每周也会出现不止一例死亡；在某些具有各种风险因素的季节，甚至更多。这足以为职业殡仪师提供工作，同样也足以喂饱一名食脑者。

殡仪师是为亡者提供遗体处理与葬礼、并为生者带来慰藉的食脑者，以吞食尸体大脑作为交换。有时要说服当地人接受“让挚爱逝去亲属的大脑被吞食”的想法并不容易，因此殡仪师会努力获得社群接纳，并以能让他人安心、而非感到不安或恶心的方式解释其所为。有时殡仪师会提供某种额外噱头来帮助推广其服务。例如，有些会担任通灵媒介：它们可能利用真正的食脑者能力从被吞噬的大脑中获取信息，也可能运用冷读技巧与欺骗，或两者结合，让幸存者得以与失去的挚爱“交流”（或至少感觉像是交流过）。

牧养者

牧养者自认是所有密室中最安全、最务实的，但它们也是最广为人知、最遭憎恨的一类。因为它们饲养有智慧生物（通常是人形生物），以其大脑为食。牧养者指出，这是唯一能确保你一定有足够大脑活下去的方法。有些牧养者会试图找借口或淡化其人形牧场的本质，声称它们与同样经营自有社群的资助者大同小异，并且会等待其佃户过完完整一生后才收割其大脑。但也有许多牧养者完全拥抱自身本性，宣称其他有智慧存在只配当饭吃。它们是否否认或接受其生活方式的令人作呕，对生活在牧场里的人几乎没有差别——因为他们对此别无选择。

对环境拥有完全控制、将自己封闭在对外界知之甚少的泡泡中的牧养者，有时能创造出看似快乐明朗的地方。在这些地方，临近寿终的人会将自愿献出大脑给其食脑者统治者视为义务与荣耀。然而，多数牧场充满了明白自身处境之恐怖的惊惧灵魂。

当牧养者聚集成群时，它们有时会统治广阔的社会，建造巨大的、脑形的生体魔法装置，以在大规模上安抚民众并防止起义。毕竟，即便你是由强大心灵术士组成的阴谋集团，成千上万绝望的人形生物联合起来的叛乱仍是极其严重的威胁。

食脑者聚居地

由于它们在世界各地随机出现，多数食脑者生活在主要由其他生物构成的聚居地中。少数食脑者聚居地令外界极度恐惧，因为它们几乎不可避免地是由牧养者密室的强大食脑者议会所统治的大型聚居地，聚居着其他有智慧生物，而统治者会饱食其公民的大脑。鉴于它们需要健康的大脑与较高的存活率，这些聚居地通常和平、维护良好，整体上也是宜居之处——只要你能无视那种终将被吞食大脑的隐秘恐怖。有些这样的聚居地要么并不理解那些“被邀请前往辉耀宫殿”的人会遭遇什么，要么明明知情却被洗脑或灌输为这是一件好事。

屠杀者

屠杀者从缜密规划者到机会主义猎人无所不包，是为了满足对大脑的饥饿而杀害有智慧存在的食脑者。多数屠杀者会通过挑选不会被想念或其死亡可被归因于其他原因的受害者来避免引人注意，例如独自穿越荒野的旅人。这样一来，只需每周到每月一次杀戮，它们就可能多年不被发现，甚至可能一生如此。相反，那些自认美食家的屠杀者会寻找具有特定品质的大脑，使其更加显眼。例如，一名只吃博学法师大脑、并声称自己喜欢奥术能量在大脑中迸裂的“滋滋声”的屠杀者，很可能很快就会被识别为连环杀害法师的凶手——即便它小心掩盖自己在吃受害者大脑的事实。还有些强大、傲慢且施虐的屠杀者会公然留下预告下一名受害者的标记，认为当大脑在知晓死亡将至的焦虑中发酵后，会更加美味。

另一些屠杀者则出于不同理由而设定标准：它们会猎杀作恶者、恶毒的有智慧野兽与诸如此类的不法之徒，作为下一顿餐食。事实上，那些经常遭遇致命战斗的冒险食脑者，往往会不费多少力气就偶然获得一两个有智慧大脑。因此，许多并非出于意识形态原因而当屠夫的食脑者冒险者，会成为猎人，或至少将其他密室的做法与猎人式的做法混合。这些冒险者认为，既然已经夺走敌人的性命，再浪费其大脑就太不尊重被杀者了。

资助者

资助者被称作食脑者族群的“慈善家”，尽管它们施予慷慨时带着相对不加掩饰的个人盘算。资助者希望避免在获取大脑一事上引发任何不当敌意，并认为最自我实现之人的大脑最为美味。一定有办法同时达成这两个目标！为此，它们提供奖学金，资助有前途的青年进入最顶尖的学院，成为名流与创新者；其契约中包含一条不寻常的附带条件：当受助者死去时，其大脑归资助者所有，这与选择将大脑或身体其他部分捐赠给医学院、帮助医生与外科医师练习的人类似。对多数人而言，这是实现梦想所需付出的微小代价；作为附带收益，这也有助于提升资助者在社群中的名声，使食脑者更容易被接纳。资助者密室的挑战之一在于，它要求食脑者拥有可观资金——足以每年资助约12到50名学生——这可能超出许多食脑者的能力。不仅如此，这个密室是一项长期投资，通常要求资助者在第一批学者开始步入老年、死亡变得频繁之前，还得另有大脑来源。

大脑风味

有时，其他族裔中好奇的成员会试图让食脑者描述不同大脑的风味。想要达成共同理解往往徒劳无功，因为这种“味道”更多取决于不同思维类型所带来的心灵能量，而非大脑的物理构成；而物理构成也会因食脑者而异。总体而言，食脑者理解其他生物用来描述“辛辣”“甜”等味道的词汇，并可能体验过可作为参照的记忆闪回。即便如此，一名食脑者可能会说，被强烈情绪所俘获之人的大脑尝起来很辣；而另一名食脑者可能认为那更像浓郁或鲜味。少数共通点是：智力更高或学识更深的生物，其大脑往往拥有更复杂、更美味的风味；而非智慧生物的大脑则会呈现一种同时寡淡又令人作呕的味道。

脑鹦鹉螺

食脑者是与构成它们的脑鹦鹉螺或人形生物截然不同的存在，但这并不意味着它们完全无关。在脑鹦鹉螺大量聚集的地区，食脑者更可能诞生；然而，这两个物种更可能彼此对立而非合作。因此，食脑者通常要么避开脑鹦鹉螺，要么主动对抗并攻击它们——往好说是争夺美味大脑的竞争者，往坏说则是被深恶痛绝的敌人。

脑鹦鹉螺诞生于深海之下，随着成熟而变为两栖；但只有少数被称为“水生食脑者”的食脑者保留在水下呼吸与移动的能力。随着脑鹦鹉螺成长为成体，它们开始渴求越来越多的精神能量，以至于简单的海洋生物已不足以维持它们。此时，它们的移动取决于附近是否有有智慧的水生或两栖族裔的本地族群。若有，成熟的脑鹦鹉螺很可能会留在水下，猎杀这些更聪明（因此也更美味）的邻近猎物。

但海洋浩瀚，其中大部分区域缺乏较大的有智慧猎物。因此，许多脑鹦鹉螺会寻找一个更能在陆地上行走的宿主，将触手钻入宿主的大脑并控制其神经。这使它们能将宿主身体当作“脑傀儡”来使用，同时从容地让自己在宿主的大脑上饱食。若它们获得了特别有用的脑傀儡，往往会尽可能久地保持其大脑与身体完整，用其来追猎并捕获其他可供盛宴的猎物。几乎像寄居蟹一样，一旦找到一个更大更好的新脑傀儡，它们便会丢弃甚至最偏爱的旧傀儡；但有时也会因磨损累积而被迫放弃某个脑傀儡。

延缓饥饿

基于个人舒适度与饥饿发作的痛苦，多数食脑者更喜欢每周吃一次有智慧生物的大脑。尽管最低饮食需求会因食脑者而略有差异，它们通常至少每月需要一次营养充足的进食。虽然这并不频繁，但有时它们很难通过完全“光明正大”的方式把脸部触手伸向一个美味的头颅——这对像四处旅行、在任何本地社群都没有根基的食脑者冒险者尤其如此。每天吃掉约一打小动物的残渣作为替代方案是可行的，屠夫密室便体现了这种生活选择，但对孤身食脑者而言可能尤其沉重。不过，就日常的大脑摄入而言，典型的冒险小队可能会每天吃掉好几只兔子，这对食脑者的短期摄食会是个不错的办法。

信念

若你的游戏使用阵营，你可以用以下倾向来指导你选择阵营；若不使用，也可用来协助刻画食脑者：食脑者天生需要吃有智慧的大脑，而将自己限制在动物大脑上的体验——如屠夫所为——并非胆小者能承受。那些为了大脑而无情收割生命者，或在牧场中饲养有智慧存在以取其大脑者，皆为邪恶；但其他多数食脑者即便在各密室内部，其哲学与眼界也差异极大。

食脑者有时会崇拜与深度思考或智识追求相关的神祇，例如阿拉卡尔的“往昔之同胞赫斯特”（Herst the Sibling of the Past）。然而，食脑者由罕见过程形成，通常不会遇到同族以交流宗教见解。因此，与其说它们会选择符合族裔整体的神祇，不如说它们更常选择契合自身个人哲学或密室的神祇。例如，一名热爱大快朵颐吃脑的食脑者，可能会崇拜与暴食或盛宴相关的神祇，如阿拉卡尔的“过度女王埃布鲁格瑟斯”（Ebrugeses the Queen of Excess）。

常见典范 牢牢抓住你的记忆并永远珍视它们，用你的心智更轻松、更高效地解决问题，把事情想清楚

常见禁忌 让一颗完好无损的大脑被白白浪费，用蛮力硬闯去解决问题，不经思考就行动

名字

除少数例外，食脑者是由一种零星且混沌的过程所产生的个体。因此，它们会为自己取名，除非它们保留了足够多的凡人或脑鹦鹉螺记忆以继续使用旧名（或将两者结合）。在罕见情况下，新形成的食脑者会遇到另一位食脑者并被其收养、获得名字。通常这位收养者可能是成为食脑者一部分的非鹦鹉螺生物的朋友或战友；对方选择接纳这一新生命形态，而非将其视为朋友死亡的象征而拒绝。

示例名字

Brainiac, Calhoun, Cerebrum, Davy, Elizavlaarx, Mastermind, Pinky, Krullxjohn, The Gentleman, Tiffany, Yuulveth.

食脑者传承

每一名食脑者都源自两种生物之间独特的心灵魔法互动，将它们锻铸为完全崭新的存在。因此，依该转化的性质，它们在能力上分化为若干传承。你在1级时选择以下一种食脑者传承。
