Devil’s Advocate.name: 魔鬼代言人 Devil’s Advocate
Devil’s Advocate.text:
摘自艾瑟莉娅·尼克斯的档案
魔鬼的辩护人

事实证明，魔鬼往往聪明绝顶、阴险狡诈，而且话多到不需要别人替他们辩护。至少，通常是这样。但有时候，他们的机敏、算计、钻营、打官司以及钻法律漏洞，会让他们陷入一种异常的境地，使得界线变得不那么清晰。

我们常听说凡人与魔鬼之间的地狱契约：凡人以出卖灵魂、堕入地狱为代价，换取某种世俗欲望。但这类契约只占所有经由地狱冥狱官僚体系流转的契约中的极小一部分；不过我们对它们如此执着也可以理解：那是最贴近我们生活的契约，是可能影响到我们认识之人的契约。对于我们之中走投无路的人来说，那也是他们或许有朝一日会考虑签下的契约。地狱库藏中的其他契约，则详述了各个魔鬼或魔鬼派系之间的义务。地狱里的每个魔鬼，至少都有一份役使契约，用以说明他们在地狱森严等级制度中的职责与位置——对于那些起初是凡人、通过签署冥狱契约而来的存在而言，甚至可能正是他们当初立下的那份誓约，规定了他们新的职位（这些算是愚蠢凡人里更“聪明”的那类：他们早做打算，为自己在地狱里讨价还价争取到更好的位置，而不是任由魔鬼随意安排）。

无论是哪一种契约，地狱那错综复杂的众多法律漏洞之一——由冥狱先例所确立——便是无情地利用所谓的“四角原则”：口头约定毫无意义，只有契约的细则才作数。通常，这被用来欺骗凡人或那些不懂细则的不谨慎魔鬼；但同样的原则也可能成为救赎：如果你摧毁地狱中那份契约的书面副本，那么无论口头约定如何，你都不再会被该契约约束。这个小细节一直是多部吟游史诗的主题：英雄远征地狱，只为将一位善良的挚爱之人的灵魂从冥狱契约中拯救出来。而确实，这样的劫掠行动，唯有最伟大的英雄才堪当。

而我们不常听到的，是那些已经生活在地狱里的魔鬼；他们在摧毁束缚自己、迫使自己服役并赋予其履行职责之力的契约方面，反而更占优势。有时，只需认识对的魔鬼、献上合适的贿赂，或拥有许可、或了解其布局，就能让凡人眼中的史诗级劫案，对一名人脉广泛的魔鬼而言变成相对平常的事情。

当一名魔鬼摆脱所有契约与地狱的羁绊后会发生什么，因魔鬼而异。他们会失去地狱本身以及其庇主赋予的力量——那股力量本用于完成他们永恒的任务——但作为交换，他们可以随心所欲。许多魔鬼选择按自己的规矩继续作恶，作为自由代理人仅为个人利益进行恶魔式交易。另一些则借此机会摆脱地狱那种僵硬恶意的影响，变成与过去完全不同的存在；对那些曾为签约凡人、并成功讨价还价保住记忆与人格的魔鬼而言，他们也许会尝试从中断处重新拾起自己的人生。这些非邪恶的魔鬼前路艰辛，因为凡人早已学会永远不要信任魔鬼——而且理由充分！这些魔鬼的存在并不意味着我们就该对所有魔鬼都抱持善意，但它给了我们一个教训：有时候，即便是魔鬼，也值得一点同情。

— 艾瑟莉娅·尼克斯，空间元素的使者，怪物的辩护人

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
Devil’s Advocate

Devils, as it turns out, tend to be endlessly clever and conniving and more than loquacious enough not to need someone else to advocate on their behalf. At least, that’s usually the case. But sometimes, their cleverness, scheming, lawyering, and loophole wriggling get them into an unusual situation where the lines are much less clear.

We hear a lot about infernal contracts between mortals and devils where the mortal gets some sort of worldly desire in exchange for damning their soul to Hell. But those types of contracts make up only a tiny fraction of all the contracts that move their way through Hell’s infernal bureaucracy, but our fixation on them is understandable: those are the contracts closest to home, the ones that might affect someone we know. For the desperate among us, those are the contracts they might someday consider signing. The other contracts in Hell’s vaults detail the obligations between various devils or factions of devils. Every devil in Hell has at least one contract of servitude detailing their role and place within Hell’s strict hierarchy—for those who began as mortals signing an infernal contract, it might have even been that self same pact they once made that details their new role (those are the wiser of these foolish mortals, who thought ahead to bargain a better place in Hell for themselves, rather than let the devils choose as they may).

Regardless of the contract, one of Hell’s many labyrinthine legal loopholes, set by infernal precedent, is ruthlessly exploiting the so-called "four corners" principle that oral agreements are irrelevant and only the fine print of the contract matters. While this is usually used to trick mortals or unwise devils who don’t understand the fine print, the same principle can be a salvation: if you destroy the contract’s written copy in Hell, you can’t be held to the contract any longer, regardless of the oral agreement. This small detail has been the subject of multiple bardic epics involving a heroic journey to Hell to save a well-intentioned loved one’s soul from an infernal contract. And truly, it’s a task for the greatest of heroes to engage in such a heist.

What we don’t hear about as often are devils who, already living in Hell, have a leg up in destroying the contracts that bind them to servitude and empower them to fulfill their role. Sometimes, all it takes is knowing the right devil, making a suitable bribe, or having access or knowledge of the layout to make what would be an epic heist for mortals a relatively pedestrian affair for a well-connected devil.

What happens when a devil becomes unbound by all contracts and ties to Hell varies from devil to devil. They give up the strength that Hell itself and their patron give them to accomplish their eternal tasks, but in exchange, they can do whatever they want. Many choose to continue their devilish ways on their own terms, making diabolic bargains as free agents for personal gain alone. Others use the opportunity to cast off Hell’s influence of rigid malevolence and become something totally unlike their former self; for those who were contracted mortals who managed to bargain to keep their memories and personality intact, they might try to pick up their life where it left off. These non-evil devils have a harsh road ahead of them, as mortals have learned never to trust a devil—and with good reason! That such devils exist doesn’t mean we have to give all devils the benefit of the doubt, but it teaches us a lesson: sometimes, even the devil deserves a little sympathy.

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Devil Ancestry.name: 魔鬼族裔 Devil Ancestry
Devil Ancestry.text:
Contractless devils are otherworldly fiends who freed themselves from Hell’s rigid hierarchy through cunning, guile, and the theft and destruction of their own infernal contract.

It’s often said that only a fool would ever trust a devil. That they speak with forked tongues, uttering gilded words in the same breath as they weave deceits. That they twist hopes and dreams into a baited trap. That they tempt and scheme, defile and corrupt. That they are architects of torment, as foul and as remorseless as death itself.

Few devils can conceive of finer compliments than these.

Much of this is truth. Devils are practiced liars and cunning manipulators, as adept at toppling nations with words as they are through their strength of arms. They’re ambitious and covetous, always striving to ascend through the ranks of Hell’s rigid hierarchy, yet willing to wait patiently for the perfect moment to strike when it’s necessary to do so to succeed. They’re experts at finding the weak points and exceptions—the chink in the armor, the crack in the wall, the loophole in the contract, the slumbering sin in the virtuous. They recognize when to tempt, when to threaten, when to bargain, and when to seize. One should never trust a devil—other devils certainly don’t!

To a devil, spoken words are simply flourishes, gambits, and tricks—they have no weight on their own and hold no innate value. They’re intangible and impermanent—simply a means to confound, convey, and influence. A deal spoken aloud has no intrinsic power. It’s the written word that has worth. Only written words can be cataloged, sorted, and reviewed. Written words alone are binding and enforceable. It’s this belief that has given rise to perhaps the most iconic of all devil trappings: the infernal contract.

Most mortals think of an infernal contract as a legally binding document wherein a mortal damns their soul to Hell in exchange for some worldly desire or minor miracle. In truth, these contracts negotiated between mortals and devils are only a fraction of the contracts that flow through Hell’s archives. Every devil has a contract that explains their place in Hell’s hierarchy, outlines their duties and responsibilities, and guarantees their eternal servitude. Regardless of topic or intent, all infernal contracts are stored in Hell and are riddled with complex vernacular, abbreviated citations, footnotes, fine print, and hidden loopholes. As a profane document, they’re magically binding and can’t be duplicated or altered—though they can be destroyed.

Yet, even an infernal contract isn’t infallible. Thanks to the four-corners principle, oral agreements are unenforceable in Hell’s bureaucracy, and that means only the contents of the contract—and their exact wording—matter. Usually, this legal precedent is used to entrap unobservant or unwise signatories, but it also means that if one’s infernal contract is destroyed, the contract is no longer binding—a surprising path to freedom. Of course, finding, stealing, and destroying a specific infernal contract amid Hell’s vast bureaucracy is easier said than done, and most who attempt this audacious act die in the attempt.

Even success is not without repercussions. At a fundamental level, infernal contracts grant power in exchange for service. This is doubly so for devils, who are empowered by Hell itself to aid them in the execution of their assigned duties and tasks. With the destruction of a devil’s infernal contract, they lose this strength and all other perks granted to them for their servitude. A contractless devil earns their freedom at the expense of their power. Yet, they also become unbound by the strictures and hierarchy of Hell—they have the potential to become more than they ever were or could be. That such a transformation could take millennia is of little consequence to a devil, as they retain their immortality and are immeasurably patient. Time is on their side.

If you want to play a cunning schemer with a dark past, you should play a devil.

You Might...

  • Enjoy manipulating others to your own benefit, to the point that you do it without thinking.
  • Get everything in writing and consider only written contracts and promises binding—and only for as long as that contract exists. Words mean nothing.
  • Assess everything from all sides, consider every outcome, and plan for every contingency.
  • Prefer to deal in favors and labor rather than in simple things like coin or treasure.

Others Probably...

  • Assume you’re untrustworthy and corrupt.
  • Trust you to find the flaws and loopholes in everything.
  • Become confused by the complex details of your convoluted plans or grandiose speeches.

Physical Description

Devils come in countless forms, from the mindless lemure to the angelic erinyes to the skeletal osyluths and everything in between. While a devil’s form reflects their place in Hell’s hierarchy, and their form can change over the eons of servitude in accordance with their ascension and fall through Hell’s ranks, a devil’s body is still their own. Thus, when a devil’s infernal contract is destroyed, they retain their current form. Free from Hell’s strictures, these devils are free to make their way across the world and through the planes as they please.

As diverse as devils are, they do share commonalities. Devils are otherworldly immortals with a palpable presence. They exude menace and feel foul. They’re entrancing but undeniably dangerous—like watching a predator far stronger than oneself, with full knowledge that it could consume you. They have hardened, fire-resistant flesh and usually possess at least one predatory feature, such as talons, fangs, horns, spines, or a sharp tail. Most devils have baleful red eyes, though other colors are possible and even likely for some heritages like gelugons.

Erinyes and phistophili look the most like mundane mortals, with human-like bodies and slight otherworldly features. Erinyes have angelic wings with feathers black as pitch, while phistophili are tall, with large curving horns and a slender tail with a triangular tip, and have patches of scales running along their arms, hands, calves, and feet.

Barbazus are strong and muscular, with curved horns, sharp teeth, goat’s legs, and a long wriggling beard they use to impale their foes in combat. The most powerful of barbazus can even use their beard to infect their enemies with debilitating illnesses and afflictions, including the dangerous disease named Avernal fever.

Cornugons are hulking and muscular, significantly larger and bulkier than barbazus, with scaly skin that tends toward red or brown but sometimes takes another color depending on the cornugon's specific proclivities and talents. A cornugon's feet split into two to three spiked digits, and they have a long reptilian tail, but their namesake feature as "horned devils" is their crownlike set of powerful horns. Some cornugons also possess larger dragonlike wings, although the size and presence of these wings can vary among individual cornugons.

Deimaviggas typically appear something like a suit of empty armor, with long clawed blades protruding from their gauntleted hands. Their head is a disembodied helmet floating slightly above the top of their empty body. However, not all deimaviggas choose to clad themselves in armor. Whatever garments they wear, whether robes or finery, they appear to be hollow accoutrements, at least when not assuming a mortal guise. Some believe this appearance is the cosmos's way of hinting at the ultimate hollowness of a traditional hellbound deimavigga's beautiful lies. Deimaviggas who aren't outfitted in gear still aren't invisible. Without clothing to define their shape, the contours of their body are indicated by floating infernal glyphs spelling out the diabolic lies that make up their form, almost like tattoos, which are normally covered by their clothing or armor. These glyphs create gaps in the neck and joints, mirroring the gaps visible in a deimavigga wearing clothing.

Hamatulas are humanoid in form but bestial in appearance, with a long tail, pointed ears, and vicious fangs. Their most prominent feature is the thick coat of namesake barbs and spines that covers their body from the top of their head to the tip of their tail and toes, with most spines over six inches long. Hamatulas can adjust the arrangement and positioning of their barbs to an extent, which can help them sit on chairs, fit into armor, or perform other tasks where being covered entirely in spined barbs would put them at a disadvantage.

Gelugons appear as large insectile monstrosities with long antennae, sharp mandibles, and four legs. The color of a gelugon's carapace ranges across shades of gray, blue, or purple, though pale blue is the most common color for a typical gelugon. Gelugons' rare icy cousins, cocytans, or betrayal devils, appear as humanoids covered in ice, though they otherwise share many of the same abilities in common with their insectile gelugon brethren.

Osyluths are large and skeletal—with a fleshless body formed out of literal bone. They have an elongated neck, vicious mandibles, and bat-like wings. Most impressive is their incredibly long tail, which is tipped with a vicious stinger.

Devil Adventurers

Contractless devil adventurers are incredibly varied in their preferences of class, with differences across both heritages and sects. Most avoid any class that forces them into the service of another, such as cleric and witch—contract chasers are an exception to this rule. Likewise, devils rarely care for the natural world and avoid classes that revolve around it, such as druids and rangers. Dynamos prefer classes that enable them to strive for personal greatness, such as fighter, magus, monk, swashbuckler, and wizard. Hierarchs prefer classes that offer structure, such as monk and wizard. Mortalbound pursue the same classes they remember from their mortal life. Most other devils favor classes that allow them to influence or manipulate others or uncover secrets, notably bard, investigator, rogue, and thaumaturge. Devils tend to have backgrounds that match the role they held in Hell or the role they’re currently pursuing, with barrister, bounty hunter, emissary, and criminal particularly common.

Society

Free-willed devils consider themselves superior to both mortals and other devils, believing themselves smarter, cleverer, and infinitely more patient. Of course, pride is common among all devils, so this opinion is hardly surprising.

As devils are well-versed in the intricacies of mortal life—all the better to tempt mortals into Hell’s service after all—most devils adapt quickly to living a life beyond the boundaries of Hell. They recognize their body’s new needs and understand how to interact with and function within the mortal world around them. After a short adjustment period, during which a devil assesses their new strengths, weaknesses, and limitations, they come into their own and begin to build a new life—though what form such a life takes varies from devil to devil.

Most devils enjoy bargains and bartering, concessions, and caveats, even when they aren't strictly necessary. They take joy in examining details, participating in clever negotiations, and seizing opportunities. Most devils quickly learn to appreciate the advantages they hold over their neighbors, primarily the time available to them. As immortals, devils are in no rush to see their complex (and often convoluted) plans come to fruition and can easily play the long game. Even so, a newly contractless devil is painfully aware of how much weaker they are than before they severed their ties to Hell. During their first few hundred years beyond Hell's borders, most devils lean toward caution—this precaution ensures that they don't draw the attention of their past enemies nor local "heroes" or other meddlers. They tend to keep a low profile (or, at least, as low a profile as a devil can while walking the mortal world). In time, as a devil regains some of their lost powers or better adjusts to their surroundings, they become bolder.

Regardless of age and experience, devils approach everything with calm and patience. They consider every situation and evaluate every problem from all sides, striving to foresee every potential outcome and to plan for every contingency. Devils understand that haste and emotion can easily result in one’s downfall, and so they try to remain objective and observant.

Devils don’t trust other devils, yet they desire the company of one another in small doses. Whether one was a soldier or bureaucrat, life in Hell’s legions is busy and oppressive but also communal. There are lots of other devils and souls around to use as targets for bullying, reluctant ears for bragging, and co-conspirators for scheming. Outside of Hell, devils grow to miss this interaction—even if the people they once interacted with were their enemies. Like a human surrounded by insects, devils are immortals surrounded by weaker beings. While most devils appreciate and enjoy their superiority, they also grow bored when no one else proves a suitable challenge.

Thus, as the centuries roll on, devils become more and more likely to reach out to one another and make contact—not as friends or as enemies, but as competitors. They form long-distance communities that gather semi-frequently to socialize and show off at private social events—much like an elite social club. They strive to impress and outdo one another—usually over how many mortals they corrupt, what following and minions they’ve gathered, or their devious schemes. They plot, plan, and jockey for position, aiming to improve their standing among their fellow devils, just as they once schemed and battled for ranking in Hell’s hierarchy. Most devils form their very first friendships at such events—though few devils would ever admit to possessing such a mundane and sentimental weakness as a friend.

Heritages

How a contractless devil reacts to their new life often depends greatly on their devil heritage. The following descriptions are for the eight most common devil heritages.

Barbazus

Barbazus serve as the foot soldiers of Hell’s armies. They march into battle without fear, fighting alongside platoons of lesser devils and heeding the commands of those greater. They revel in the thrill of battle, the spilling of blood, and the snuffing of lives. Freed from Hell’s hierarchy, barbazus are free to satiate their blood lust however they desire. Often, this drives barbazus into the dark, shady underbelly of the world, where they indulge in violence and destruction and revel in misery and anguish. Many barbazus become murderers, serial killers, or bandits, with those with loftier ambitions becoming the commander of a mercenary unit or a tyrannical warlord. Barbazus who thirst for battle but favor less foul methods of satiating their need for bloodshed, lean toward the life of a gladiator or adventurer.

Cornugons

Cornugons are the commanders of Hell’s armies, forged and reforged in the fires of Hell from the fiercest warriors among devils. They inspire their diabolic armies through fear, and they attain their positions by enduring torment, both their own and that of others. The reforging process is intensely painful, but most cornugons undergo it willingly, knowing that every few millennia, the most powerful among them will become malebranche, infernal legends known as Malebranche. But this enticement isn’t enough for every cornugon. Some grow resentful of the pain they must endure, all for the unattainable promise of some far-off promotion. They realize that the only way to escape the shackles of Hell’s order is to reject the entire framework; the only way to win a rigged game is not to play it at all. It can be hard for a cornugon to surrender their vaunted position as commander after they’ve worked so hard and endured so much pain to achieve it, but the unexpected betrayal of one so highly ranked also makes it easier for them to find and access their infernal contract in order to destroy it. Contractless cornugons typically try to find leadership positions in the mortal realm. Some businesses or militaries that lack scruples are eager to hire a hellspawn to lead their ranks, believing this will boost efficiency, regardless of whether the cornugon terrorizes their subordinates. On the other hand, cornugons who want to turn over a new leaf might find it harder to locate trustworthy employers or companions.

Deimaviggas

Deimaviggas are among the most solitary of devilkind, existing somewhat apart from Hell’s hierarchy. While pit fiends and unique devils can issue orders to a deimavigga when necessary, in practice most deimaviggas operate completely independently and spend their time in the mortal realm sowing the seeds of heresy and apostasy. As the front line in Hell’s information warfare, deimaviggas spend much more time among mortals than they do among their fellow devils. For the most part, such activity results in thousands of successful missions, corrupting even the purest and most sanctified of mortal souls. But an exchange of ideas, even one undertaken in bad faith, requires connecting with other people and learning to understand them. And once in a million times or fewer, that connection can touch the deimavigga fundamentally enough to cause an “apostate devil” to instead turn apostate against Hell, changing their views away from Hell’s orthodoxy instead of twisting the beliefs of others. Because they’re so independent and inscrutable to begin with, when a deimavigga manages to break their contract and become contractless, it can often take Hell a long time before they actually figure it out. In at least one case, a contractless deimavigga who replaced their real contract with a convincing fake managed to trick Hell into believing they were still serving their role for centuries. While their actions were confusing to other devils who happened to hear about them, those devils assumed it was all part of Hell’s plans.

Erinyes

The first erinyes were said to be fallen angels, cast out of Heaven and condemned to Hell for their sins and their temper, lest their ideas spread among the heavenly host. Whether this is truth or lie is long lost to history and buried in dogma. However, it’s a proven fact that today, erinyes are most often risen from the souls of the damned, transformed through infernal contract, or promoted as they rise through the ranks of Hell’s hierarchy. In Hell, erinyes are the executors of justice—the hand raised to extract bloody punishment upon a creature or soul for their crimes and sins. They are Hell’s torturers—a task from which most derive sadistic satisfaction. Erinyes tend to be stern and unrelenting while creative and fond of poetic irony. They have a fiery temper and can fly into a fury with little provocation. Contractless erinyes tend to remain focused on passing judgment or enacting punishment upon others. Thanks to their angelic appearance, they most easily find mainstream acceptance among mortals. While most erinyes become judges, lawmakers, executioners, torturers, or members of law enforcement, erinyes who embrace their independence instead drift toward the life of a vigilante, dealing out violent and unrelenting justice on their own terms and based on their own criteria.

Gelugons

Despite their insectile appearance, gelugons are brilliant and intuitive strategists, and many once served as the tacticians and commanders of Hell’s armies. They’re contemplative masterminds, yet their musings are never ponderous, as they can rapidly assess a situation and devise a cunning counter-response. To a gelugon, life is a complex strategy game, and they’re always thinking ten steps ahead of their competition, preparing countermeasures and contingencies, and intuiting the behavior of their foes before their foes even know what they’ll do. This has instilled in gelugons strong feelings of superiority and unwavering pride—traits which cause them to isolate themselves from others. Gelugons find life predictable and are rarely impressed or surprised. When they do experience moments of shock or wonder, they tend to become temporarily fascinated with the source of this surprise, whether it be a topic, object, location, or individual. This can be dangerous for the subject of their fixation, as a gelugon’s interest is counterbalanced by their hatred of failure. Contractless gelugons tend to occupy hidden positions of leadership, such as the leader of a criminal organization, the advisor to royalty, or a military strategist.

Hamatulas

Hamatulas serve as the guardians of Hell, protecting its countless vaults and archives from intruders. This gives hamatulas who attempt to destroy their infernal contract a distinct advantage over their fiendish brethren. Ironically, hamatulas enjoy their eternal vigil and are among the least likely of all devils to destroy their own infernal contract. Contractless hamatulas usually spend a short time embracing their freedom before instinctively setting themselves up as guardians of something. What they choose to protect varies but often includes a location, an object, or an individual. Regardless of what they protect, many hamatulas enjoy owning material possessions, leading them to become hoarders or collectors. Thanks to their continual obsession with better protecting their charge, hamatulas tend toward isolationism and paranoia.

Osyluths

Osyluths are religious fanatics who serve as the spiritual guides and inquisitors for Hell’s fiendish legions. Those who serve within Hell seek out the impious and disrespectful and drive them into the infernal fold. Those who serve outside of Hell track down and destroy the propagators of heresies, as well as any who would interfere with Hell’s plans. Regardless of posting, osyluths take sadistic pleasure from inflicting torments on heretics and traitors. Contractless osyluths are, by default, heretics themselves, having destroyed their own infernal contract in an act of defiance. Most osyluths have trouble reconciling this fact, with some even deluding themselves into believing they still work for Hell in a more freeform capacity. To compensate, some contractless osyluths hunt heretics, and those they believe might "hinder Hell’s machinations" with a fervor unmatched in their previous life. Their prime targets usually include religious leaders and other contractless devils—a fact that doesn’t endear them to their infernal contemporaries. Those osyluths who resist this urge tend to become interrogators, bounty hunters, detectives, or religious leaders.

Phistophili

Phistophili are the quintessential devil—the silver-tongued manipulator who tempts mortals into signing an infernal contract. They’re the clerks, scribes, lawyers, and bureaucrats within Hell. Once freed from Hell’s hierarchy, they change little, save that they work on behalf of themselves rather than for Hell. A typical contractless phistophilus bargains, convinces, and manipulates their way into power, expanding their influence with each new signatory. Most phistophili prefer to keep their growing network a secret, or at least subtle, enabling them to manipulate others from the shadows or in plain sight. Phistophili who choose to maintain a public presence often do so as lawyers, merchants, investigators, or politicians.

Devil Settlements

Devils rarely form settlements, with those few in existence almost entirely inhabited by hierarchs. Instead, devils communicate with one another over long distances and then gather (infrequently) at social gatherings. Messages are typically communicated magically or dispatched as coded missives. Devils sending such letters are always careful never to commit, or accidentally imply commitment, to anything in writing, lest their written words be used against them in the future. Meanwhile, social gatherings are a lofty affair, much like a high-end social club, with the devils in attendance constantly trying to one-up each other and subtly show off.

Sects

“Sects” is an unofficial term used by infernal scholars to categorize the myriad ways in which a contractless devil might navigate their life in the mortal realm. Each sect lives life by a different philosophy. The following are the seven most common sects.

Contract Chasers

Some devils deeply regret their decision to destroy their infernal contract and strive to re-enter Hell’s service. Insultingly nicknamed contract chasers, these devils are much maligned and hated by other devils, both contracted and contractless alike, who generally assume that contract chasers lack determination or drive. Unfortunately for contract chasers, it’s difficult to re-enter Hell’s service, particularly when one lacks a soul with which to barter. Rarely does a devil within Hell’s bureaucracy offer an infernal contract to a contract chaser, as all contractless devils have already proven themselves incapable of abiding by Hell’s rules and respecting the terms of their service. Furthermore, a contract chaser lacks the ability to reinsert themselves into Hell’s hierarchy, even if they found their way physically back into Hell. Thus, contract chasers go to greater and greater lengths to contact other devils or perform acts of service for other devils, hoping to earn their way back into Hell’s good graces eventually. Occasionally, the devil who penned the infernal contract that the contract chaser broke may give them a second chance—with horrible terms of service in a miserable form, on the very bottom of Hell’s hierarchy. While other devils often look down upon contract chasers, and they can come off as desperate and obsessive, this doesn’t mean they’re weak. In fact, one of the most effective ways for a contract chaser to earn back their position in Hell is to make themselves such a thorn in Hell’s side that other devils offer them a place in Hell just to get them out of the way.

Dynamos

Dynamos are devils who deeply miss the strength and powers they once possessed while in Hell’s service and become obsessed with re-attaining their lost power. Deep down, most dynamos fear that their old enemies—now much stronger than they are—will hunt them down and destroy them. While some dynamos focus only on physical strength or magical capabilities, most dynamos strive to increase both aspects in varying degrees through intensive training and study or by questing for powerful relics, mystical boons, and magic wishes. In either case dynamos usually stand among the most personally powerful devils, as they rarely stop their efforts when they achieve their goals, instead pushing themselves further and harder until they far surpass their old glory and limitations. Despite their impressive forms and power, dynamos typically struggle with their confidence and sense of personal value, feeling inferior or insignificant compared to the might of Hell or their past selves. Dynamos tend to cover this inner turmoil with bombastic behavior and obnoxious displays of bravado, becoming known as braggarts, gloaters, and egotists. As they grow in strength, other contractless devils often hold up dynamos as paragons of their kind, renowned for their achievements.

Hierarchs

Life in Hell is one of strict rules and rigid obedience. Free from this oppressive, overbearing structure, most devils flourish, becoming something entirely new based on their own interests and desires. Despite being free from Hell’s rule, hierarchs are devils who find the mortal realm too chaotic and long for more order in their life. In short, hierarchs miss Hell’s rigid hierarchy and strive to impose order on the world around them. Hierarchs usually span the spectrum from bureaucrats to enforcers to tyrants. Often, hierarchs attempt to impose order on the mortals or mortal societies around them. Sometimes, hierarchs gather instinctively with other devils and naturally form a new hierarchy, either modeled after Hell or entirely of their own invention. In either instance, hierarchs tend to be organized, overbearing, and controlling. They often strive for grand plans, so they see dissent or disagreement as an impediment or obstacle to their grand vision.

Mortalbound

Mortalbound are contractless devils who were once mortal and still retain their mortal memories and personality. While this strange and unique circumstance is sometimes a fluke, error, or oversight, mortalbound usually attained their state purposefully by bargaining while signing their infernal contract to ensure they retained their identity after death. Mortalbound remember their past life and try to reintegrate into it, picking up life where they left off. Unfortunately for a mortalbound, achieving this is far more challenging than they expect. Mortalbound have trouble being believed and trusted—who could truly believe that the osyluth who just moved in next door is the same person as the old wizard who died there last year? Those who do prove their identity still face an uphill battle, as their presence makes people uncomfortable, and it’s incredibly hard for their family, friends, and loved ones to come to terms with their transformation, with their relationships shattering or changing completely.

Mortalbound who seek to retain the power and possessions of their mortal life and ignore their past friends and family mind these hardships little and tend to be happy with their new life. However, mortalbound who seek to retain the families, lovers, and friendships they forged in life find these hardships heartbreaking, and tend to regret their transformation deeply, or lash out at those they once loved who now reject them.

Redeemed

Redeemed includes all those devils who cast off Hell’s influence of rigid malevolence and start a new life on their own terms. They rebel by becoming "good"—perhaps virtuous, charitable, or kind—and unlike their former self. They purposefully break the mold, shatter taboos, and push boundaries. Sometimes, this change is an affectation that never quite reaches their twisted soul. Often, it’s a positive change undertaken for the wrong reasons, with the act of rebellion as the main driving impetus for the devil, rather than introspective reflection and a stronger moral compass. Sometimes, the change is heartfelt and true, and the devil truly becomes redeemed. Such devils have a harsh road to acceptance and redemption. For, after all, who could trust a devil? Redeemed devils whose change is true tend to be brooders who deeply regret their past actions and strive to make up for their sins. Redeemed devils whose change is only skin deep tend to be bold, boisterous, and rebellious and to harbor deep resentment toward Hell and its servants.

Seculars

Seculars aim to acquire power and shape the mortal world into their own image—literally—by making themselves an object of worship. Seculars typically use subterfuge to achieve their goals by imitating a god or controlling a high-ranking priest and tricking an already established congregation into worshiping them. Sometimes, they instead corrupt the pious through temptation and gifts, leading priests or worshipers on a slow descent into evil and making them reliant upon the devil, thereby attaining a following who knows what they’re doing and to whom they owe their fealty but who don’t usually enjoy their predicament. The boldest of seculars openly seek to be worshiped as themselves, either appearing to a group of diabolists or forming a cult of personality around themselves. Seculars who become cult leaders attempt to maintain a benevolent, wise, understanding, or indulgent public persona meant to attract followers and build a respectable reputation. Sometimes, it’s a bit more than a facade, and the secular actually seeks to build a better life for their worshipers, though even then, mainly to further their own ego and spread their cult of personality further. After all, the worshiper’s fate and prosperity can be seen as a reflection of the quality of the secular’s teachings and wisdom.

Wellsprings

Devils understand and respect the power of a contract, and most continue to rely upon them throughout their lives, forging deals, bargaining, or otherwise asserting their ownership or power through signed contracts. Wellspring devils take this one step further, gaining personal power through forging contracts with mortals. Free from Hell’s control, wellsprings continue their devilish ways on their own terms, making diabolic bargains as free agents or for personal gain alone, empowering themselves as they once empowered Hell. Contract by contract, they add to their own strength and influence. As a drip becomes a trickle becomes a rushing river over time, so too does such a devil become a wellspring of power. Wellsprings owe their success to their cunning, charm, forked tongue, and talent at negotiation. Most go to great lengths to ensure the safety and defense of their contracts, lest the loophole in the four corners principle that set them free work in someone else’s favor.

Beliefs

You can use the following tendencies to guide you in choosing an alignment if you’re playing with alignment in your game or to assist with characterization for devils even if you aren’t. It should come as no surprise that devils tend toward acts of evil, with lying, entrapment, and violence ranking among the most commonly expressed of their foul deeds. Some devil sects, notably redeemed and mortalbound, change their lives entirely once they’re free from the tyranny of Hell. These devils transform into good, honorable members of society—or at least, they cease to be despicable villains—but they are not the exception. Though some contractless devils remain devoted to a life of rigid structure and tyranny much like the one they always knew, most tend toward following the rules and laws that suit them and ignoring or altering those that don’t.

Contractless devils rarely show interest in matters of faith, save to curse and snub those gods who they blame for their past servitude. Seculars instead turn themselves into objects of worship, forming cults and religions based around themselves, while mortalbound tend to continue to worship the same gods they worshiped in their mortal life. Dynamos sometimes lean towards gods of self-perfection, or who possess the traits they seek to re-attain, while redeemed sometimes adopt the worship of goodly gods whose tenets they wish to emulate.

Popular Edicts embrace your freedom, seize power, use guile to take advantage of others

Popular Anathemas betray another contractless devil to their enemies, perform an act of charity without a self-serving reason, re-enter Hell’s service

Names

Like many fiends, devils possess a "true name," which they keep secret, as anyone who knows a devil’s true name gains power over them. True names tend to be a single, long name, of at least three syllables. In addition, devils possess the name they used in Hell—usually dubbed a "Hell name" by contractless devils—and an epithet that describes their deeds. Some contractless devils continue to use this name, while others abandon it completely, selecting a new name for themselves, free from Hell’s influence. Regardless, devils prefer old-fashioned, grand-sounding names and epithets that instill fear or celebrate a devil’s glory.

Sample Names

Albion, Barakil, Hasorit, Kezrazorn, Lulandra, Runarifer, Seldarrin, Zenforix.

Sample Titles

Cursed One, Bringer of Death, Destroyer, Grand Temptation, Herald of Lies, Petulant Sin, Vilifier.

What About Celestials?

The vast majority of devils are incredibly self-centered, and once they have destroyed their infernal contract and severed their connection to Hell, they tend to forget about celestials entirely. They harbor no hatred against angels, archons, azatas, or other celestial beings simply for being who they are. That said, they do hold grudges against anyone who moves against them or interferes with their plans—celestials included. Grudges held against immortals—including celestials and other devils—tend to be slow-burning but respectful, with the enemies occasionally becoming akin to colleagues or friends, if only due to prolonged contact and mutual respect. This doesn’t mean that the pair will ever agree with one another or change one another, only that, by virtue of exposure, devils become accustomed to the presence of their enemies and come to secretly enjoy their interactions. Oddly, despite the shared immortality, most devils don’t consider undead worthwhile socializing partners.

Devil Heritages

Each heritage of devil is incredibly distinct. Choose one of the following six devil heritages at 1st level, which includes your size. If you choose a different heritage without a listed size, such as a versatile heritage, your devil is typically Medium, though your GM can grant you permission to be Large instead, as best suits your character background concept.

Contract Clauses

Just because contractless devils escaped their own infernal contracts, it doesn’t mean they’re any less eager to continue creating contracts in their new life. While they’re careful to read the fine print and ensure that they don’t wind up on the bad end of a deal, many contractless devils continue using the tricks they learned back in Hell to create confusing contract clauses that put them at an advantage.

Two Heads Are Better Than One.name: 两个头胜过一个 Two Heads Are Better Than One
Two Heads Are Better Than One.text:
摘自艾瑟莉娅·尼克斯的档案
两颗头胜过一颗

那些喜欢自称为“人”的我们，常常被我们选择贴上“怪物”标签的最强大存在所碾压。我们宣称自己能战胜这些渺茫胜算的方法，是拥有无与伦比的团队合作。当然，这同样不过是赤裸裸的自我中心在作祟。世上有许多族裔更有资格宣称能通过协作获得力量，而其中尤以一种出人意料的族裔——融合——为甚。融合在表面上与群血族有些粗略的相似：群血族是由大量彼此相连的个体构成的单一整体；但融合比群血族要分离且个体化得多，而且大多数融合与群血族也不认为彼此是并列的存在。融合的各个成员在肉体与魔法能力上差异极大，且完全不与他者共享。他们并不共享相同的心智通路。他们所共享的，是身体的某些部分——通常是躯干与双腿。这意味着，从本质上说，他们是恰好一起旅行的不同之人，类似关系极为亲密的兄弟姐妹。这些肉体与形而上学的标志定义了融合这一族裔；而在此之外，融合之间仍存在鲜明的外形差异——若不是学术著作中呈现的魔法图式，你很难说服我：一个双头巨人、一只刻耳柏洛斯，以及一条九头蛇，竟都应被归类为同一族裔的一部分！

不用说，那些喜欢自称为“正常”的族裔，往往难以决定究竟该如何与融合互动。熟悉群血族、乔科里或其他倾向复数性族裔的人，可能会误把融合当作一个单一系统；而对其他族裔更不熟悉的人，甚至会错得更离谱，把融合当作一个孤独的自我。我们对融合的缺乏理解，使我们将他们视为怪物——尽管我们重视家庭、友谊与群体团结，而这些正是成功的融合所体现的一切。有些人因此不信任或畏惧融合。另一些人则出于嫉妒；他们在误解之下，竟开始羡慕融合的成就——仿佛那是任何一个人凭一己之力都无法达成的。但对理解融合真实本质的人而言，这不该令人意外。毕竟，两颗头胜过一颗！

—— 艾瑟莉娅·尼克斯，空间元素的先驱，怪物代言人

From the Archives of Aitheria Nyx
Two Heads Are Better Than One

Those who like to call ourselves “people” are often outclassed by the mightiest beings we choose to label “monsters.” We claim that the way we overcome these long odds is by possessing unparalleled teamwork. Of course, that, too, is sheer egocentrism at play. There’s many ancestries out there with a far greater claim to power through teamwork, and none more so than the unlikely ancestry known as fusions. Fusions bear some cursory surface similarity to swarmbloods, which are a single whole made up of a large interconnected system of individuals, but fusions are significantly more separate and individual than swarmbloods, and most fusions and swarmbloods don’t see themselves as parallel. The individual members of a fusion have dramatically different physical and magical capabilities that aren’t shared with the others at all. They don’t share the same mental pathways. What they do share is certain portions of their body, typically the torso and legs. This means that they are in essence separate people who happen to travel together, akin to very close siblings. These physical and metaphysical hallmarks define the fusion ancestry, which otherwise bears stark physical differences—if it weren’t for magical schemas presented in scholarly works, you’d have a hard time convincing me that a two-headed giant, a cerberus, and a hydra should all be classified as part of the same ancestry!

Needless to say, the ancestries who like to call ourselves “normal” often have trouble deciding exactly how to interact with fusions. Those familiar with swarmbloods, chochori, or other ancestries with a tendency towards plurality might incorrectly treat a fusion as a singular system, while those who are even less familiar with other ancestries might be even more mistaken, treating a fusion as a solitary self. Our lack of understanding of fusions makes us see them as monstrous, even as we value family, friendship, and group solidarity, all things that successful fusions embody. Some mistrust or fear fusions as a result. Others are jealous, in their misunderstanding coming to envy fusions for their accomplishments, seemingly more than any one person could achieve on their own. But it shouldn’t come as any surprise to those who understand a fusion’s true nature. After all, two heads are better than one!

— Aitheria Nyx, Herald of the Space Element, Advocate of Monsters

Fusion Ancestry.name: 融合 族裔 Fusion Ancestry
Fusion Ancestry.text:
整体大于部分之和。世上再没有任何存在比被称为“融合体”的奇异血统更能证明这句格言。

无论是人类、巨龙，甚至是地城的化身，各个血统都由个体组成；血统中的每一名成员都应当是一只单独且明确的生物……至少按理说应当如此。然而，有些传承由多个个体同处一具身体组成，这种现象在物质与形而上层面的含义，连最睿智的哲学家都感到困惑。由于这些传承彼此差异巨大，一些主流学者甚至认为它们完全无关；但血统学者最近将这些“格式塔”实体归类为一个推测性的单一血统，并称之为“融合体”。这些融合体外观上可能天差地别，就像巨树地城、地下迷宫地城与群岛地城之间千差万别的表型一样；但正如那些地城传承，融合体共享着更深层的相似之处：融合体是多只生物共享同一具身体。

你可能会……

  • 有时会在危机中该如何行动这件事上，与融合体中的其他角色争论。
  • 无论出于选择还是迫于必要，学会与他人共享并协调。
  • 即使被孤立也能泰然处之，因为你从不真正孤单。

其他人很可能会……

  • 忘记或无法理解你其实是多个人，而不是单一个体。
  • 钦佩你的融合体那种纯粹的破坏力——来自其多次行动或合并活动——并错误地把你与另一只单独的生物相提并论。
  • 把你误认为融合体中的另一个成员，尤其当你未穿戴可区分的道具时。

生理描述

融合体是体型庞大的存在，拥有多个头颅；每个头颅都是一只独立的生物，共享同一具身体。它们的体态与其他生理细节会因传承而剧烈不同，差异大到学者花了数百年才因其形而上的相似性将其归类为同一血统。除多个头颅外，融合体另一个明显标志，是其身体某一部分覆盖着拼接的装备，使融合体内的各个独立生物可以根据自身职业穿戴不同的道具。

双头巨人外观大致类人，拥有两个头，并有两条或四条手臂；每个头都是一只独立生物，分别控制一半手臂（两臂中的一臂或四臂中的两臂）。

刻耳柏洛斯呈现为强壮巨大的三头犬，带有一种异界般的气质。它们能从身体上显现最多六条类似手臂的附肢，通常以锁链的形态出现；尽管外观如此，这些锁链仍能握持并操控物体。每个头都是一只独立生物，分别控制刻耳柏洛斯的两条附肢。

奇美拉拥有强健的四足躯体与三个多样的头颅，这些头看起来像属于不同种类的生物；传统上分别为山羊头、狮子头与龙头。然而，不同奇美拉的头颅组合并不相同。一些传奇奇美拉的尾巴上还有第四个蛇头；尽管融合体奇美拉并不符合该传说，但它们在身体各处有六条通常呈蛇形、可抓握的肢体，使其能够握持并操控物体。即便没有蛇头，奇美拉的这些肢体也通常呈蛇形；这正是学者在将融合体归类为同一血统时最初使用的线索之一。

九头蛇是巨大的四头巨蛇，拥有细长蜿蜒的颈项与庞大的中央躯体。它们能从身体伸出八条蛇形、可抓握的肢体，看起来几乎像一团盘绕的尾巴。它们可以用这些肢体握持并操控物体。每个头都是一只独立生物，分别控制九头蛇四分之一的肢体。

五合体或许是融合体中最不寻常的一支：它们由五个被称为“五合体元件”（quintrite）的活体生物组成。每个五合体元件都拥有带有动物特征的金属躯体，使其看起来隐约像某种构装体或自动机。虽然这些五合体元件可以作为独立的金属动物分别生活，但在必要时，五者也能合并成一种巨大的类人形态，称为五合体（quintron）。组成五合体的每个五合体元件身上都有两个可以握持并操控物体的位置，但这些位置未必靠近类人生物的“手”所在之处。例如，一只大象形态的五合体元件可能在其象鼻与尾巴的位置具备这些部位。当然，当五合体元件折叠并合并为五合体时，其身体部件的位置也可能随之改变。通常，组成五合体的五个五合体元件分别呈现不同动物形态，并且颜色各不相同。

社会

由于融合体外观可能像巨人、犬科或蛇形生物，其他血统的成员有时会错误地认为融合体必然野性且不合群。若他们愿意思考，而非仅凭外表下判断，便会明白事实不可能如此。由于多只彼此独立、各具性格的生物共享同一具身体，融合体从幼年起就被迫学习如何分享、如何沟通、以及如何协作。正因如此，融合体能够成为出人意料地高效的领袖或调停者——至少对于愿意听从一条巨大九头蛇命令或建议的人而言。

融合体冒险者

融合体常常成为冒险者；它们强大的技能组合使其能够出类拔萃，而其他血统的人也更愿意忽略它们的差异。融合体倾向于选择多种不同的职业，并通常挑选能与融合体内其他角色互补的职业，以便更容易协作。它们较不可能拥有那种需要无缝融入其他血统社会的背景（例如酒保），但除此之外，它们的背景差异极大。同一个融合体内的角色，其背景总是彼此相容；这并不意味着背景必须相同或相似，因为每个角色都可能有各自的兴趣与训练，但当涉及它们共享的身体曾位于何处时，它们不能拥有彼此矛盾的背景。

传承

除了因其格式塔本质而共享的特质外，融合体的社会倾向会因传承而大不相同。

双头巨人

双头巨人的生活方式与其他类人生物最为相似，其社会结构在来访者看来也与大多数其他血统颇为相近。有些类人生物的兄弟姐妹几乎形影不离，因此类人生物也能想象双头巨人不过是这种关系更极端的情形。双头巨人的两个自我往往会发展出能良好配合的技能：要么相似且互补，要么差异足以弥补彼此空缺。这并不总是因为他们相处得完美无缺——有时双头巨人的两个自我会走上截然不同的道路，并非有意通过弥补弱点来帮助彼此，而是因为他们彼此看不顺眼，想证明自己有多不同。另一些时候，竞争心促使他们培养互补的技能组合，彼此都想胜过对方。尽管如此，大多数双头巨人的两个自我在非争议性决策之外都相处得相当融洽。

刻耳柏洛斯

刻耳柏洛斯天生倾向于守卫地点或物品，这使它们能与其他守卫型血统（如斯芬克斯与石像鬼）相处融洽。刻耳柏洛斯常常“独自”守卫，但有其他自我相伴，它们从不真正孤单。它们彼此作伴，并通过一起玩耍来缓解任务中的无聊，通常是打闹嬉戏或进行智力竞赛。刻耳柏洛斯拥有敏锐的感官，多数还对音乐有特别的鉴赏力。常常，只要献上一首新歌，就能在它们警惕或戒备陌生人时让其放松下来。有时，某只刻耳柏洛斯厌倦了守卫工作，或渴望聆听更多音乐，便踏上宏大的冒险旅途。其他刻耳柏洛斯偶尔会一边嘀咕这些流浪同类，一边带着几分羡慕注视它们的冒险。

融合体聚居地

对于刻耳柏洛斯与九头蛇而言，大于一个小家庭规模的聚居地十分罕见；但双头巨人常会在丘陵或山脉中形成与世隔绝的村落。奇美拉甚至很少形成家庭群体；而五合体拥有丰富的社会文化，若其数量更多，也愿意建立大型聚居地。虽然许多融合体都是“独居”的（除融合体内的同伴之外），但也有一些会适应在其他血统的聚居地中生活，甚至更偏好住在那里。

奇美拉

由于解剖结构的多样性，奇美拉能生活在最广泛的环境中；其中山地与森林略为更常见。然而，奇美拉在其他气候与环境中也同样常见。例如，沙漠中的奇美拉可能拥有骆驼头、蛇头与一颗天蓝色调的龙头，或是任何其他沙漠动物与生物的组合。由于它们的头与身体是由其他生物拼接而成，奇美拉往往比其他任何融合体都更容易在组成其融合体的个体之间出现巨大差异。这会导致鲜明的意见，而这又可能引发三名个体之间的不和；因此，当它们无法达成一致时，奇美拉会投票决定结果，三票中两票定案，剩下一位同意服从。由于在争论或决策的具体情境下相对容易达成交易或妥协，以拆分任何可能形成的投票联盟，因此这种安排很少导致某一个体在每次投票中都持续失利、而另外两位总是结成同盟。若出现两名个体几乎完全一致的罕见情况，可能使第三名持续落败者心生怨怼；因此从奇美拉整体利益出发，通常最好找到某种方式伸出橄榄枝。

九头蛇

九头蛇常在沼泽与湿地和平生活，捕猎食物，并防御侵入的生物或将其误认为巨龙、以为其拥有巨额财宝而来寻宝的冒险者。那些格外好奇或被环境所迫的九头蛇，会离开泥沼家园去寻求冒险。九头蛇在这些旅程中往往大获成功，因为单独一名九头蛇冒险者就能完成通常需要整个冒险队才能达成的壮举。九头蛇往往个性强烈，融合体内每个个体都有鲜明特征；不过有时当九头蛇的某个头被斩断后，新长出来的头会带有略有不同的观点。

五合体

五合体是看起来近似机器的活体生物，而这种外观并非巧合：据说它们被如此设计，并被赋予生命；其活体金属的五合体元件躯体能够组合形成五合体。根据传说，第一批五合体的原始蓝图是在外位面的都会乌托邦中创造的。然而，最初的五合体之后的个体能够自行繁衍，而这一传承整体通常也并非外位面。它们与任何其他融合体一样，都是凡世的住民。其“乌托邦起源”带来的主要副作用之一，是五合体往往能比人们对由五个不同五合体元件组成的融合体所预期的更容易保持一致、协作更好。讽刺的是，五合体为其五个个体使用“quintrite（五合体元件）”这一术语，而其他传承并无对应的专用术语。这种区别源于五合体天生能够分离为五个五合体元件，并随意重新合并。因此，尽管它们更可能彼此协调并保持一致，五合体也天生具备自主行动能力：若有必要，其五合体元件可分离并远行。五合体与五合体元件在决策与言谈中通常偏好效率、简洁与直截了当；在需要听取五种意见时，这种习惯能让事情保持聚焦。因此它们偶尔会使用极具描述性的绰号，如“精灵弓手”，或不那么好听的“吵闹的人类”。不过，若这些绰号无法进行唯一识别，它们就不会使用；例如面对一群精灵弓手时，“精灵弓手”就帮助不大。

需要两个？

与其他任何血统不同，融合体是由多名玩家的角色共享同一具身体的单一血统！这些角色各自是独立的人，拥有各自的职业与各自的背景；但同一融合体中的两个角色，其背景需要避免相互矛盾。例如，若一个角色的背景是由森林中的动物抚养长大，而另一个角色的背景是作为王宫中的公主被抚养长大，就可能无法调和。虽然它们共享同一具身体与空间，每个角色分别掷骰自己的先攻，分别穿戴、注入并携带自己的魔法物品。对于那些通常不可能在同一身体上同时穿戴的魔法物品，它们会以魔法方式调整以适配融合体角色。例如，一个由法师与战士组成的双头巨人融合体，可能身体左侧穿着魔法长袍，右侧穿着一套重甲。

融合体内的每只生物都是独立的生物，会分别成为目标，拥有各自的生命值与状态。这意味着：融合体中的一只生物可能死亡而其余仍活着；融合体中的一只生物可能被变成石头而融合体其他部分没有；或融合体中的一只生物可能发生变形而其余没有（此时它们看起来像一只怪异的奇美拉）。然而，改变生物体型的效果除非能同时影响融合体内所有生物，否则无法对融合体生效；让九头蛇的一个头变大或变小，并不足以改变整条九头蛇的体型。还可能存在其他类似效果，由GM裁定除非能影响融合体中的所有生物，否则就无法生效。

与任何其他PC一样，通常由不同玩家负责创建并扮演融合体中的每个角色；只要人员合适，这会带来非常有趣的角色扮演体验。然而，在玩家人数少于常规、团队考虑由一名玩家操控多个角色的情况下，让该玩家操控彼此相连的融合体角色会是绝佳选择。例如，在一对一游戏中，单个玩家可能扮演九头蛇的四个头。

融合体综合学说

融合体通常更在意自己与融合体内其他个体的互动方式，而非与其他融合体相处如何，或它们可能形成的社会群体。不过，融合体之间存在一些粗略的哲学取向，学者称之为“综合学说”，用以区分主要思想流派。当然，融合体绝非铁板一块：其内部的个体甚至可能分别来自不同的综合学说！

格式塔派

整体大于部分之和。几乎任何融合体都会同意，但格式塔派将这一原则推向极端：相较于融合体内的其他个体，他们更倾向于将自身意志融入格式塔之中，并以近乎冥想的方式试图消解自我。当融合体内所有个体都是格式塔派时，它们有时会展现惊人的协调性，近似某种诡异的“群体心智”；这会让其他融合体感到毛骨悚然，但对不熟悉融合体典型行为的其他血统成员而言却显得正常。若由一名玩家控制融合体内所有人，除非该玩家想扮演性格冲突，否则让他们全都成为格式塔派是合理的。

和事佬

尽管和事佬可能同意格式塔派“大家和睦相处是好事”，但他们的做法截然不同。和事佬会运用逻辑、奉承、情感诉求、威胁或任何必要手段，在自身融合体内部维持健康的共识；当与非同一融合体的其他人合作时，他们也会将同样的哲学贯彻其中。即便这一综合学说经常磨炼领导技能，他们仍偏好“和事佬”这一称呼，因为它比起“领袖”等听起来更像掌权者的名称，更容易让他们与自尊心强烈的性格打交道。

强势个性

强势个性以其“比人生动”的特质得名，这往往意味着该综合学说的成员比其他融合体的个体更自我，与格式塔派形成对比。强势个性在融合体之外通常非常合群，常常尝试并成功与其他融合体以及非融合体血统的成员建立私人关系。当然，由于融合体的其他部分通常被迫始终与其同在，这可能引发冲突：强势个性想社交或开派对，而融合体其他部分真的想休息，或至少想远离人群喘口气，而不是尴尬地转身离开让强势个性自顾自玩。反过来，若强势个性的社交需求被过于频繁地拒绝，也可能成为融合体内部冲突的来源。

学究派

对更深入理解融合体生理与心理感到兴奋的融合体个体，属于学究派综合学说。学究派不断测试自己与融合体内其他个体，以衡量他们之间的联系、相似与差异。他们相信，越了解自身血统这种独特的相互关联方式，就越能突破极限；甚至有朝一日，这可能为更宏大的、人际层面的问题提供解决方案，而这些问题的影响远远超出单一融合体的边界。最为激昂的学究派甚至相信，他们的研究能帮助理解冲突——乃至战争——并提供预防之道。

信念

融合体在观点与哲学上差异极大，甚至同一融合体内的个体亦然。有时一个残忍而另一个仁慈；或一个冲动而另一个谨慎。如此一来，它们能够磨平彼此的棱角，共同做出更平衡的决定。

融合体崇拜形形色色的神祇，有时在神学问题上与自身其他自我出现巨大分歧。来世研究的学者会好奇信仰差异极大的融合体是否会产生特殊后果，但融合体认为这与其他血统中不同家庭成员信仰不同并无区别。

常见典范 比你的各个个体能力之和更强大，协作以达成成功，在你的融合体各个个体之间保持你自身的独特性

常见禁忌 背叛你们融合体共同做出的决定，出于怨恨而破坏你的融合体，沦为机器里的另一个齿轮

合并活动

融合体为动作与活动引入一种新特性：“合并”特性，表示融合体中的每个角色都在共同执行它。合并活动有两个独立步骤。首先，融合体为该活动支付相应的动作消耗。然后他们执行该活动（也称为“授予其好处”），可能在同一回合进行，也可能在融合体中另一名角色的回合进行。

在遭遇中每一轮内，某个特定融合体的角色第一次开始其回合时，融合体内的所有角色都可以同意共同进行一个或多个合并动作。合并活动对融合体中的每个角色都需要消耗所列出的动作数：第一个行动的角色支出自己的动作，而融合体内其他角色则在其下一回合贡献同等数量的未来动作。为进行合并活动，融合体内所有角色都必须能够行动，并且预计在其下一回合能获得足够的动作以贡献给该合并活动；同时他们各自都必须满足该合并活动的任何需求。一旦动作被承诺，融合体决定由哪名角色在其回合执行该合并活动。对大多数合并活动而言，在贡献动作与未来动作之后，融合体可以选择立刻在融合体中第一个行动角色的回合执行合并活动，或等待由融合体内另一名角色在其回合执行。有些合并活动对执行时机有更严格的规则；例如，“升起路障”只能在融合体中正持用盾牌的角色回合执行。若融合体中的某名角色在先前已为合并活动贡献了其下一回合的动作之后，变得目眩、迟缓或以其他方式无法行动（或无法具体使用该合并活动），则除非其效果已经发生，否则该合并活动被扰乱。例如，若融合体中的一名角色处于禁足（状态），融合体便无法使用合并移动活动，因为禁足（状态）阻止该角色移动。

要选择带有合并特性的专长，融合体中的每个角色都必须共同选择获得该专长。在这种情况下，他们都会获得该专长的好处（通常允许他们使用一种新的合并活动）。

尽管合并活动最初是专为融合体血统设计的，你与GM也可以考虑将其改造给其他血统中协调性极强的角色团队使用，以表现强力的组合攻击！还有什么比一队冒险者打出连携攻击更酷呢？

名字

融合体几乎总会为融合体内每个个体取名，但只有一部分融合体会为融合体整体取一个集体名称。个体名字与融合体名字也会因传承而异，其中奇美拉与九头蛇的名字最为相似，因为两者通常都源自龙语。尽管它们并非外位面存在，或许因为其最初构想源于集体主义的乌托邦领域，五合体最可能为每个五合体元件采用基于明显特征的简单个体名（如“黑狮”或“红暴龙”），同时也最可能拥有融合体名字。五合体的融合体名字往往略显夸张，即便五合体本身相当谦逊，例如“Gigatron”或“Omegazoid”。双头巨人很少拥有融合体名字；但当它们确实有时，该融合体名字往往是两个个体名字的字面组合。例如，若双头巨人的个体名为“Grog”和“Thog”，那么其融合体名字可能是“Grogthog”。

示例个体名

Argus（刻耳柏洛斯），Black Lion（五合体），Draxmont（奇美拉），Gorgamoz（奇美拉或九头蛇），Grog（双头巨人），Laelaps（刻耳柏洛斯），Red Tyrannosaurus（五合体），Ssithilis（九头蛇），Thog（双头巨人）

示例融合体名

Amalgam（奇美拉），Araxamandus（奇美拉或九头蛇），Asphodelia（刻耳柏洛斯），Gigatron（五合体），Grogthog（双头巨人），Lernaea（九头蛇），Memetos（刻耳柏洛斯），Omegazoid（五合体）

融合体传承

融合体传承彼此外观差异过于巨大，以至于学者花了很长时间才决定将其归类为同一血统。在1级时从以下融合体传承中选择一个。一个融合体的所有成员共享相同的传承，且融合体不能拥有多用传承。

双头巨人

为什么有免费专长？

融合体会为融合体中的每个角色在成长过程中获得不止一个、最终三个免费的合并专长，从而获得强力好处；这看起来或许不寻常。然而，这些免费的合并专长有助于抵消多名玩家需要使用同一具身体协作所带来的后勤挑战，包括需要与其他玩家协调，以及那些本想在战场不同位置站位的角色可能被迫共享同一位置。若你的团队具有异常高水平的团队合作与协调，并认为共享身体本身总体上就是优势（例如，构筑以确保融合体所有成员都在同一射程下表现最佳，同时熟练利用高机动角色的合并移动来抵消其他角色的速度惩罚），你可以考虑为你的团队移除这些免费合并专长。若由单个玩家控制融合体内所有角色，则这种情况更有可能发生。

即便在高度协调的小队中，能够使用一些合并动作而不必耗尽多个角色的所有血统专长，通常也会更有趣；因此若你决定移除融合体角色的免费合并专长，考虑为团队中的每个角色使用“血统典范”变体规则，以在拉平强度差距的同时，为融合体角色提供更多空间学习组合能力！

Orthrus及更多

双头巨人、刻耳柏洛斯与九头蛇分别专为两名、三名与四名角色设计，其合并动作也围绕这些人数进行平衡。然而，你的团队可能非常想出于主题或美学原因，扮演一只三头九头蛇或三头巨人，或一只双头犬科（通常称为orthus而非刻耳柏洛斯）。在这种情况下，你可以调整现有能力以适配不同人数的角色，或将其重新风味化以适配不同传承。

五合体

告别手臂

各个传承都具备某种可充当手臂的东西，使你无论融合体内每个人扮演何种职业与构筑，都能与团队一起扮演融合体角色，而不是被迫只能选择施法者、武僧等在没有手的情况下也更能应付的职业。不过，如果你与同为融合体的玩家更愿意，你也可以移除这些附肢。

合并专长的强度与定位

合并专长要求融合体中的每个角色都选择该专长，因此与一般血统专长相比，它们的获得成本更高；此外，使用它们所需的动作消耗也要求多名角色共同付出。因此，它们往往拥有一些格外强力的效果。例如，Omega Beam 若脱离语境来看，其伤害数值可能显得失控。但另一方面，四名角色必须协力才能制造出 Omega Beam，而且它在蓄力期间也给了扰乱它或远离它的时间窗口。合并活动以这种方式进行平衡；但由于将其合并为一次掷骰，它们可能表现得非常“不是大赚就是血亏”。这也是它们拥有不寻常的“大成功或大失败”条目、而非使用基础豁免的原因之一，以避免整队面对“翻倍或归零”的局面。

The whole is greater than the sum of its parts. No existence in the world proves this saying more than the unusual ancestry known as the fusion.

Whether they’re a human, a dragon, or even the avatar of a dungeon, ancestries are made up of individuals, with each member of an ancestry being a single distinct creature… or at least, that should be the case. However, some heritages composed of multiple individuals in a single body baffle even the most sage of philosophers as to the physical and metaphysical implications. While these heritages are different enough that some mainstream scholars consider them to be wholly unrelated, ancestry scholars have recently classified these gestalt entities into a single speculative ancestry, which they’ve called “fusions.” These fusions might have outwardly disparate appearances, much like the wildly varying phenotype between a giant tree dungeon, an underground labyrinth dungeon, and an archipelago dungeon, but just like those dungeon heritages, fusions share a deeper similarity: fusions are multiple creatures sharing a single body.

You Might...

  • Sometimes get into arguments with the other characters in your fusion about how to act in a crisis.
  • Learn how to share and coordinate with others, whether by choice or by necessity.
  • Be at ease even when isolated because you’re never truly alone.

Others Probably...

  • Forget or fail to understand that you are multiple people, rather than a single individual.
  • Admire the sheer destructive power of your fusion’s many actions or combined activities, mistakenly comparing you to a single other creature.
  • Confuse you for another member of your fusion, especially when you aren’t wearing distinguishing gear.

Physical Description

Fusions are large beings with multiple heads, each of which is a separate creature sharing a single body. Their body shape and other physical details vary dramatically depending on their heritage, so much so that it took centuries for scholars to classify them as a single ancestry due to their metaphysical similarities. In addition to multiple heads, the other telltale hallmark of a fusion is a patchwork of equipment covering a particular portion of the fusion’s body, allowing the separate creatures within the fusion to wear different gear appropriate for their class.

Two-headed giants are roughly humanoid-looking giants with two heads and either two or four arms, with each head a separate creature, controlling half the arms (one of the two arms or two of the four arms).

Cerberi take the form of large powerful three-head-ed dogs with an other-worldly cast to them. They are capable of manifesting up to six armlike appendages from their body, typical-ly in the form of chains, which are capable of holding and manipulating objects despite their appearance. Each head is a separate creature, controlling two of the cerberus’s appendages.

Chimeras have powerful quadrupedal bodies with three varied heads that seem like they belong to different sorts of creatures, traditionally one goat head, one lion head, and one dragon head. However, the exact distribution of heads varies from chimera to chimera. Some legendary chimeras have a fourth snake head on their tail, and while fusion chimeras don't match that legend, they do have six typically snakelike prehensile limbs across their body, which allow them to hold and manipulate objects. The fact that these limbs are usually serpentine for chimeras, even without a snake head, is one of the clues scholars initially used when creating the classification of fusions as a single ancestry.

Hydra are large four-headed serpents with long sinuous necks and a massive central body. They have eight snakelike prehensile limbs they can extend from their body, which appear almost like a mass of coiled tails. They can use these limbs to hold and manipulate objects. Each head is a separate creature, controlling one-quarter of the hydra’s limbs.

Quintrons are perhaps the most unusual of fusions: they are formed from five living beings called quintrites. Each quintrite possesses a metallic body with animal features that make them look vaguely like some kind of constructs or automata. While these quintrites can live separately as individual metallic animals, when necessary, all five can also combine into one giant humanoid form, called a quintron. Each individual quintrite that makes up the quintron has two spots on their body that can hold and manipulate objects, though they aren’t necessarily placed anywhere near where hands would be on a humanoid creature. For instance, an elephant quintrite might have these where its trunk and tail would be. Of course, when a quintrite folds up and combines into a quintron, the position of their body parts might change as well. Typically, each of the five quintrites that makes up a quintron is shaped like a different animal, as well as a different color.

Society

Since fusions appear as giants, canines, or serpents, members of other ancestries sometimes incorrectly conclude fusions must be bestial and antisocial. If they stopped to think about it, rather than judging solely based on appearances, they would realize this can’t truly be the case. By virtue of sharing a body between multiple separate creatures, each with their own distinct personalities, fusions are forced from an early age to learn how to share, how to communicate, and how to work together. Because of this, fusions can make surprisingly effective leaders or mediators, at least for people willing to take orders or advice from a giant hydra.

Fusion Adventurers

Fusions often become adventurers, where their powerful combination of skills allow them to excel, and people of other ancestries are more willing to overlook their differences. Fusions tend toward a wide variety of classes, and they usually choose classes that are complementary to the other characters in their fusion so that it’s easier to work together. They’re less likely to have backgrounds that involve integrating seamlessly into other ancestries’ societies, like bartender, but they otherwise have backgrounds that vary widely. Characters in the same fusion always have backgrounds that are compatible with each other; while that doesn’t mean they need to have the same or even similar background, since each character might have their own interests and training, they can’t share backgrounds that contradict each other when it comes to where their shared body was located.

Heritages

Beyond their shared traits due to their gestalt nature, fusion’s social tendencies vary greatly by heritage.

Two-headed Giants

Two-headed giants live the most similarly to other humanoids, with societal structures that seem reminiscent to visitors of most other ancestries. Some humanoid siblings are basically inseparable, and so humanoids can imagine a two-headed giant as simply a more extreme case of that. Two-headed giants’ two selves often take up skills that work well together, whether because they’re similar and complementary or different enough to cover each other’s gaps. This isn’t always because they get along perfectly—sometimes a two-headed giant’s two selves choose incredibly different paths not intentionally to aid each other by covering a weakness but because they rub each other the wrong way and want to prove how different they are. Other times, it’s a sense of rivalry that helps them develop complementary skill sets, each seeking to outdo the other. Still, most two-headed giants’ two selves get along with each other fairly well, outside of contentious decisions.

Cerberi

Cerberi have an innate disposition toward guarding places or things which helps them get along well with other guardian ancestries like sphinxes and gargoyles. Cerberi often guard “alone,” but they’re never truly alone with their other selves. They keep each other company and alleviate any boredom they feel in their tasks by playing together, often via either roughhousing or intellectual competitions. Cerberi have keen senses and most have a particular ear for music. Often, the offer of a new song can set a cerberus more at ease when they’re wary or on guard against a new person. Sometimes, a cerberus tires of being a guardian or yearns to hear more music and leaves on a grand adventure. Other cerberi sometimes grumble about these wandering cerberi while looking on at their adventures half in envy.

Fusion Settlements

Settlements larger than a small family are rare among cerberi and hydras, but two-headed giants often form isolated villages hidden in the hills or mountains. Chimeras rarely even form family groups, while quintrons have a rich social culture and would be willing to create large settlements if there were more of them around. While many fusions are “solitary” (aside from their fellows in the fusion), there are some who adapt to life in settlements of other ancestries or even prefer to live there.

Chimeras

Chimeras live in the widest possible environments due to their anatomical variety, with mountains and forests being slightly more common than other terrains. However, chimeras also exhibit a strong presence in other climates and environments. For instance, a chimera in a desert might have a camel head, a snake head, and an azure-hued dragon head, or any other combination of desert animals and creatures. Because their heads and bodies are a patchwork of other creatures, chimeras tend to have bigger differences among the individuals who make up their fusion than any other fusion. This can lead to distinctive opinions, which in turn can potentially lead to discord between the three individuals, so when they can’t agree, chimeras vote on an outcome with two out of three deciding the result and the other agreeing to acquiesce. Such an arrangement rarely leads to a situation where one individual constantly loses out on every vote and the others vote in a bloc, since it’s relatively easy to make a deal or compromise that would split up any such bloc depending on the circumstances of the argument or decision. In the unlikely event that two individuals remain in near-perfect accord, it can lead to feelings of resentment in the third, who keeps on losing, so it’s usually in the chimera’s best interest as a whole to find some way to extend an olive branch or two.

Hydras

Hydras often live peacefully in swamps and wetlands, hunting food and defending themselves against encroaching creatures or adventurers who mistake them for dragons and expect them to possess a huge hoard of treasure. Those who are especially curious or are pushed by their circumstances leave their marshy homes in search of adventure. Hydras often find great success in their quests, as a single hydra adventurer is capable of feats that would normally require an entire adventuring party to accomplish. Hydras often have strong personalities and traits that distinguish each individual within the fusion, though sometimes after a hydra’s head is cut off, the new head that grows back has a slightly different perspective.

Quintrons

Quintrons are living creatures that look akin to machines, and that appearance is no coincidence: they were supposedly designed that way and brought to life with their living metallic quintrite bodies capable of combining to form a quintron. According to legend, the original blueprints for the first quintrons were created in the extraplanar metropolis of Utopia. However, quintrons past the originals reproduce on their own, and the heritage as a whole isn’t normally extraplanar. They’re denizens of the mortal world just like any other fusion. One of the main side effects of their reputed Utopian origin is that quintrons often manage to stay in accord and work together better than would otherwise be expected for a fusion of five different individual quintrites. It’s ironic, then, that quintrons have the term “quintrite” for their five individuals while other heritages don’t have an equivalent term. This distinction comes about because of the fact that quintrons by their nature can separate into their five quintrites and recombine at will. Thus, while they’re more likely to coordinate and be in accord with each other, quintrons are also inherently capable of autonomous action, with their quintrites separating and traveling afar if necessary. Quintrons and quintrites tend to prefer efficiency, conciseness, and straightforwardness in their decision-making and speech. Such behavior helps keep things focused with five opinions to be heard. As a result, they occasionally utilize nicknames that are particularly descriptive, like “Elf Archer” or the less flattering “Loud Human.” They won’t use these nicknames if they aren’t uniquely identifying enough, however, so for instance, if they were dealing with a group of elven archers, “Elf Archer” wouldn’t help much.

It Takes Two?

Unlike any other ancestry, fusions are a single ancestry shared by multiple player characters in the same body! Each of these characters is a separate person, with their own class and their own background, though the backgrounds for two characters who are part of the same fusion need to avoid contradicting each other. For instance, if one of the characters has a background about being raised by animals in the forest and another has a background about being raised as a princess in the royal palace, it might not be possible to reconcile them. While they share the same body and space, each character rolls their own initiative, wears and invests and carries their own magic items. In the case of magic items that are normally impossible to wear together on the same body, they magically adjust to fit together onto a fusion character. For instance, a two-headed giant fusion of a wizard and a fighter might have a magical robe on the left side of their body and a suit of heavy armor on the right side of their body.

Each creature within a fusion is a separate creature and is targeted separately with their own Hit Points and conditions. This means it’s possible for one creature in a fusion to die while the other creature or creatures remain alive, for one creature in a fusion to be turned to stone while the rest of the fusion isn’t, or for one creature in a fusion to polymorph while the rest of the fusion hasn’t (in which case they look like a bizarre chimera). However, effects that alter a creature’s size don’t work on a fusion unless they can affect all creatures in the fusion at the same time; growing or shrinking one head of the hydra isn’t enough to increase the whole hydra’s size. There might be other similar effects that the GM determines don’t work unless they affect all creatures in the fusion.

As with any other PCs, normally a different player is responsible for creating and portraying each character in a fusion. This can be incredibly fun to roleplay with the right person or people. However, in a situation with fewer players than normal where the group was considering having one player control multiple characters, having that player control linked fusion characters can be an excellent choice. For instance, in a one-on-one game, the single player might portray all four heads of a hydra.

Fusion Syntheses

Fusions are generally more concerned with the way they interact with the other individuals in their fusion than the way they get along with other fusions or with the social groupings they might form. However, there are some rough philosophies among fusions, which scholars call “syntheses,” that distinguish the main schools of thought. Of course, a fusion is by no means a monolith: they might have individuals who are all from different syntheses!

Gestaltists

The whole is greater than the sum of its parts. Almost any fusion would agree, but gestaltists take this principle to a particular extreme: more than any other individual within a fusion, they subsume their will into the gestalt and try to lose themselves in an almost meditative way. When all individuals in a fusion are gestaltists, they can sometimes exhibit surprising coordination, resembling an almost-hivemind that appears eerie to other fusions but seems normal to members of different ancestries who are unfamiliar with typical fusion behavior. If one player controls all the people in a fusion, it makes sense to have them all be gestaltists unless the player wants to roleplay the personality conflicts.

Peacemakers

While peacemakers can agree with gestaltists that it’s good for everyone to get along, the way they go about it is completely different. Peacemakers use logic, flattery, emotional appeals, threats, or whatever else it takes to keep a healthy consensus among their own fusion, and they bring that same philosophy to bear when working in a group with other people who aren’t individuals in the same fusion. Even though their synthesis often practices leadership skills, they prefer the name peacemaker because it makes it easier for them to deal with prideful personalities than a name that made them sound more like they were leaders or otherwise in charge of others.

Personalities

Personalities are named for their larger-than-life traits, which often indicate that members of this synthesis possess a greater ego than individuals in other fusions, contrasting with gestaltists. Personalities tend to be highly social outside their fusion, often trying and succeeding to form personal bonds with other fusions as well as members of non-fusion ancestries. Of course, since the rest of their fusion is usually forced to be right there with them, this can lead to conflicts where a personality wants to hang out or party while the rest of the fusion really wants to rest or at least take a break from people without just awkwardly turning away while the personality does their thing. On the other hand, a personality whose social needs are denied too often can become a source of conflict within the fusion.

Scholastics

Individuals within a fusion who are excited to better understand fusion physiology and psychology are members of the scholastic synthesis. Scholastics are constantly testing themselves and the other individuals in their fusion to measure their connection, their similarities, and their differences. They believe that better understanding the unique way their ancestry interrelates will allow them to exceed their limits, and it might even lead to solutions someday to larger interpersonal issues that ramify far beyond the boundaries of a single fusion. The most vociferous scholastics even believe that their research could help understand conflicts, up to and including wars, and provide ways to prevent them.

Beliefs

Fusions vary greatly in their outlooks and philosophies, even among the individuals in the same fusion. Sometimes one is cruel while another is kind, or one is impetuous while another is careful. In this way, they can temper each other’s rough edges and come to a balanced decision together.

Fusions worship all sorts of deities, sometimes splitting dramatically from their other selves on theological matters. Scholars of the afterlife wonder if there’s any special consequences for fusions of wildly differing faiths, but fusions believe it’s no different than for various family members in another ancestry.

Popular Edicts be greater than the sum of your individual abilities, work together to achieve success, maintain your own distinctiveness among the individuals in your fusion

Popular Anathemas betray a decision your fusion has decided together, sabotage your fusion out of spite, become nothing more than another cog in the machine

Combined Activities

Fusions introduce a new trait for actions and activities, the “combined” trait, which means that every character in a fusion is working together to perform it. Combined activities have two separate steps. First, the fusion pays the appropriate action cost for the activity. Then they perform the activity (also known as “granting the benefits”), possibly on the same turn, and possibly on the turn of another character in the fusion.

Each round during an encounter, the first time a character who is part of a particular fusion takes their turn, all characters in the fusion can agree to take one or more combined actions together. Combined activities cost the listed number of actions for every character in the fusion: the first character to act spends their actions, and all the other characters in the fusion contribute the same number of future actions on their next turns. In order to take a combined activity, all characters in the fusion must be able to act and must be expecting to gain enough actions on their next turn to contribute to the combined activity, and they each must meet any requirements for the combined activity. Once the actions are committed, the fusion decides which character will perform the combined activity on their turn. For most combined activities, after contributing actions and future actions, the fusion can choose to perform the combined activity immediately on the turn of the first character in the fusion to act, or to wait and have another character in the fusion perform the combined activity on their turn. Some combined activities have stricter rules for when they’re performed; for instance, Raise a Barricade can only be performed on the turn of a character in the fusion wielding a shield. If a character in a fusion becomes stunned, slowed, or otherwise unable to act (or to specifically use the combined activity) after previously contributing their next turn’s actions to a combined activity, the combined activity is disrupted unless its effects have already occurred. For example, if one character in a fusion is immobilized, the fusion can’t use a combined move activity because the immobilized condition prevents that character from moving.

To select a feat with the combined trait, every character in the fusion must choose to take the feat together. In this case, they all gain the benefit of the feat (typically allowing them to use a new combined activity).

While combined activities were initially designed specifically for the fusion ancestry, you and your GM can consider adapting them to be used by especially coordinated groups of characters from other ancestries to represent powerful combination attacks! What could be cooler than a team of adventurers making a combo attack?

Names

Fusions almost always have names for each individual in the fusion, but only some fusions have a collective name for the fusion as a whole. The individual names and fusion names also vary from heritage to heritage, with chimera and hydra names being the most similar since they are both typically derived from the Draconic language. While they aren’t extraplanar beings, perhaps because they were originally conceived in the collectivist realm of Utopia, quintrons are the most likely to have simple individual names based on obvious features of each individual quintrite, such as “black lion” or “red tyrannosaurus,” while also being the most likely to have a fusion name. Quintron fusion names tend to be a bit bombastic, even if the quintrons themselves are quite humble, with names like “Gigatron” or “Omegazoid.” Two-headed giants rarely have fusion names, though when they do, the fusion name is often a literal combination of the two individual names. For instance, if a two-headed giant’s individual names are “Grog” and “Thog,” then their fusion name might be “Grogthog.”

Sample Individual Names

Argus (cerberus), Black Lion (quintron), Draxmont (chimera), Gorgamoz (chimera or hydra), Grog (two-headed giant), Laelaps (cerberus), Red Tyrannosaurus (quintron), Ssithilis (hydra), Thog (two-headed giant)

Sample Fusion Names

Amalgam (chimera), Araxamandus (chimera or hydra), Asphodelia (cerberus), Gigatron (quintron), Grogthog (two-headed giant), Lernaea (hydra), Memetos (cerberus), Omegazoid (quintron)

Fusion Heritages

Fusion heritages look so wildly different from each other that it took a long time before any scholars chose to classify them as a single ancestry. Choose one of the following fusion heritages at 1st level. All members of a fusion share the same heritage, and fusions can’t have versatile heritages.

Two-headed Giant
